results.title: Fixit linter+AI coding
results.abstract:
This proposal introduces an innovative approach to Python code quality enforcement by combining fixit (a linting framework based on libcst) with generative AI to create custom linters tailored to team-specific coding standards. 

Traditional linters like ruff provide general-purpose rules but struggle to address organization-specific requirements and coding conventions. This creates challenges where code review becomes subjective and dependent on individual reviewers' knowledge. Our solution leverages AI to generate fixit rules from natural language descriptions, dramatically reducing the barrier to creating and maintaining custom linting rules.

The core innovation lies in using libcst's Concrete Syntax Tree (CST) representation, which preserves formatting, comments, and whitespace—unlike traditional Abstract Syntax Trees (AST). This enables safe, automated code transformations that maintain the original code's style while enforcing new standards. By combining AI-assisted rule generation with fixit's powerful transformation capabilities, teams can quickly implement and enforce new coding standards across entire codebases, eliminating review subjectivity and accelerating modernization efforts.
results.description:
## Background and Motivation:
Organizations face diverse logging practices across different teams and projects. The industry trend shows increasing migration from Python's legacy logging module to structlog for better structured logging capabilities. However, existing general-purpose linters cannot adequately address organization-specific migration requirements and coding standards, leading to inconsistent code reviews and slow adoption.

## The Custom Linter Approach:
Custom linters address three critical needs: (1) eliminating subjectivity in code reviews by codifying standards, (2) automatically enforcing "ideal patterns" defined by the organization, and (3) shifting from human-dependent review processes to machine-validated enforcement.

## Technical Foundation - AST vs. CST:
The solution builds on libcst, which uses Concrete Syntax Trees (CST) rather than Abstract Syntax Trees (AST). While AST captures only structural information, discarding comments, whitespace, and formatting, CST preserves the complete source representation. This distinction is crucial for production environments where preserving existing code style and comments is essential for safe automated refactoring.

## AI-Powered Rule Generation:
Developers can describe desired transformations in natural language (e.g., "Replace logging.getLogger usage with structlog.get_logger"), and AI generates corresponding fixit rules. This dramatically lowers the technical barrier for creating custom linters, enabling rapid iteration on coding standards.

## Practical Implementation:
The presentation demonstrates a complete example: transforming 
```
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
``` 
to 
```
import structlog
logger = structlog.get_logger()
``` 
The generated fixit rule handles both import statements and function calls, ensuring comprehensive migration across codebases.
results.notes:
## Key Takeaways:
1. **Flexibility:** The AI + fixit approach enables automatic generation of linters that adapt to team-specific coding styles and organizational requirements, rather than forcing teams to conform to rigid, general-purpose rules.

2. **Practical Migration Path:** The logging→structlog migration example demonstrates real-world applicability. Organizations can modernize legacy codebases efficiently while maintaining code quality and consistency.

3. **Cultural Impact:** By codifying standards into automated linters, organizations can balance code quality enforcement with team culture. New team members immediately understand expected patterns through automated feedback, reducing onboarding friction.

4. **Elimination of Key-Person Dependency:** Knowledge about coding standards moves from individuals' tacit knowledge into executable rules, reducing organizational risk when team members change roles or leave.

## Implementation Considerations:
- Fixit rules can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines for continuous enforcement
- The approach scales to any Python coding standard beyond logging migration
- AI-generated rules should be reviewed and tested before deployment
- The CST-based approach ensures production code safety during automated transformations

## Future Potential:
This methodology extends beyond logging migration to any Python refactoring scenario: dependency updates, API migrations, security pattern enforcement, or performance optimizations. As AI models improve, the gap between describing desired code transformations and implementing them continues to narrow, democratizing custom static analysis tool development.

results.title: Battle of the Baby Brains: 10 Small Language Models Go Head-to-Head
results.abstract: Small Language Models (SLMs) with 1B–8B parameters are becoming increasingly practical as efficient alternatives to massive LLMs. But with frequent open-source releases, developers often struggle to decide which one fits their needs. This talk presents a comparative evaluation of 10 popular pretrained SLMs across benchmark scores and real-world performance, highlighting trade-offs in speed, memory, accuracy, and adaptability. By the end, attendees will have a clear view of the strengths and weaknesses of leading SLMs, plus a practical framework for model selection and experimentation.
results.description:
This talk is for LLM beginners, developers, and enthusiasts experimenting with small language models, curious about benchmarks, performance, learnability, and deployment on CPU, limited GPU, or low-resource devices.  

**The comparison covers:**
- Hugging Face open-llm leaderboard benchmark scores  
- Inference speed, memory consumption on CPU-only and GPU setups  
- Latency on resource-constrained devices with quantization  
- Accuracy and correctness on datasets like TruthfulQA, MMLU, HumanEval  
- Finetuning a small dataset across models to explore adaptability
  
**Outlines:**  
1. **Introduction (5 min):** Why small LLMs?  
2. **Benchmarks Explained (5 min):** Interpreting Hugging Face OpenLLM leaderboard benchmarks.  
3. **Comparative Evaluation (10 min):** Speed, memory, GPU vs CPU trade-offs, accuracy, benchmark scores, learnability.  
4. **Q&A (10 min):** Open discussion.

results.title: Designing a Fast, Offline-Capable Reverse Geocoder in Python: An Open Source Alternative to Big Geo APIs
results.abstract:
While commercial reverse geocoding APIs, such as Google Maps or Mapbox, are effective, they are also costly, have rate limitations, and are not appropriate for offline or privacy-sensitive settings.

Using available datasets and Python modules like cKDTree, shapely, and geopandas, we will demonstrate how to create a quick, scalable, offline-capable reverse geocoding system in Python in this session.

You will learn how to:

Convert geographic shapefiles into effective spatial indices
Perform location lookups in milliseconds using tree search and vector mathematics
Handle edge cases like unclear borders, cities with identical names, and GPS noise
Improve performance and memory usage through multiprocessing
The system is fully open source and has been production-tested in a high-throughput environment. Whether you are developing applications for edge inference, mapping, or logistics, this talk will help you take control of your geospatial infrastructure without depending on costly commercial APIs.
results.description:
Reverse geocoding — converting coordinates into readable place names — is a core building block of applications in logistics, mapping, mobility, and location intelligence. Yet developers are often locked into commercial APIs that are expensive, rate-limited, and unsuitable for offline or privacy-first use cases.

In this talk, we’ll walk through the architecture and implementation of a fast reverse geocoding engine built entirely in Python using open-source tooling. You’ll see how spatial data (such as OpenStreetMap shapefiles) can be indexed efficiently using scipy's cKDTree, queried with millisecond latency, and integrated into real-world systems.

We’ll explore performance trade-offs, data preprocessing techniques, and methods for dealing with ambiguous or noisy GPS data. The session includes benchmarks and a live walkthrough of the code powering the reverse geocoder — which is lightweight enough to run on a laptop or edge device.

Attendees will leave with a clear understanding of how to build and adapt this system for their own needs — and gain insight into how geospatial systems work behind the scenes.

results.title: How does face recognition work? The basics shown via live coding.
results.abstract: In this talk, we will live code a simple face recognition application. We will go over the basics of how face recognition work, and go over improvements that the audience can try to practice what they have learned.
results.description:
Face recognition has improved over the years. So much so, that there are Python libraries that makes it easy and simple to implement.

We will be live coding an application to recognize faces (of course, for privacy reasons, the speaker will be pointing their camera away from the audience).

In addition, we will briefly go over how face recognition works and its limitations.

results.title: Let's implement useless Python objects
results.abstract:
Let's implement objects that are useless for anything.
For example, an object whose length returned by the len() function is different every time, or an object that returns a bullshit result when you check if a value exists with the in operator.
And through the implementation, you will get a better understanding of Python data types.
results.description:
The Python objects implemented in this presentation are not useful at all.
However, implementing completely useless objects will give you a better understanding of Python data types.
You will then be able to implement useful objects.

There are two topics covered in this presentation.

The first is understanding what happens behind the scenes of the len() function, the in operator, and the for statement.
For example, if you pass an object ``obj`` to the ``len()`` function, the result of ``len(obj)`` will be ``obj.__len__()``.
In other words, if you screw up the implementation of ``obj.__len__()``, the result of ``len(obj)`` will also be screwed up.
You will understand what Python does behind the scenes, why you can create useless objects, and how to implement objects that behave correctly.

Second, you will understand how to use the abstract base classes in ``collections.abc``.
Python's built-in containers include lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets.
And the built-in containers, simply put, consist of the abstract base classes ``Sized``, ``Container`` and ``Iterable`` in ``collections.abc``.
If you try to implement a useless object and implement it in an absurd way, you will not be able to use it properly.
Using the abstract base class, you can create useless objects whose behaviour is absurd, but which also work with existing Python objects.
Through this experience, you will learn how to use abstract base classes and how to create your own containers correctly.

The basic agenda is as follows

- Preliminaries: let's create a totally useless object.
- Basics: start with ``Sized``, ``Container`` and ``Iterable``.
  - ``ElasticSized``: an object that changes the return value of the ``len()`` function each time it is executed.
  - ``ForgottenContainer``: an object where the result of the in operator changes each time it is executed.
  - ``ShuffledIterable``: An object whose ``for`` statement changes the result each time it is executed.
- Applications: Uncontrolled containers.
  - ``UncontrolledSequence``: An uncontrolled sequence.
  - ``MisprintedDictionary``: A misprinted dictionary.
  - ``CrowdSet``: A crowd set
results.notes:
This presentation was originally given by the author at PyCon APAC 2023.
We are presenting it again here with revisions.

results.title: Why Python Works Well for Kids (Visual to Text Coding Transition)
results.abstract: Python offers a simple, readable syntax that helps young learners shift smoothly from block coding to text-based programming, supporting deeper problem-solving habits and clearer logical thinking.
results.description: This session looks at why Python fits well in early programming classes, with practical strategies for guiding students as they move from visual blocks to written code. The talk covers classroom techniques, sample activities, and ways to build confidence in beginners so they can write code with purpose.

results.title: Live Demo: Setting Up Your Python Development Environment
results.abstract:
Two hosts from PyCon JP TV will live-stream their process of setting up an environment to start developing in Python.
Starting with Python installation, they'll configure VS Code and introduce tools like venv, pip, ruff, and mypy, then proceed to actually write Python programs.

PyCon JP TV(https://tv.pycon.jp/) is a monthly YouTube live stream hosted by the PyCon JP Association. It primarily shares information about the Python community and Python-related topics.

* Setting up VS Code

results.title: How I Built a Substack-Powered AI Assistant With Python
results.abstract: Most of my readers ask similar questions about data science careers, AI, and breaking into the industry without a CS degree, but the answers are usually buried across 50+ of my substack blog posts (https://aiweekender.substack.com). So I built a Python-based AI assistant (https://assistant.ds-claudia.com) that can answer their questions directly, using my past writing as the knowledge base. In this talk, I’ll walk through how I used Supabase, OpenAI, and Streamlit to build a lightweight retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system that: 1. Retrieves relevant posts, 2. Generates personalized responses, 3. Helps readers discover content they would have missed. This is a practical, end-to-end look at turning messy real-world text into a useful assistant that people actually rely on, all without fine-tuning or heavy infrastructure.
results.description:
This talk is a practical, end-to-end walkthrough of building a lightweight AI assistant on top of my Substack content. I’ll cover:

- Parsing real-world text from RSS feeds and HTML
- Converting posts into embeddings for semantic search
- Storing and querying embeddings with Supabase and pgvector
- Generating personalized answers with OpenAI LLMs
- Streaming responses in Streamlit, including source citations
- Logging queries to understand reader needs and improve the system

Attendees will leave with a clear picture of how to build a RAG-based assistant for their own blogs, documentation, or newsletters.
results.notes:
Outline: 
1. Why I built this assistant: repeated questions, scattered content (3 min)
2. Architecture overview: Supabase, OpenAI, Streamlit (4 min)
3. Content ingestion: handling Substack RSS, HTML cleanup, archived posts (6 min)
4. Vector search pipeline: embeddings, similar search (5 min)
5. Generating answers: prompting, streaming, and avoiding hallucinations (5 min)
6. Making it usable: fast responses, citations, logging (3 min)
7. What I learned: brittleness, latency, costs, UX surprises (4 min)

results.title: Asyncio vs Multithreading: Python Concurrency for Absolute Beginners
results.abstract: Python lets you do many things at once—but how? Should you use threads, or is asyncio the way to go? This beginner-friendly session breaks down concurrency in Python into simple, digestible concepts. You’ll learn what happens when your code runs in multiple threads versus an event loop, when to choose one over the other, and how to write your first non-blocking Python programs without getting overwhelmed.
results.description:
This is a hands-on session designed for absolute beginners who want to understand Python concurrency without drowning in complex theory. Using simple, easy-to-grasp analogies, we’ll make concepts like threads and asyncio intuitive and memorable, so you leave with zero doubts.

In this workshop, we’ll cover:

What multithreading is and how Python threads work (and why they sometimes don’t behave as expected)

What asyncio is and why Python uses an event loop for async tasks

The key differences between threads and async programming in terms of performance, blocking, and simplicity

Practical examples: simple multithreaded tasks vs async tasks

How to decide when to use threads, when to use asyncio, and when both are unnecessary

By the end of the session, you’ll have a clear mental model of Python concurrency, be able to write your first async function, and understand exactly how threads and async tasks behave in real code—all taught using fun analogies to make learning simple, engaging, and doubt-free.

results.title: Small Chips, Big Models: The Art of Squeezing LLMs onto Budget Hardware
results.abstract: Large language models traditionally require GPUs for fast, low-latency inference. However, GPUs aren't available on most edge devices, smartphones or CPU-only computers. This talk explains how proper techniques, such as quantization, GGUF formatting, and efficient inference engines, can enable the deployment of these models on everyday hardware. This talk is for AI/ML beginners or anyone curious about limited resource deployment.
results.description:
**Expect to learn:**  
- The challenges in deploying language models on resource-constrained devices  
- Solutions with quantization, model compression, and efficient formats  
- Practical implementation using llama.cpp and Python bindings  

**Outlines:**  
1. **Introduction (5 mins):** Why on-device deployment?  
2. **Challenges (5 mins):** What are the challenges in On-device deployment?  
3. **Optimization (10 mins):** Quantization, GGUF, model compression for faster inference.  
4. **Demo (5mins):** Deploying language model on Redmi Note 13 Smartphone (Snapdragon 685 8GB).  
5. **Q&A (5 mins):** Audience discussion and open-source resources.

results.title: Tight Genes: Intro to Genetic Algorithms
results.abstract:
This talk introduces the concept of Genetic Algorithms, including:
- What are genetic algorithms?
- Why are they useful?
- Brief history
- Examples of pre-existing usage
- Overview of how they work
- Walkthrough of creating ones to solve different kinds of problems (e.g., knapsack, traveling salesman, and/or others, as time allows), pointing out terms, tradeoffs, nuances, etc. along the way
results.description:
Yes, that's right, gene***T***ic, not gene***R***ic.  Genetic algorithms are shortcuts to "evolve" solutions to hard problems, by mimicking real-world biological evolution.  They find "good enough" solutions much more quickly than precise calculation or brute-forcing the solution space.  Their randomness often reveals great solutions that humans probably would _never_ have thought of, such as NASA's twisty ST5 spacecraft antenna, developed by a genetic algorithm in 2006!

This talk will explain the concept, and then walk through some examples, explaining terms and pointing out tradeoffs and nuances along the way.

You will learn a powerful technique for solving problems that might otherwise be computationally intractable, including in ways no human would probably ever think of directly!
results.notes:
This talk is simple enough that juniors can grok it, but it can be useful for devs of all levels.

I have done this talk for several conferences so far, but not yet in the Philippines.  You can see the videos in my playlist at https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLMrm16n64Buag1--3P3xf9KnrH330nrPc .

results.title: No Cloud, No Problem: My Journey to a 1.5 B Local-First Python Assistant
results.abstract: LLM chatbots like ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot have transformed how we learn Python and debug code. However, they come with privacy concerns, usage limits, and moderation restrictions. In this talk, I’ll share how I built PyBuddy, a 1.5B-parameter open-source Python learning & coding assistant that runs entirely on a 4 GB smartphone. I'll walk through the complete journey. First, selecting models by analyzing HuggingFace open-LLM benchmarks. Then, GPU-optimized fine-tuning with LoRA/QLoRA. Finally, quantization techniques and GGUF formatting for faster inference on CPUs and mobile phones.
results.description:
This talk is for AI/ML beginners, people interested in low-GPU training, or anyone curious about on-device, CPU, and mobile deployment.  

**Outlines:**   
1. **Introduction (5 min):** Why on-device LLMs & core challenges  
2. **Model Selection Strategy (5 min):** Analyzing HF benchmarks and resource consumption  
3. **Fine-tuning (5 min):** PEFT (LoRA/QLoRA) - how they work and reduce GPU memory/time  
4. **Deployment (5 min):** Quantization, GGUF formatting, and inference engines  
5. **Demo (5 min):** Running the model on Snapdragon 680 4GB RAM phone  
6. **Q&A (5 min):** Audience discussion and open-source resources

results.title: Analyzing Trends and Biases in Generative AI Output with NLTK and the Gemini API
results.abstract: More and more people are turning to generative AI tools for support in their day-to-day lives, but biases abound, potentially reinforcing harmful stereotypes. Join me to learn how I used Python to accelerate my research on gender bias in AI-generated advice - and how anyone can use NLTK and the Gemini API to investigate systemic patterns in generative AI output and draw conclusions about both AI biases and prevailing human sentiment.
results.description:
In this beginner-friendly talk, learn how to statistically analyze trends in AI-generated text by...
* Producing hundreds of samples with Python's Gemini API.
* Visualizing common themes with NLTK's WordCloud.
* Performing statistical tests using data from NLTK's FreqDist.

We'll also explore how identified patterns in generative AI output can potentially be used to guide market research and inquiries into popular thought.

results.title: Streaming Protocols for Conversational AI
results.abstract:
Conversational AI now demands real-time responsiveness, yet many Python-based chatbots still suffer from multi-second delays that break user flow. This session demystifies the streaming architectures that power modern AI systems—from ChatGPT to Gemini Live—and shows how Python developers can build equally fluid, low-latency experiences. We go beyond traditional HTTP request-response bottlenecks to explore WebSockets, Server-Sent Events (SSE), and emerging protocols such as WebTransport and gRPC bidirectional streaming.

Attendees will learn how token-level streaming works under the hood, why major AI providers standardize on SSE for text and WebSockets for multimodal/voice, and how to implement these patterns efficiently with FastAPI and asyncio. The talk presents updated production lessons from 2025–2026 industry practices, including backpressure handling, real-time token cancellation, flow-control strategies, and optimizing network paths for LLM inference workloads.

By the end, Python developers will have a clear mental framework for selecting the right protocol, designing scalable streaming pipelines, and building conversational AI interfaces that feel instantaneous, interactive, and resilient—even under heavy load.
results.description: This talk begins by examining the rising user expectations around real-time conversational AI and why traditional synchronous HTTP patterns create unacceptable perceived latency for Python-based chatbots. We then break down the architectural limitations of request–response workflows before exploring how WebSockets provide full-duplex, low-latency communication ideal for voice, multimodal, and interactive agent use cases. Next, we look at Server-Sent Events (SSE) as a lightweight, resilient alternative for unidirectional token streaming, explaining why it has become the default for major LLM APIs. The session then presents a clear decision framework comparing WebSockets, SSE, WebTransport, and gRPC streaming, with practical production insights on flow control, backpressure, reconnection strategies, and asyncio-based implementations. We wrap up with forward-looking techniques—such as Agentic UI patterns and HTTP/3-based streaming—that help Python developers design scalable, modern real-time AI systems.

results.title: Python Stings Your Ego: Finding Pride in Community, Not Just Code
results.abstract:
In tech, it’s easy to get caught up in ego. Chasing the smartest solutions, writing the most complex code, or proving who’s the best in the room. But Python has a funny way of humbling us. Its simplicity reminds us that good code isn’t about showing off; it’s about making things clear, accessible, and collaborative.

This talk is about what happens after that sting of humility. Python doesn’t just strip away ego, it builds communities that make you feel proud to belong. I’ll share stories from my journey with Black Python Devs and other Python groups, showing how people from all walks of life come together, support one another, and create space for growth.

By the end, you’ll see that the real magic of Python isn’t just the language. It’s the people, the openness, and the sense of pride that comes from being part of something bigger than yourself.
results.description:
1. Introduction – The Ego in Tech

Quick story: how we programmers often wear ego like a badge (the smartest language, the toughest code, the fanciest framework).
Why ego sometimes gets in the way of real collaboration.

2. How Python Humbles You

- Python’s simplicity removes the need for showing off.
- Instead of flexing with complexity, Python forces you to focus on clarity and readability.
- The joy of seeing even beginners write code that works.

3. Communities Over Competition

- Python culture thrives on inclusion, mentorship, and collaboration.

- Examples: PyCascade, PyCon, PyConUS, PyConAsia, Black Python Devs, these are communities where humility drives growth.

- How diversity and openness create stronger communities.

4. The Pride That Comes After Ego

- Counterintuitive idea: once ego is humbled, you discover a deeper pride.

- Pride in shared wins, community achievements, and seeing newcomers succeed.

- Personal reflection: how being part of Python communities has shaped my journey.

5. Call to Action

- Encouraging the audience to embrace humility in their coding journey.

- Remind them: Python is not about ego, it’s about people, problems solved, and communities built.

results.title: Agentic AI: From Scripts to Autonomous Python Systems
results.abstract: Agentic AI turns LLMs from simple responders into systems that can **plan**, use **tools**, and act **autonomously**. This talk shows how Python developers can design reliable and controllable agentic systems using patterns for planning, memory, guardrails, and feedback loops. Learn how to go beyond prompts and build AI that actually *gets things done*.
results.description:
We’ve all done it: wired an LLM into a Python script, tried a few prompts, got a clever answer, and thought — *this could automate everything*. But after a week, reality hits. The model forgets context, makes odd decisions, loops forever, or breaks the moment things get complex.

That moment reveals the limits of “single-prompt AI”… and the beginning of agentic systems.

This talk is about what happens after the honeymoon phase. It’s the story of how simple scripts evolve into **agentic AI** — systems that plan, use tools, remember, self-correct, and act with purpose.

We’ll explore the core pieces behind real agentic systems: planners, tool routers, memory, guardrails, and evaluators — and how Python ties them together. No live coding, just clear mental models and patterns used by teams shipping production AI.

You’ll learn when agents shine, when they fail, and how to design them so they’re reliable instead of chaotic. If your LLM prototype felt magical in a notebook but brittle in production, this talk gives you the missing architecture.

By the end, you’ll know how to move from “prompt engineering” to **building AI that truly gets things done** — and why Python is the perfect place to build it.

results.title: Retriever Matters: Evaluating and Improving Retrieval Quality in RAG Systems
results.abstract:
The success of any RAG system depends on the quality of its retriever, yet this component is often overlooked or superficially evaluated. An LLM can’t make up for irrelevant or incomplete context. That’s why ensuring relevance and diversity in the context passed to the LLM is crucial.

This workshop will be fully hands-on and 100% practical, focusing on strategies to measure, improve, and optimize retrievers and memory usage for indexing using the Qdrant Vector Database Python client.
results.description:
In Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, the retriever is often the hidden bottleneck. RAG systems are only as good as their retrievers, but how do you know if yours is working well?  In this workshop session, we’ll explore practical methods to evaluate retrieval quality beyond simple top-k accuracy, including semantic relevance, diversity, and latency. 

Three common issues that developers address are memory usage in indexing the data, improving search performance, and evaluating the context retrieved against the user query and response. 

We will understand search techniques that consistently deliver strong results in practice, including Maximal Marginal Relevance for balancing relevance and diversity and hybrid search powered by Reciprocal Rank Fusion to merge lexical and vector embeddings. We will also address memory efficiency during indexing, covering payload design, quantization, and chunking strategies that reduce resource usage while preserving retrieval quality. 

For the workshop, we will use the Qdrant Python client, i.e., open source, for the hands-on session.

results.title: Python + Google ADK: Your 15-Minute Portal to LLM Agents
results.abstract:
Agentic AI enables systems to plan, act, and interact autonomously, and Python developers can now leverage the **Google Agent Development Kit (ADK)** to bring LLM-powered agents to life quickly and modularly.  

In this session, attendees will build a functional Python agent in just 15 minutes, learn how to integrate basic tools and memory for multi-step tasks, and see how to extend it into a workflow agent using ADK’s SequentialAgent and ParallelAgent constructs. Practical considerations, including modular design patterns, safety guardrails, and deployment options, will also be covered.  

This hands-on talk is ideal for Python developers, ML engineers, and open-source contributors who want to gain practical experience building and orchestrating autonomous LLM agents using the ADK open-source ecosystem.
results.description:
Learn how to bring an autonomous AI agent to life in Python in just 15 minutes!

Agentic AI - systems that can plan, act, and interact autonomously - is becoming an increasingly important part of modern development workflows. Python developers can now use the Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) to create LLM-powered agents in a structured, modular, and open-source-friendly way.

In this session, attendees will build a functional Python LLM agent in 15 minutes, learning how to define an agent, integrate basic tools, and manage memory for simple multi-step tasks. We will also demonstrate, briefly, how to extend this agent into a workflow agent using ADK’s SequentialAgent and ParallelAgent constructs for slightly more complex operations.

The talk will cover practical considerations for small-scale agent development, including modular design patterns, simple safety guardrails, and a brief overview of deployment options. Attendees will also see how Python developers can explore the ADK open-source ecosystem to extend agents for their own projects.

This session is designed for Python developers, ML engineers, and open-source contributors interested in hands-on, practical experience with agentic AI. By the end, participants will have a working Python agent, a clear understanding of workflow orchestration, and the confidence to start building and extending LLM agents using Google ADK.

results.title: Build Your Own Linter: Empower Your Python Code with Custom Static Analysis
results.abstract: Ever wondered how tools like Flake8, Ruff, or Pylint actually “understand” your Python code? In this beginner-friendly session, we demystify static analysis and walk through building your own mini-linter from scratch using Python’s ast module. You’ll learn how to parse code, detect patterns, enforce conventions, and design rules tailored to your team or project — all without needing compiler-level knowledge.
results.description:
Static analysis often feels magical — linters spot errors before you run your program, enforce style, and even suggest improvements. But behind the scenes, these tools are just smart tree-walkers powered by Python’s abstract syntax tree (AST). In this session, we break down that magic into simple, approachable concepts.

We’ll start with the fundamentals: what ASTs are, how Python turns source code into a tree, and how you can walk that tree to find exactly what you need. Then, through hands-on examples, we’ll build a tiny linter together — writing custom rules like detecting unused variables, enforcing naming patterns, or flagging risky code constructs. No prior knowledge of parsers or compilers required.

By the end of the session, you’ll walk away with the skills — and inspiration — to craft your own code analyzer tailored to your team’s coding style or project standards. Whether you want to automate reviews, enforce architecture rules, or simply understand your codebase better, building your own linter opens up a whole new world of possibilities.

0. Setup & Warm-Up (5 min)

Quick intro + what we’ll build

Ensure everyone can run Python files

1. Static Analysis 101 (10 min)

What static analysis is

Why linters exist (quality, automation, CI)

How Python turns code into AST (visual example)

2. Python’s AST Module Basics (15 min)

ast.parse() walkthrough

Understanding core node types (FunctionDef, Assign, Call)

Building a simple NodeVisitor

Hands-on mini exercise: Print all function names in a file.

3. Writing Your First Rule (20 min)

Concept of a “rule” in a linter

Implementing one basic rule (e.g., disallow print() in code)

Capturing filename, line number, and a clear message

Hands-on: Participants implement their first rule.

4. Building a Mini Linter Engine (20 min)

Rule registry system

Running multiple rules in sequence

Simple CLI runner (python mylinter.py file.py)

Pretty-printing results

Hands-on: Add a second rule (e.g., long function detector).

5. Guided Hands-On: Build 2 Custom Rules (25 min)

Participants choose and implement any two:

Naming convention checker (snake_case)

Detecting unused variables

Flagging banned imports

Detecting large functions or too many arguments

Instructor assists, shows patterns.

6. Enhancing the Linter (15 min)

Severity levels (warning vs. error)

Config support (config.json or .yaml)

Adding colored output

Integrating with pre-commit or CI

7. Wrap-Up + Resources + Q&A (10 min)

Recap: AST → rules → linter engine

Future upgrades: auto-fixes, type-aware rules, plugin system

Share GitHub scaffolding and learning resources

results.title: Building Multi-Agent Systems with Google ADK
results.abstract: Learn to architect multi-agent systems from the ground up using the Google ADK Python package. This end-to-end workshop covers everything from basic principles to advanced application. You will walk away ready to start building the specific AI agents you need.
results.description: In this hands-on workshop, you will be learning three different topics: agent building foundation, equipping agents with tools, and orchestrating the agents to build more advanced and complex multi-agent architectures, You will need to bring your own laptop and have a gmail address to sign up for a free billing account.
results.notes: I will cover required cloud credits for this lab. Also, I would like to ask whether there will be volunteers who can help guide the attendees.

results.title: Policy-as-Code for Python SaaS: Multi-Tenant RBAC/ABAC with Open Policy Agent
results.abstract:
Modern Python SaaS applications are increasingly multi-tenant, distributed, and subject to stricter security and compliance requirements. In many teams though, authorization is still implemented as ad hoc checks like `if user.is_admin` scattered through application logic. This tight coupling of business logic and access control makes it hard to reason about who can do what, nearly impossible to audit, and painful to evolve when requirements change or new tenants arrive.

In this talk, we introduce policy-as-code as an alternative, using Open Policy Agent (OPA) and its declarative policy language Rego to centralize and externalize authorization decisions for Python services. OPA is a Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) graduated project, which means it has a mature ecosystem and is widely adopted across cloud-native platforms and applications. Rego policies describe what should be allowed in a clear, testable way, while your Python code focuses on how to serve requests.

We will design a multi-tenant RBAC/ABAC model for a Python SaaS, implement it in Rego, and integrate it into a microservice in a demo. Attendees will leave with a practical understanding of why embedding authorization directly in application code does not scale, how OPA and Rego address those drawbacks, and concrete patterns and snippets they can adapt to their own SaaS architectures.
results.description:
This session begins by grounding the audience in authorization: deciding what an authenticated user is allowed to do, and how that differs from authentication, which only answers who they are. We will look at how Python teams traditionally implement authorization with ad hoc conditionals (`if user.is_admin`), framework-specific hooks, and scattered permission checks across views, services, and database layers. While this works at first, it quickly becomes fragile in a multi-tenant SaaS world, making it difficult to understand who can do what, hard to audit and test, and painful to update when new roles, tenants, or compliance rules appear.

From there, we introduce Open Policy Agent (OPA) as a general-purpose, CNCF-graduated policy engine and Rego as its declarative policy language for expressing authorization rules over structured data such as API requests and resource metadata. We then present a fictional but realistic Python SaaS application, a multi-tenant project management service, and use it as the running example for the rest of the talk.

First, we design a tenant-scoped RBAC model (tenant admins, project owners, members, read-only users) and layer ABAC conditions on top, using attributes such as tenant, region, sensitivity, and status to express fine-grained authorization without exploding the number of roles. This includes per-tenant customization, where tenant-specific configuration is modeled as OPA data and Rego reads it to enforce stricter or specialized rules, for example, additional approval steps or business-hour restrictions.

Next, we connect these policies to a Python microservice. We show how to assemble a rich JSON input object in a FastAPI app and query OPA over HTTP, following the “decision-as-a-service” pattern. From there, we pivot to in-process Rego, using regopy (a Python wrapper over the rego-cpp interpreter) and Python bindings for Regorus, a fast Rust-based Rego engine, to evaluate the same policies directly inside the Python process.

We close by comparing the sidecar and in-process approaches in terms of latency, deployment complexity, language interoperability, and policy reuse, and we highlight how teams can mix both: some services call a central OPA instance, while latency-sensitive Python services embed Rego natively using the growing polyglot tooling around rego-cpp and Regorus.

results.title: From Kernel to Framework: The Complete Journey of an HTTP Request in Python
results.abstract:
Ever wondered what really happens when someone hits your FastAPI endpoint? This talk traces the complete journey of an *HTTP* request, from the moment network packets arrive at your server's *NIC*, through the *Linux kernel*, into Python's `asyncio` event loop and finally to your *ASGI* application.

We'll explore kernel socket buffers, system calls, `epoll/kqueue` multiplexing and the *ASGI* protocol, connecting low-level OS concepts to high-level Python frameworks. You'll see how a single async thread can handle thousands of concurrent connections and understand why *ASGI* is designed the way it is.

By the end, you’ll understand what really happens **"under the hood"** every time your Python web app handles a request, enabling better debugging, performance tuning and architectural decision-making.
results.description:
Most Python developers interact with *HTTP* only at the framework level, writing endpoints, returning responses and letting tools like *Uvicorn* or *FastAPI* handle the rest. But every *HTTP* request takes a far longer journey before it reaches your application. This talk unpacks that entire path in a clear, layered, and visual way.

We start at the operating system. When a client sends a request, the NIC receives packets and hands them to the Linux kernel’s `TCP/IP` stack. The kernel manages connection handshakes, maintains accept and receive queues and buffers incoming bytes until a Python process is ready to read them.

From there, we enter Python’s runtime. You’ll learn how *ASGI* servers set up listening sockets, how the event loop uses mechanisms like *epoll* or *kqueue* to detect readiness, and how the server reads incoming bytes without blocking the entire process.

Next, we'll see the raw *HTTP* bytes: parsing method, path, headers, and body. These pieces are then transformed into an *ASGI* `scope` the structured representation passed into every ASGI application.


Finally, we follow the response back through the server, into the kernel’s send buffers, through *TCP* segmentation, and back to the client.

By visualizing this full lifecycle end-to-end, from kernel to Python to *ASGI*, you'll gain a deeper, more intuitive knowledge for debugging, performance tuning and building reliable web systems.
results.notes:
## Talk Outline 

### Introduction 
- What happens between browser click and response?
- Why this matters: Performance, debugging, architecture decisions
- Our journey: 5 layers from hardware to application

### Hardware & Kernel
- Network card interrupts and DMA transfers
- TCP/IP stack processing in kernel space
- Socket buffers: The kernel-userspace bridge

### System Calls & File Descriptors
- Sockets as file descriptors
- System call overhead: Crossing the privilege boundary
- Blocking vs non-blocking sockets
- Why blocking I/O doesn't scale

### Event Loops & I/O Multiplexing
- How to handle thousands of connections in one thread?
- How asyncio wraps epoll/kqueue
- Event loop processing multiple sockets
- Performance comparison: Threading vs async (with graphs)

### ASGI Protocol
- The ASGI interface: scope, receive, send
- How Uvicorn bridges sockets and applications
- Complete flow from socket to FastAPI handler

### Putting It All Together
- Following one request through all layers
- Latency breakdown: Where time is actually spent

results.title: From One Agent to Many — Orchestrating Durable Multi‑Agent Systems in Python
results.abstract: Most teams begin with a single large language model bot and encounter challenges such as shallow domain expertise, limited throughput, and difficult security separation. This talk presents Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF), a new toolkit for building cooperative, specialized agents that operate reliably at enterprise scale. Attendees will learn about the core components including the orchestrator, intent classification, agent registry, supervised hierarchies, and abstractions for threads, memory, and state, along with MAF's integration with Semantic Kernel and Azure for deployment. MAF introduces built-in durable threads, stateful memory, and orchestration patterns like parallel, sequential, group chat, and handoff, enabling robust workflows. Compared to traditional single bot setups, MAF provides improved long-running execution, granular governance, and modular flexibility. The session includes a comparison with LangChain and AutoGen and practical Python scenarios such as code migration pipelines and incident response, empowering you to choose the right approach for your needs.
results.description:
Introduction and goals (2 min)
	• Introduces the speaker, and explains the session’s objectives and relevance.

Motivation for multi-agent systems (4 min)
	• Explains limitations of single LLM agent, such as restricted domain expertise and throughput.
	• Discusses how agent specialization and coordinated execution solve these challenges.

Core concepts of the Microsoft Agent Framework (7 min)
	• Describes key elements like the orchestrator, intent classifier, and agent registry.
	• Covers how agent memory and state management enable persistence and recovery within multi-agent workflows.

Implementation in Python (5 min)
	• Shows how agents use threads as sessions and how to start, resume, or hand off threads.
	• Details persistence strategies while considering conflict avoidance and privacy issues in state management.

Reusable orchestration patterns (4 min)
	• Overviews orchestration methods such as parallel, sequential, group chat, handoff, and hierarchical supervision.
	• Discusses when to choose each approach and highlights common pitfalls and how to avoid them.

Comparison with other frameworks (4 min)
	• Provides a brief analysis of MAF versus LangChain, Semantic Kernel and AutoGen.
	• Offers guidance on selecting the best framework for Python projects.

Case studies and demonstrations (2 min)
	• Demonstrates workflow traces and slides for practical understanding.

Q&A and wrap-up (2 min)
	• Addresses practical concerns around costs, latency, governance, and next steps.
	• Offers resources and suggestions for future exploration.
results.notes: I am a solutions engineer at Microsoft Japan, collaborating with enterprise teams to implement, review, and deploy AI agents across regulated industries. Drawing on real-world experience with orchestration, memory and state management, governance, and major frameworks, I will share Python-friendly patterns and practical insights for adopting MAF and related tooling.

results.title: Unlocking Python’s GIL: A batch executor framework
results.abstract:
In this talk, I will discuss how we can use the Python GIL disablement to create a batch process framework.

With the ability to manage the Interpreter execution through the InterpreterPoolExecutor , I will describe how multiple interpreter instances can run independently at scheduled times - creating a very efficient batch framework leveraging the mutiple cores to run batch jobs simultaneously and in a robusst way.
results.description:
I have beeen very interested in using python for scheduling, but it was dependent on a main python file running multiple loops in async or other such hacks. 

Although the main purpose of removal of the GIL seems to be So CPython can use multiple CPU cores for CPU-bound work in a single process, but I will use it to demonstrate the ability to create a functional batch framework which is more efficient than single threaded options before the removal of GIL.

The batch framework is already POCed in a repo I have created, but I will improve it with a UI and CLI to control the interpreter runs, errors etc.

I will highlight its efficiency compared to airflow or luigi (not done yet - but I will include it in the deck).

I will also try to see if it can have some advantages when run for interdependent ML tasks which need scheduliing controls - I am not sure If there are advantages, but I will study and brrng up my study

results.title: From Developer to DevRel: 50 Talks Later
results.abstract:
What motivates someone to go on stage to give a talk once, twice… 50 times? When you are an artist, it is part of the job. But when you are a developer?

In this talk, I will walk you through my journey from developer to DevRel and share both the how and the why. What keeps me going, flying to the next event over and over, sometimes every day. I will talk about the unexpected consequences, good and bad, of constant travel and living out of a suitcase. I will also share what DevRel means to me personally, both as a DevRel and as one of the founding members at a startup, and what it means in general beyond the free travel and conferences.
results.description: I will share my perspective on the mental blockers I often see in people who want to try public speaking but never take the first step, and how to overcome them. So if you have always wanted to get on stage but do not know what to talk about, or maybe never even considered it before, I hope this session will give you a clearer picture of what DevRel really looks like, whether it might be for you, and some practical ways to start speaking even when your mind tries to talk you out of it.

results.title: Build, Deploy, Monetize: The Future of Developer Economy
results.abstract: The Creator Developer Economy is rapidly reshaping how developers turn technical skills into sustainable, independent income. In this session, we’ll explore practical ways developers can build, deploy, and monetize data-driven automation and web-scraping solutions—using both open-source tools and hosted platforms.
results.description:
This talk will walk through the full lifecycle of creating production-ready scrapers and automation workflows in four steps:

- Development – Comparing popular open-source scraping and automation tools such as Crawlee for Python, Scrapy, Playwright, and Selenium, and discussing when to choose each.

- Publication & Monetization Setup – Exploring options for distributing your tools, from publishing open-source packages to hosting reusable “agents” on platforms like Apify or deploying them independently.

- Testing & Reliability – Demonstrating best practices for debugging, monitoring, and scaling scraping/automation solutions.

- Promotion & Community Building – Sharing strategies for developer visibility: documentation, GitHub presence, community engagement, Discord groups, and contributing to open-source ecosystems.

The session will also feature a live demo showing how to quickly build and deploy an automation workflow using the Apify CLI alongside examples built with open-source alternatives. Attendees will see how different tools approach the same problem, helping them make informed architectural choices.

To conclude, we’ll highlight real stories of developers who have built meaningful income streams—both through open-source projects and through hosted automation platforms—illustrating the diverse opportunities in today’s developer-led economy.
results.notes: I am a seasoned speaker and have spoken at more than 20 PyCon so far from Africa to Europe to Asia, everywhere. Would love to meet PythonAsia community and talk with them.

results.title: Seeing the Invisible: Practical Approaches to Observability in AI-Powered Python Systems
results.abstract: AI systems behave very differently from traditional applications — they involve model calls, vector DB lookups, async pipelines, and nondeterministic behaviour. In this talk, we explore practical ways to bring observability into these AI-driven Python systems. Through real-world patterns, we’ll combine structured logging with structlog, distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry, and actionable metrics you can ship to Prometheus or any backend. You’ll learn how to track latency in each ML stage, debug production model issues, and build visibility into high-throughput pipelines without drowning in data.
results.description:
Observability becomes critical when your Python application includes AI components like LLM calls, feature pipelines, embeddings generation, or retrieval-augmented workflows. Traditional logging is rarely enough — without structure, correlation, or performance insight, debugging becomes slow and costly. This talk brings clarity by breaking down observability into three pillars: structured logs, traces, and metrics, and shows how to combine them into one cohesive, developer-friendly workflow.

We’ll walk through real code examples using structlog for contextual logging, OpenTelemetry for end-to-end tracing, and lightweight metric instrumentation to understand throughput, latency, error patterns, and model performance. You’ll see how each request flows through your AI pipeline: prompt creation → model call → vector store → post-processing — all visible, traceable, and measurable.

Attendees will walk away with a plug-and-play template they can apply instantly to FastAPI, Django, or any Python service using LLMs or custom ML models. No specialized infra knowledge required — only a willingness to bring order to the chaos of modern AI systems.

results.title: Raspberry Pi : Building Edge AI on the Pi
results.abstract:
In this talk, I will talk about how we can run AI models on Small devices. I will talk about the architecture of smaller models FFNs, RNNs, CNNs that can interface with sensor inputs to power various use cases.

I will use Raspberry Pi Ver 4 to demonstrate the use cases and talk about edge intelligence use cases and its advantages.
results.description: This talk will be about Edge AI and Various architectures which can run on raspberry Pi. I will discuss the importance of Edge AI and how Raspberry Pi platform can be used to test tiny Models with small footprints.

results.title: Build a Better PyCon: My Annual Reflection
results.abstract:
Every year, PyCon organizers face not only logistical and operational challenges but also emotional and mental ones. Behind the success of each conference are countless hours of coordination, problem-solving, and reflection.
In this talk, I’ll share my journey as a senior organizer of PyCon Hong Kong — the mental resilience needed to handle pressure, the practical steps we take to improve year after year, and how we continue to find meaning in community work. From sponsorship strategy and volunteer coordination to maintaining team motivation and personal balance, this session offers both practical insights and honest reflections.
My goal is to inspire fellow organizers to pause, reflect, and keep improving — not just to build a better PyCon, but to build a healthier, happier community behind it.
results.description:
Outline: 
2 mins - My personal background in last 10 years+ on PyCon and Open Source Conference 
6 mins - Issue on my PyCon experience, from operation to community building
10 mins - How to build a better conference 
4 mins - Behind the Scene: The Mental Side of Organizing
4 mins - From Reflection to Action 
4 mins - Balancing Passion, People, and Pressure

Target Audience: 
Community builder / Conference organizer community
Volunteer whom want to participate more conference design.
results.notes: Just wanna boost up some committees around Asia whom struggle every year.

results.title: Inside a Database: A Code-Level Walkthrough of an RDBMS I Built in Python
results.abstract:
This talk explores the inner workings of an RDBMS through KeiPyDB, a custom RDBMS I implemented in Python.We will follow the processing flow of a SELECT statement step by step, covering lexical analysis, parsing, abstract syntax tree construction, query planning, and execution, using code examples and debugger output.
The talk also covers the storage layer. By inspecting actual data files with a hex viewer, we will observe how pages and records are stored on disk, and how INSERT and DELETE operations modify them.

The goal of this session is to provide a clear, concrete understanding of the main components that make up an RDBMS. It is intended for attendees who are familiar with SQL but have not yet examined how a database processes queries internally.
results.description:
As generative AI becomes increasingly prevalent in code generation, understanding "why this code works" becomes more important than ever. Relational databases are used every day, yet many developers may not be familiar with how a query is processed internally. This talk explains the key internal components of an RDBMS by examining KeiPyDB, a custom implementation written in Python.
Structure
- Introduction (3 min) : Speaker introduction, motivation for building KeiPyDB, and high-level architecture overview
- SQL Parser and Execution Engine (15 min) : Using a SELECT statement as an example, we will trace how SQL is tokenized by the lexer, parsed into an AST, transformed by the query planner, and executed by the executor, with Python code and debugger output.
- Disk I/O and Storage Format (8 min) We will inspect actual data files with a hex viewer to observe how INSERT and DELETE operations modify physical storage.
- Endianness (2 min) Brief explanation of endianness and its role in binary record formats.

An RDBMS consists of multiple subsystems—lexer, parser, planner, executor, and storage layer—each with a clearly defined responsibility. By following the flow of a query step by step, this session will provide attendees with a practical understanding of how relational databases process and store data internally.
results.notes:
A Japanese version of this talk was presented at PyCon JP 2025 (https://2025.pycon.jp/en/timetable/talk/D7D9R3). 

The complete source code for KeiPyDB is publicly available at https://github.com/kei-kmj/KeiPyDB, demonstrating that this is a working implementation rather than a conceptual talk.

slide at PyCon JP 2025
https://pycon2025-231bt38co-kei-kmjs-projects.vercel.app/

results.title: Dissecting ASGI Protocol: Craft Your Own Web Framework
results.abstract:
Have you ever wondered what really happens inside frameworks like **FastAPI** or **Starlette**?
In this hands-on workshop, we will demystify the **ASGI protocol**, the foundation that powers modern, high-performance Python web frameworks and walk step by step through building your own ASGI-compliant micro framework from scratch.

We will start from the basics, handling raw HTTP requests with `asyncio` and then gradually build up the layers: ASGI scopes, message passing, routing, and middleware. Along the way, you will learn how the same principles make frameworks like FastAPI so fast and flexible.

By the end of the session, you will have both a clear understanding of how ASGI works and a simple working framework that you built yourself.
results.description:
Modern Python web frameworks are built on **ASGI (Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface)**, a protocol that enables true concurrency, `WebSockets`, and `async I/O`. But while most developers use ASGI through frameworks like **FastAPI** or **Starlette**, few truly understand what happens under the hood.

This workshop takes you on a guided journey through the entire ASGI stack from network I/O to framework internals by building your own minimal ASGI web framework step by step.

You will learn how an ASGI server like **Uvicorn** communicates with your app using the `scope`, `receive`, and `send` interfaces. Then, we will implement simple routing, middleware, and request handling to bring it all together.

This is not another FastAPI tutorial, it’s about understanding the architecture that powers the modern Python web ecosystem and experiencing the magic of ASGI firsthand.
results.notes:
### **Workshop Outline**

* **Introduction to ASGI and async I/O**
  Get familiar with why *ASGI* was introduced, how it differs from *WSGI*, and the fundamentals of async I/O in Python.

* **Setting up an Async Server**
  Use `asyncio.start_server` to handle TCP connections and see how raw requests flow through the network stack.

* **Parsing HTTP and Creating ASGI Scopes**
  Convert raw request bytes into structured data, method, path, headers and build the *ASGI* scope object.

* **Implementing the ASGI Protocol**
  Write your own `receive` and `send` functions, and build a minimal ASGI app that responds to requests.

* **Adding Routing and Middleware**
  Implement a simple router and middleware layer, the foundation of frameworks like Starlette.

* **Connecting the Dots**
  Compare your implementation to how Starlette and FastAPI handle requests internally, and discuss how these frameworks extend ASGI for real-world applications.

* **Wrap-up and Q&A**
  Recap what you’ve built, highlight the key lessons, and share resources for learning more about ASGI and async systems.

results.title: Let's live code a game with Arcade in less than 30 minutes!
results.abstract: Arcade is a game programming engine built on top of Python. Compared to pygame, Arcade uses OOP concepts to speed up development of games. It also comes with built-in collision detection and other functionality useful to making a game!
results.description:
We will be making a game in less than 30 minutes. The speaker will use live coding to showcase how easy and fast it is to make a game.

We will be using the game programming engine, Arcade. It has a lot of built-in functionality used for game development.

You will learn how to make a game and if you're a beginner, you will see how Object Oriented Programming (OOP) is used in video games.

results.title: Getting started in quantum programming with GPUs using CUDA-Q
results.abstract: The age of quantum computing is upon us. But how can we prepare for this new type of programming in Python without a quantum computer hooked to our rigs or with free access? One good way is to prepare our code by running it on GPUs as quantum simulators. NVIDIA CUDA-Q enables the development of pure or hybrid quantum programs that run on both GPUs and quantum computers. In this workshop, we will cover an introduction to quantum programming and how to use NVIDIA CUDA-Q to run basic quantum programs, and we will see examples of how to apply them to applied cases such as quantum machine learning and optimization.
results.description:
This workshop will focus on laying out the fundamentals of quantum programming, primarily through Python coding and a few key math and physics concepts. Specifically, we will cover:
* What are qubits and quantum registers
* Quantum circuits and kernels
* Designing a simple hybrid quantum algorithm

Bonus demos: quantum neural networks and quantum optimization in CUDA-Q
This workshop is designed for Pythonistas interested in quantum computing who have experience with NumPy or CuPy. **You don’t have to be a math or physics major to join this workshop**. No access to an actual quantum computer is required. After this workshop, participants can prepare hybrid quantum-ready workflows for NVIDIA GPUs and different types of quantum computers.

results.title: Increasing trust in the software supply chain with Trusted Publishing and Digital Attestations
results.abstract:
Supply chain attacks are becoming increasingly common, targeting popular packages (and their maintainers) that power modern applications.

This talk examines two (relatively new) security features in PyPI (Python Package Index): Trusted Publishing and Digital Attestations. We'll explore how these features are being adopted in the broader open-source ecosystem, and how these security features can be used by maintainers, security researchers, and developers.
results.description:
Learn how to configure trusted publishing for your packages, and how to produce and consume digital attestations from PyPI (Python Package Index).

Trusted Publishing -- a recommended security capability by the OpenSSF Securing Software Repositories Working Group -- increases security by as it removes the need to securely manage a long-lived API token in the build system. This method builds on top of the OIDC (OpenID Connect) standard and works through exchanging OIDC identity tokens for short-lived and tightly scoped API tokens for authenticating with package repository publishing APIs.

Digital Attestations provide verifiable proof of software provenance using cryptographic signatures. These signatures bind a package to a strong cryptographic digest of its contents, allowing both PyPI and downstream users to verify that a particular package was attested to by a particular identity (i.e. a Trusted Publisher such as a GitHub Actions workflow).
results.notes:
I have presented a version of this talk to my local Python WA meetup group (Perth, Australia).

- [Annotated slides of the talk](https://jduabe.dev/posts/2025/trusted-publishing-attestations/)
- [Python WA September meetup](https://www.meetup.com/pythonwa/events/308746488/)

I have also built a couple of small side-projects that are related to Trusted Publishing and Digital Attestations:

- [npm package provenance stats](https://jduabe.dev/posts/2025/npm-package-provenance-stats/): A website that shows which of the top 500 packages from npm have been uploaded with Digital Attestations  (inspired by [Are we PEP 740 yet?](https://trailofbits.github.io/are-we-pep740-yet/) which does the same thing but for packages in PyPI)
- [Are we Trusted Publishing yet?](https://jduabe.dev/posts/2025/are-we-trusted-publishing-yet/): A timeline that shows which package registries e.g. PyPI, npm, RubyGems, etc. support trusted publishing and when it was added h

results.title: Python and Structures: Python in the Structural Engineering Industry
results.abstract: In this talk, I will give an overview of two of the uses of Python for structural engineers. First, Python can be used to "control" commercial structural engineering software using the _Application Programming Interface_ (API), and even add features and workflow improvements that these software do not provide out of the box. Second, several Python libraries such as `pyniteFEA`, `openseespy`, `concreteproperties` and `steelpy` can be used to analyze structures using the _finite element method_ (FEM). The latter use has the advantages of being free to use compared to commercial software, and being able to elevate the creation of design templates that we traditionally do using spreadsheets.
results.description:
This talk is catered towards
- civil and structural engineers who want to elevate their practice through the incorporation of Python in their workflows,
- Python programmers who create software solutions as a solo developer or as part of a team/company, and
- Python programmers who are curious and interested in the recent usage of Python in various fields in the industry (in this case, structural engineering).

Proposed outline of the talk
- Traditional workflow in the structural design of structures
- Part 1: Communicating with the software via API using Python
- A quick overview of the finite element method (FEM)
- Part 2: Python libraries for structural engineering and FEM
results.notes:
Proposed timing of the outline (total 30 min)
- 5 min
- 5 min
- 5 min
- 10 min
- Q&A: 5 min

References
[`pyniteFEA` Python library](https://github.com/JWock82/PyNite)
[`openseespy` Python library](https://openseespydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)
[ETABS OpenAPI by ReFrame Software](https://youtu.be/VlnJCV_cy8I?si=WaaE-AYSi2CsIx1u)

Other information
