results.title: Yellow Cab, Jollybee, Haircuts and Smoothies: Building Legendary Communities through experiences beyond the walls
results.abstract:
In the late 2000's to Early 2010's, I visited the Philippines a total of 6 times while serving in the United States Marine Corps. There I had found memories of enjoying my time with others and sharing in the amazing food and experiences. In the moment, the experiences were great but they didn't become legendary until word got around and the hype was built.

In 2026 much of our storytelling has changed from diving deep to buzz and hype in shortform. FOMO has never been higher but it's from the comfort of our couches and glossed by AI summary and algorithm-chasing. This is coming at the cost of community. ""Why participate when I can get the talks online and AI can teach me all the things""?

This talk tells stories of community and legend. It shares how communities can grow from ice cream parlors and random meetings at 5am morning walks.
results.description:
This talk share about how community grows outside the venue walls. It addresses the origins of community growth and learning about new things from my experiences in the Philippines 15 years ago and how some of the staples of those deployments became legendary.

Then the talk transitions to the legends made in my decade of being in the community. The secret, legends happen outside the walls of the conference.

Lastly, I'll showcase how a thought can start at the conference and only truly develop when you have the ability to develop it over time with the people that you meet at the conference.

The community is a place to learn but the benefit is beyond the talks and comes from the power in how a talk can bring people into a room that will change the Python world forever!

results.title: Secure AI via Local Agentic RAG (Offline, No API Use)
results.abstract: Running AI inference locally, processing AI models on an organization’s own hardware, such as on-premises servers or devices, rather than relying on cloud-based services, has become an increasingly popular choice across various industries. The primary appeal lies in the enhanced control and security it offers over sensitive data. In this workshop session, we will build how to build a completely local Agentic RAG without any external APIs using an Open-Source LLM (Gemma or Mistral) and Qdrant Vector Search Engine, both running locally on Google Colab. We will also trace and monitor the application using Comet OPIK, which is completely open source.
results.description:
With all the improvements and releases made in AI, should your data ever leave your machine? For many Python developers with an ML background, the answer is a resounding "no". Similarly, many organizations struggle with privacy and security while working with Large Language Model applications. 

With open source/weights LLMs getting slowly better,  in this workshop session, we will build a complete hands-on prototype of a local-first Agentic RAG system where you have complete sovereignty over your data, your models, and your entire tech stack. The end goal for this workshop is to understand how to manage sensitive data without relying on external APIs. I will also address how one can utilize the memory tradeoffs technique from the Qdrant, and further have the evals pipeline (Metrics-based) for the same application. We will also trace and monitor the application using Comet OPIK, which is completely open source.
results.notes:
Breakdown:
- Talk about the 7 failure points in RAG (slides)
- Address the issues with search and how to build a Hybrid search pipeline using a local tech stack without any APIs. (slides)
- Memory tradeoff for the RAG when running the app locally. (slides)
- Code demonstration using LangGraph (for statement management), Colab (free GPU instance), Qdrant (Vector search engine), and Monitoring (OPIK).

results.title: Why Python Matters in Basic Education: Building Thinkers, Not Just Coders
results.abstract: Across the past decade, education research has shifted from teaching code through strict syntax to building broad problem-solving skills. Python fits this shift well because it is clear, flexible, and beginner friendly. This session highlights global trends in computational thinking and explains why Python works as a foundation for K to 12 learners. It helps students focus on logic, structure, and creative problem solving, which strengthens digital literacy and prepares them for a tech-driven future.
results.description: Many classrooms still rely on heavy syntax drills that leave beginners frustrated. Research shows that students learn better when lessons center on thinking skills rather than memorizing rules. Python supports this approach because its clean design lets learners focus on reasoning. The session connects key findings in computational thinking research with practical examples, from block-based transitions to simple robotics and data tasks. Attendees will see how Python can help schools worldwide build confident problem solvers who can adapt to any digital tool they meet later on.

results.title: How Python is Powering the Next Generation of Intelligent Physical Products- for Keynote Talk
results.abstract: The future of startups isn't just in apps and websites—it's in intelligent physical products that sense, think, and act. From smart agriculture and healthcare devices to robotics and autonomous systems, Python has emerged as the unexpected champion of hardware innovation. This keynote explores how a language traditionally known for web development and data science is now powering breakthrough hardware products with embedded machine learning. Discover why startups are choosing Python to build everything from IoT sensors to robotic systems, and learn how this democratizes hardware innovation for founders who never thought they could build physical products. Real-world examples, live demonstrations, and a vision of where Python-powered hardware is taking us next.
results.description:
The Hardware Revolution Running on Python
We're entering an era where intelligence moves from the cloud to the edge—where devices don't just collect data, they make decisions. Coffee machines that predict maintenance needs. Agricultural sensors that optimize crop yields in real-time. Wearable health monitors that detect anomalies before symptoms appear. What's surprising? Many of these innovations are built with Python.
This keynote takes startup founders, entrepreneurs, and innovators on a journey through the emerging world of Python-powered physical hardware and machine learning. You'll discover why Python—a language many associate only with websites and data analysis—has become the secret weapon for hardware startups building intelligent physical products.
What Makes Python Perfect for Hardware Innovation
From Prototype to Product in One Language
Traditional hardware development required teams fluent in C++, electronics engineering, and separate languages for data processing. Python changes this. Build your entire product—hardware control, data processing, machine learning, and user interface—in a single, readable language. This means faster iteration, smaller teams, and lower costs for startups.
Machine Learning at the Edge
Python's rich ML ecosystem (TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn) now runs on everything from Raspberry Pi to specialized edge AI chips. Your device can learn, adapt, and make intelligent decisions without requiring constant cloud connectivity. This opens possibilities for autonomous robots, offline medical devices, and industrial sensors in remote locations.
The Arduino to AI Pipeline
See how Python seamlessly connects the physical and digital worlds—from reading sensors to training neural networks to controlling actuators. One language, complete control.
results.notes:
This is an inspirational, forward-looking keynote designed to open minds to possibilities rather than teach specific implementation details. It's perfect for the main stage because it:

Appeals to diverse audience (technical and non-technical founders)
Showcases innovation and emerging opportunities
Demonstrates regional relevance (agriculture, infrastructure)
Inspires without requiring prerequisite knowledge
Includes impressive visual demonstrations

results.title: Unfreezing Data: Why Polars is the Next Big Thing in Python Data Analysis
results.abstract:
We all know that **Python's DataFrames** rolls as fast as **pandas**, but have you ever thought about whether it can move at a **polar** bear speed? Enter **Polars**, the next generation DataFrame library built on Rust and Apache Arrow that is designed for speed, efficiency, and simplicity.

In this talk, we'll explore what Polars bring to the Python data ecosystem, why it matters for both **beginners and professionals** alike, and how you can start using it today. We'll *unfreeze* your data skills and show you how Polars help you work smarter, not slower. Along the way, we'll highlight how it differs from **pandas**, but mainly focus on how and why **Polars** work.
results.description:
# Description
**Pandas** has always been the default DataFrame library in Python for who knows how long, but as datasets grow, its limits become more noticeable. Enter **Polars**, an open-source library for data manipulation, known for being one of the fastest data processing solutions on a single machine. It's Rust-powered, it has lazy evaluation, built-in parallelism, all the while keeping Python syntax accessible. The talk will be mainly introducing **Polars** to the audience while briefly comparing it to its counterpart, **pandas**.

# Outline

## Opening: Why Look Beyond Pandas? (3-5 mins)
In this section, we'll do a quick discussion of why we'd want to consider using **Polars** than **pandas**. We'll show them both the strengths and weakness of **pandas**. Furthermore, We'll be looking at some articles and blogs of where companies have started shifting from using pandas to using polars instead to optimize their workflows.

## What is Polars? (20 mins)
In this section, it will be a combination of live-demo and discussion of what Polars is. We'll be showing what Polars brings to the table such as: speed, syntax, lazy API, it's philosophies, etc.
I am hoping that this will be a beginner-intermediate talk, hence why we'll be going from the basic of Polars such as how you can read a csv, some performance metrics comparison of both pandas and polars, showing basic querying of Polars, and highlighting the readability of Polars. Not just that, but also show how we can integrate Polars in a professional setting.

## Wrap-Up: The Future of DataFrames (5 mins)
This is finally summarizing my whole talk about **Polars** and some of the key takeaways from the talk. This is the part where I try to encourage people to try out Polars and learn Polars along the way. I'd give some resources to learn Polars and finally *unfreeze* everyone's data skills.
results.notes:
## References
**Polars documentation** https://pola.rs/
**Polars blogs** https://pola.rs/posts/
**Polars source-code** https://github.com/pola-rs/polars

## Other Key Notes
The whole **What is Polars?** is just an idea of what could be spoken in this talk and will be organized into a more structured outline once I have created the idea I want to go with.

## Speaking Experience
- **Pixel Party: Walk the Dinosaur**: My first talk, very unprepared as I was a last-minute add-on (literally), where I talked about Python and Object-Oriented Programming to Grade 11-12 STEM students. It was a collaboration between an Organization (Club Simulation) and Devcon Kids.
- A seminar within our school, **National University of Dasmariñas**, where I talked about the reality of AI and debunking myths about it. The target audience were 1st years and 2nd years students.

results.title: Beyond the GIL: Let’s talk Python Concurrency Essentials
results.abstract:
Python’s Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is often portrayed as a limitation but it doesn’t have to be a blocker.  In this talk, we will discuss the core concepts behind Python concurrency and explore practical ways to work with  GIL rather than working against it. From threading to multiprocessing, async IO to real-world patterns, we will break down what’s really going on under the hood and when to use which approach.

Whether you are building data pipelines, web backends, or automating workflows, understanding concurrency is key to writing responsive and efficient Python code. I will use simple examples and real life scenarios to show how concurrency works in modern Python and share some hard earned lessons from debugging slow, blocking code in production systems.
results.description:
Concurrency is one of the most misunderstood yet powerful concepts in Python development. This session takes a practical dive into how Python handles concurrency  beyond the myths surrounding the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL). We will explore the mechanisms that make concurrent execution possible, compare different approaches like threading, multiprocessing, and asyncio, and discuss how to choose the right tool for the job.

Through clear examples and lessons drawn from real-world systems, this talk will show how to write efficient, responsive Python applications without falling into common concurrency traps. Attendees will walk away with a deeper understanding of how Python runs code behind the scenes, how to design programs that make the most of concurrency, and how to think about performance in a more holistic way.

Attendees will learn:
What the GIL is and how it affects concurrency in Python

When to use threads, processes, or asyncio (and when not to)

Practical patterns for real-world Python projects

How to think about performance and responsiveness beyond "faster code"

results.title: DsPy the Declarative Programming making prompts go brrrrrrr
results.abstract:
DSPy is a declarative framework for building modular AI software. It allows you to iterate fast on structured code, rather than brittle strings, and offers algorithms that compile AI programs into effective prompts and weights for your language models, whether you're building simple classifiers, sophisticated RAG pipelines, or Agent loops.

Instead of wrangling prompts or training jobs, DSPy (Declarative Self-improving Python) enables you to build AI software from natural-language modules and to generically compose them with different models, inference strategies, or learning algorithms. This makes AI software more reliable, maintainable, and portable across models and strategies.
results.description:
Table of contents
Setup and Bootstrapping DSPy
LLM powered Q&A Machine
Key DSPy concepts via code snippets
Signatures
Modules (e.g., Predict, ChainOfThought, ReAct, ProgramOfThought, MultiChainComparison)
Pipelines / Text-transformation graphs
Teleprompter Optimizers (prompt & weight optimizers)
Compiler (declarative-to-prompt/train code compiler)
Self‑improving (bootstrapping prompts/weights via feedback)
Assertions (computational constraints for module outputs)
Metrics (for evaluation and optimization targets)
Adapters (model-specific formatters and parser logic)
Inference strategies (e.g., few-shot, chain-of-thought, ReAct)
Composability / Modularity
Model agnosticism (swap models without rewrites)
results.notes: DSPy offers a suite of functionalities designed to simplify and improve the process of working with Large Language Models (LLMs). Instead of the traditional method of manually crafting prompts, DSPy allows developers to "program" LLMs using a structured, modular approach. In essence, DSPy shifts the focus from manually adjusting prompts to defining the task and allowing the framework to handle the complexities of optimization, resulting in more robust, scalable, and efficient LLM applications

results.title: Python for Post-Quantum Security: Simulating Cryptographic Transitions with Confidence
results.abstract: As quantum computing edges closer to practicality, cryptographic systems must evolve. This talk demonstrates how Python can simulate post-quantum cryptographic transitions—bridging classical and quantum-safe algorithms through modular, reproducible code. We’ll walk through a live demo of hybrid encryption, performance benchmarking, and risk visualization, empowering developers and researchers to prototype secure-by-design systems today. Whether you're in cybersecurity, fintech, or policy, this session equips you to think ahead—strategically, technically, and ethically.
results.description:
Why this matters: Quantum computing threatens to break widely used cryptographic schemes like RSA and ECC. But transitioning to post-quantum cryptography (PQC) isn’t just about swapping algorithms—it’s about understanding trade-offs, interoperability, and risk exposure during the migration phase.

What you’ll learn: In this 30-minute session, we’ll explore how Python can be used to simulate cryptographic transitions using hybrid encryption models (e.g., RSA + Kyber), benchmark performance, and visualize risk scenarios. You’ll see how modular Python code can help teams prototype, test, and communicate cryptographic strategies—without needing a PhD in quantum theory.

Live demo highlights:
1. Simulating hybrid encryption (RSA + Kyber) using Python wrappers
2. Benchmarking encryption/decryption speed and key sizes
3. Visualizing transition risk using matplotlib and pandas
4. Designing reproducible, modular code for teaching and auditing

Who should attend: Python developers, security engineers, researchers, educators, and anyone curious about the future of secure systems. No prior quantum background required—just a passion for building resilient systems.

results.title: Stop Writing ETL Scripts: Build Reliable ELT Pipelines with dbt
results.abstract:
Many Python developers build data pipelines the hard way; downloading data from different sources, applying transformations, and saving the final results to a database.
Sooner or later, though, they realize that data providers aren’t always as reliable as we’d like: null values appear, outliers slip in, and data integrity issues add unexpected complexity to their codebases.

But what if there were a better way to handle this?

In this talk, we’ll explore how dbt brings software engineering best practices such as modularity, testing, version control, and more to data transformations inside your data warehouse.
results.description:
- Introduction: Why to shift from ETL to ELT (5 min)
- DBT key concepts (10min)
- Structuring a dbt project (10 min)
results.notes:
Hi, I’m Roberto head of data engineering at Axyon AI, a pioneering Italian AI fintech company which improves asset manager performance by delivering alpha opportunities via AI-based rankings and AI model strategies.

I came as a speaker last year for the pycon APAC 2025, I would love to join the next year event

results.title: Supercharge your ML Research with PyTorch/XLA and Google Cloud TPUs
results.abstract:
This talk will demonstrate how **Python ML/DL Engineers** can accelerate model training and AI research using PyTorch/XLA and **Google Tensor Processing Units (TPUs)** at no cost.

Supported by **Google Research’s TPU Research Cloud (TRC) Team**, this talk will cover the **PyTorch/XLA** framework, key differences between GPUs and AI-specific chips like Google TPUs and AWS Tranium2, and a step-by-step guide to securing **free access** for your AI research.
results.description:
ML/DL Researchers have used AI Accelerators like powerful **NVIDIA GPUs**, but would you be surprised that there are faster alternatives you might not have heard of? Enter Deep Learning-specific chips, such as **Google Tensor Processing Units** and **AWS Tranium2**. These chips have gained popularity as we move into the age of AI and are accessible on platforms like **Google Colab** and **Kaggle**, matching and even outperforming GPUs (ex., TPU v4 > H100)

But how do the code and concepts differ from frameworks like **PyTorch, Tensorflow, or JAX**? How much is the performance increase of using AI Accelerators vs. GPUs? Is there deep coupling with the platforms, so we can be vendor locked? What are the pros and cons & when and when not to use them? Can we get access to these accelerators for free?

In this talk, we'll cover **PyTorch/XLA and Google TPUs**, and answer these questions. We'll go beyond Deep Learning fundamentals to focus on the **practical implementation** of **PyTorch/XLA**, comparing the low-level architecture and programming paradigms of GPUs and TPUs.

This talk will also introduce you to **Google's TPU Research Cloud (TRC)**, and briefly compare with competitor frameworks like **PyTorch NeuronX** and dedicated chips like **AWS Tranium2**.

This is an intermediate-level talk for Python programmers interested in accelerating their model development and AI Research using PyTorch and its extension, PyTorch/XLA.
results.notes:
This talk has been shared with various local communities in the Philippines, free and paid:
- **Google Cloud Manila's Devfest** (Oct 11, 2025): https://www.facebook.com/share/16dwmAQuRg
- **SOFTCON 2025** (Oct 17, 2025): https://softconph.com/schedule/

Both talks were and will be supported by **Google Research's TPU Research Cloud team**: https://sites.research.google/trc/about, and so will this be too. Slides for the previous talk are:
- [SOFTCON 2025](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1C6ccqrJz--90Po2eo1G8F4Uko0TOejT6SNwInZswiJ4/edit?usp=sharing)
- [Google Cloud Manila](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1TZMmXumbaCf4PEIHcNVOuhwniLFJyOvLjPoZrnanb78/edit?usp=sharing)

This talk will be supported by the speaker's affiliations with GitHub, Kollab, and FEU Institute of Technology.

results.title: The Python Autopsy: Reverse Engineering Advanced Attacks Through Packet Forensics
results.abstract:
When a sophisticated cyber-attack strikes, the network traffic is the unalterable crime scene. But how do you move from a packet capture (pcap) to a full understanding of the adversary's playbook? Forget expensive, open-source tool the most powerful forensic toolkit is already in your hands: Python.  In this talk, we'll perform a digital autopsy on a real-world advanced persistent threat (APT) simulation. We'll use Python to carve through megabytes of packet data, reconstruct malicious conversations, and extract the attacker's tools and techniques directly from the wire.
You will learn how to:
Leverage libraries like PyShark to seamlessly bridge the power of Wireshark with the flexibility of the Python data ecosystem.
Reverse-engineer network protocols by analyzing network flows and decrypting custom encryption schemes.
results.description:
A megabyte packets capture lands on your desk. The only clue: "Suspicious lateral movement." Opening this in a traditional forensic tool is a recipe for overwhelm. The real story is buried in the packets, waiting to be uncovered not by clicking through a GUI, but by writing code that can ask the right questions.
Join us for a deep dive where we treat a packet capture like a crime scene. Using Python as our primary tool, we will perform a live forensic investigation, stitching together a sophisticated attack chain from the raw network data. This is modern digital forensics: scriptable, repeatable, and powerful.
Our investigation will unfold step-by-step:
Rapid Triage: We'll start by parsing the massive PCAP programmatically, quickly separating normal noise from potentially malicious traffic.
Hunting the Attacker: We'll write scripts to expose the attacker's footprint—finding covert channels, detecting beaconing implants, and spotting protocol abuses that standard tools might miss.
Connecting the Dots: Each Python script will reveal a new piece of the puzzle, from initial compromise to lateral movement, building a comprehensive timeline of the breach.
You Will Learn:
Practical Skill: How to leverage Python libraries like PyShark to automate forensic tasks and analyze traffic at scale.
Actionable Methodology: A structured, repeatable process for investigating network-based incidents, from a single alert to a full attack narrative.
Deep Understanding: How to move beyond relying on Wireshark's query language and generated reports, toward building your own comprehensive, tailored forensic analysis tools.

results.title: Fast Pandas: Performance Tricks You Wish You Knew Earlier
results.abstract: Pandas is one of the most widely used tools in Python, yet many developers unintentionally write slow or memory heavy DataFrame code. This talk covers practical performance techniques that can significantly speed up Pandas workflows: vectorization, avoiding apply, optimizing data types, reducing memory usage, minimizing DataFrame copies, improving joins and groupbys, and using chunked loading for large files. We also look at when to extend Pandas with Polars, Apache Arrow, or DuckDB for faster execution. If you work with data at any scale, this session gives you simple, actionable tricks to make your Pandas pipelines faster and more production ready.
results.description:
Pandas is powerful, flexible, and easy to use, but it can also become painfully slow or memory hungry when the data grows or the operations get complex. Most performance problems in Pandas come from a small set of common patterns: unnecessary loops, incorrect dtypes, inefficient joins, and operations that silently create large copies behind the scenes.

In this talk, we explore practical, real world techniques to make Pandas fast without rewriting your entire pipeline or switching to heavier systems like Spark. You will learn how to diagnose slow DataFrame code, apply vectorization effectively, use categoricals to reduce memory, avoid hidden allocations, optimize I/O, and use modern tools like Polars or DuckDB when needed, while still keeping Pandas as the main tool in your workflow.

The session includes before and after examples, benchmarks, and lessons from real production data pipelines. Whether you are a backend engineer, data engineer, or ML practitioner, you will leave with tools and tricks that make your Pandas code much faster and more predictable.

results.title: Understanding Python's logging: Combine components like Lego blocks!
results.abstract:
Are you using Python's standard logging module? I am, but I found it challenging to understand.

It seems this module is **difficult not just for me, but for the entire Python community**. After exploring several popular libraries released post-ChatGPT, I noticed they all had room for improvement in their logging implementations. In production, I want to log everything to understand my application's behavior thoroughly, but many libraries have idiosyncratic logging that requires *hacks* from the library user's side... Real-world logging can be quite frustrating!

From these experiences, I've become confident in teaching Python logging essentials. To help others avoid my struggles, I'll share the fundamental pattern of Python logging. Since logging is non-functional, it often gets less attention than feature implementation. I hope this talk and materials can provide one solid pattern for combining logging module components (though you're free to choose not to adopt it after understanding it!).
results.description:
The logging module contains various elements: Logger, Handler, Formatter, and Filter. I'll demonstrate **one pattern for combining** these components:

**Logging implementation in libraries (imported code):**
- Use Logger (+ NullHandler)

**Logging implementation in applications (code that imports libraries):**
- Attach Handler to the root logger
- Attach Formatter to the Handler
- Set Filter and log levels on the Handler

### Outline (30 minutes)

**Introduction and Summary (3 minutes)**

**1. Components of the logging module (4 minutes)**
- Logger
- Handler
- Formatter
- Filter

**2. How is a logger's level determined? — NOTSET (5 minutes)**
- Log records are created for calls at or above the logger's level
- What is the level when you `getLogger()` in library code?
- Logger hierarchy
- **Changing a parent logger's level changes its children's levels too**

**3. Let's log to the root logger (8 minutes)**
- Library authors don't care about logging format or output destination
- **propagate**: Log records created by child loggers are passed to parent loggers
- Attach Handler to the root logger (the ultimate parent logger)
- Attach Formatter to that Handler
- Example with multiple Handlers: "Log INFO+ to file, ERROR+ to console too"
- Example of swapping Formatter: Output logs in JSON format

**4. Dealing with problematic logging in real-world libraries (6 minutes)**

*Message: "Please implement logging properly so we don't need these hacks"*

- `print()` is not logging (can only avoid using such libraries)
    - Differences between print and logging
    - For colored logging: Introduce Formatter replacement
- Don't touch the root logger in libraries
    - The secret of basicConfig: It touches the root logger!
    - Resulting double output due to propagate
    - How to fix it (manipulate root logger's handlers)

**5. Gleanings (4 minutes)**
- If no handler is attached to library loggers: "handler of last resort" outputs WARNING+ messages
- Configuration via files instead of code
- Introduce easier libraries like loguru
results.notes:
Japanese version in PyCon JP 2025: https://2025.pycon.jp/ja/timetable/talk/Z8ZYFA
the positive response from the Japanese community at PyCon JP 2025, I decided to share this knowledge with the international community as well.

**Background:**
This talk is based on my blog post and a previous presentation at PyCon mini Shizuoka 2024. As a machine learning engineer working with LLMs and NLP, I want to log all LLM inputs/outputs to understand everything thoroughly. Through developing LLM applications, I've gained deep insights into Python logging patterns.

results.title: From Maps to Models: An End-to-End Journey in Geospatial Machine Learning with Python
results.abstract: The convergence of Location Intelligence and Artificial Intelligence—known as GeoAI—is reshaping how we interpret the physical world. From using satellite imagery to predict crop yields to analyzing GPS traces for logistics, applying machine learning to spatial data is becoming essential for modern data scientists. This two-hour workshop offers a practical roadmap for building end-to-end Geospatial Machine Learning pipelines in Python.
results.description:
We begin by covering core geospatial concepts, moving beyond standard tables to understand coordinate reference systems, spatial resolution, and the differences between vector and raster data. Participants will learn why treating coordinates as simple X/Y columns can break models and how to preserve spatial relationships.

The workshop then focuses on data preprocessing—the most demanding stage of any GeoAI workflow. Using GeoPandas, Rasterio, and Shapely, attendees will perform spatial joins, engineer proximity features, and prepare satellite imagery for model ingestion.

Next, we address model training and validation, emphasizing the challenges of spatial autocorrelation. Instead of random splits, participants will implement spatial cross-validation techniques, such as block CV, to avoid geographic leakage.

Finally, we explore deployment strategies, including serving spatial models via APIs and monitoring concept drift caused by seasonal or urban changes. Attendees will leave with both a conceptual framework and a practical code template for taking geospatial data from raw input to production-ready ML solutions.
results.notes:
- This will require internet access.
- Data and other instructions will be provided on the actual workshop itself.

results.title: Building a Recommendation System with a Hybrid Search From Scratch.
results.abstract:
This hands-on workshop introduces participants to building a simple recommendation application based on the hybrid search technique, which combines semantic vector search and full-text search.

Using **Django**, the **MongoDB** family (including Full-Text and Vector Search, VoyageAI embeddings), and **LangChain**, we will create a web-based recommendation application that handles hybrid search, document chunking, embedding generation, and result reranking. 

By the end of this two-hour session, we will have gained a strong fundamental understanding that can be applied to the recommendation system we built. A beginner's level of Python is recommended, but no prior knowledge of AI or search systems is required.
results.description:
- **Introduction to Hybrid Search**. (10 minutes)
   **Short description**: Why hybrid search matters - overview of lexical vs. semantic retrieval, architecture we’ll build.
-  **Environment Setup**. (20 minutes)
   **Short description**: Configure MongoDB Atlas Vector Search; install LangChain, Gradio, VoyageAI; test API keys.
-  **Chunking fundamentals**. (20 minutes)
   **Short description**: Techniques for document chunking; effects on embedding quality, recall vs. context window.
-  **Embedding Generation with VoyageAI**. (20 minutes)
   **Short description**: Generate and store embeddings in MongoDB, inspect vector dimensions and index types.
- **Similarity functions of Vector Search** (20 minutes)
   **Short description**: Fundamentals of cosine, Euclidean, and dot product distance to perform vector search.
- **Building a recommendation application** (25 minutes)
   **Short description**: Build a recommendation application based on the Django framework to visualise search results using Hybrid search (Full Text Search and Vector Search).
- **Q&A** (5 minutes)
results.notes: I've been working with databases and software development for 20 years. Currently, I'm a MongoDB senior consulting engineer based in Singapore. I've previously spoken at conferences such as PyCon APAC 2025, PyCon SG 2025, PyCon Thailand 2025, and Global Azure Thailand 2025. I’m also in the community leader team for the MongoDB and PyLanna User Group in Thailand, which brings together 3,000 developers.

results.title: Expectation from Python 3.15: Faster Startup, Better Profiling, and a Smarter Standard Library
results.abstract: See what really matters in CPython 3.15: a new production-grade sampling profiler, UTF-8 as the default everywhere, and explicit lazy imports for faster startup — plus a few runtime tweaks that will quietly speed up your Python code.
results.description:
First, we’ll look at PEP 799’s new sampling profiler in the profiling module: a high-frequency, low-overhead profiler that can attach to a running process and produce flamegraphs without any code changes. You’ll see how to use it on real services, what its numbers actually mean, and how it compares to cProfile and external tools.

Next, we’ll cover PEP 686: UTF-8 as the default encoding. Python 3.15 now assumes UTF-8 for file and stdio I/O when you don’t pass encoding= — a big win for portability, but a breaking change for some Windows and legacy setups. We’ll walk through what actually changes, how to find risky code with EncodingWarning, and when to use encoding="locale" instead.

Then we’ll talk about performance-adjacent features:
- PEP 810 explicit lazy imports (expected in 3.15): new lazy import syntax to cut CLI and test startup time by 2–3× on real tools. 
- PEP 782 PyBytesWriter and bytearray.take_bytes() for faster and cleaner bytes handling in compression, parsing, and networking stacks. 
- The new math.integer module (PEP 791) and small language/stdlib tweaks that quietly improve correctness and ergonomics. 

This talk focuses only on 3.15 changes and the tail-calling interpreter from 3.14 story.

results.title: Validate very complex JSON in a one-time operation with Pydantic
results.abstract:
Pydantic is a useful library for validating data.

This talk will step-by-step explain how to perform validation on very complex JSON used in real projects using Pydantic in a one-time operation.

The existing implementation used JSON Schema for validation, but because there were branching operations in the Python code as well, it became a complex process.

First, I will share the specifications of the target JSON. Then, while demonstrating each validation step implemented with Pydantic, we'll introduce its various features. We'll also cover the process of generating Pydantic code from JSON Schema using datamodel-code-generator.

By attending this talk, you'll learn about Pydantic's capabilities and how to leverage them.
results.description:

results.title: Database Sorcery 101: Ditch the Ancient SQL Scrolls for SQLModel
results.abstract:
For many **Python beginners**, learning databases feels like cracking open an ancient spellbook: mysterious runes (SQL syntax), lengthy incantations (boilerplate code), and a constant fear of summoning errors you don’t understand. But what if working with databases felt more like casting clean modern Python magic? This is precisely what **SQLModel** provides! It lets you define your database tables, validate your data, and power your APIs with a single Python class. No need to chant raw SQL spells or copy schemas in multiple places, one class/model will do all the magic. 

In this beginner-friendly talk, we’ll banish the old scrolls of boilerplate and conjure a live demo: a **spellbook API** powered by SQLModel and FastAPI.
results.description:
# Description This talk is about **SQLModel** (and a bit of **FastAPI**) for beginners with a twist of *magic*. SQLModel is a library that simplifies SQL statements just like magic with the use of Python objects. It is written by the same author of FastAPI, *Sebastián Ramírez*, and is powered by **Pydantics + SQLAlchemy**. I believe that this library is a hidden gem that will define the future of ORM in Python. It is underutilized, not well known, but to be fair, it's a fairly new library (published in 2021). Moreover, based on official documentation, SQLModel is: - **Intuitive to write**: Great editor support. Completion everywhere. Less time debugging. Designed to be easy to use and learn. Less time reading docs. - **Easy to use**: It has sensible defaults and does a lot of work underneath to simplify the code you write. - **Compatible**: It is designed to be compatible with FastAPI, Pydantic, and SQLAlchemy. - **Extensible**: You have all the power of SQLAlchemy and Pydantic underneath. - **Short**: Minimize code duplication. A single type annotation does a lot of work. No need to duplicate models in SQLAlchemy and Pydantic. I believe that this library is something useful specifically for both **Beginners** and **Intermediate** developers:
  1. No need to learn and memorize SQL syntax for beginners; instead, you can write normal Python code, and you'll get a database.
  2. It removes a lot of boilerplate code for SQL statements. Where, instead of using statements, we use Python objects and functions instead. So instead of spending a lot of time defining your schema and writing a bunch of queries, you can head straight to coding a single class.
  3. Faster development time because of how fast you can create a working database + API with SQLModel, which can result in better motivation in writing code as well!
# Outline ## Opening: The Ancient Spellbook (5 Mins) In this section, we introduce the metaphor/idea of how **raw SQL** is basically **ancient and cryptic runes** within a magical universe. We can show an example of how a typical beginner writes raw SQL queries with **SQLite3** and how tedious it can be. ## SQLModel: The New Wand (10 Mins) In this section, we explain what **SQLModel** is, why we should use it, and why it exists. We can go further, highlighting the strengths of using a model in Python with features like **type hints and modern data validation**. This section includes a brief and beginner-friendly explanation of what **FastAPI** is, as well as what an **API** is, so that we can emphasize the importance of SQLModel in the modern world. ## Live Spellcasting: Writing Spells to the Spellbook (10 minutes) This section is a live demonstration of using **SQLModel** by creating a **mini-app** for a Spellbook Database API. The demonstration could show the use of creating and querying a database with SQLModel, and then pairing it with FastAPI to create a working **Spellbook API**. ## The Call to Adventure (3-5 minutes) Finally, summarizing my whole talk about **SQLModel** and how it's basically modern magic. Also tell them the limitations of SQLModel, especially when it comes to advanced-SQL. This is the section where I would try to encourage the audience to try SQLModel for their next projects and share some resources for their *magical training*.
results.notes:
## Target Audience
My target audience are **Beginners** as I hope that they would learn a better and easy approach towards creating databases in Python. **SQLModel** is not only for FastAPI, but generally for creating databases with Python. Furthermore, it introduces Model Oriented Programming to beginners which introduces them to learning how to handle data more cleanly with Python.

## References
- **SQLModel docs** https://sqlmodel.tiangolo.com/ & https://github.com/fastapi/sqlmodel
- **FastAPI docs** https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/

## Speaking Experience
- **Pixel Party: Walk the Dinosaur**: My first talk, very unprepared as I was a last-minute add-on (literally), where I talked about Python and Object-Oriented Programming to Grade 11-12 STEM students. It was a collaboration between an Organization (Club Simulation) and Devcon Kids.
- A seminar within our school, **National University of Dasmariñas**, where I talked about the reality of AI and debunking myths about it. The target audience were 1st years and 2nd years students.

results.title: Building Agentic AI with ADK
results.abstract: Agentic AI is transforming how we build intelligent, autonomous systems. In this session, we’ll dive into Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK), an open framework for developing robust, goal-directed agents. You’ll learn the core components of ADK, how to structure agents for real-world environments, and how to incorporate planning, memory, and tool use effectively. This can be a practical, example-rich guide to building AI agents that act, learn, and adapt.
results.description: Agent Development Kit (ADK) is a flexible and modular python framework for developing and deploying AI agents. While optimized for Gemini and the Google ecosystem, ADK is model-agnostic, deployment-agnostic, and is built for compatibility with other frameworks. ADK was designed to make agent development feel more like software development, to help developers create, deploy, and orchestrate agentic architectures.

results.title: Under the Hood: Hacking Python Data Types for Fun and Power
results.abstract:
All of us have wondered sometime, what really happens when a list is created, or compare dictionaries, or use objects as set keys in Python? This talk explores the inner workings of Python’s core data types — lists, tuples, dicts, sets, and more — from the perspective of internal implementation, memory, mutability, method resolution and more. We'll uncover how these types are implemented under the hood, and what it means to override or extend their behavior.

Along the way, we'll look at practical examples of customizing these behaviors using special methods. Let's make an immutable list or a mutable tuple, we’ll explore what’s possible, what’s dangerous, and what’s just plain fun.

Whether you're a Python enthusiast curious about how things work or a developer looking to write cleaner, more powerful abstractions, this talk will give you fresh insights into Python’s data model and how to harness it effectively.
results.description:
## Outline / Breakdown (30 minutes)

1. **Introduction (3 min)**  
   - Why look under the hood?  
   - Teaser: an indexed set and immutable list in action.  

2. **Python Data Model in Action (10 min)**  
   - How Python decides if objects can be compared or stored in sets/dicts.  
   - How small implementation tweaks affect mutability and usability.  

3. **The Hacks & Demos (14 min)**  
   - Demo 1: Making a list immutable  
   - Demo 2: Mutable tuple (and its pitfalls)  
   - Demo 3: Hashable dict  
   - Demo 4: Ordered set - Custom ordering rules for sets/dicts  
   - Demo 5–8: Additional fun/edge cases (slots, shadowing built-ins, subclass quirks, etc.)  

4. **The Good, the Bad, and the Dangerous (2 min)**  
   - When these hacks help in real-world scenarios.  
   - When they become risky or unmaintainable.  

5. **Wrap-up + Q&A (1 min)**  
   - Key lessons to remember.  
   - Closing thoughts: Python gives you superpowers — use wisely.

results.title: Kill All Mutants! (Intro to Mutation Testing)
results.abstract:
This talk presents the concept of Mutation Testing, including:
- What is mutation testing?
- Why isn't test coverage enough?
- What are its pros and cons?
- How does it work (overview *and* details)?
- Simple example (finding and fixing bad test)
- Complex example (finding and fixing bad/missing test)
- Complex example (finding and fixing redundant code)
- FAQs as time allows, such as: history, where to fit it into dev process, etc.
results.description:
Would your test suite still pass if the tested code was changed?  If so, you probably have gaps in your test suite, ineffective code, or maybe even both!

Mutation Testing reveals these cases, and provides some guidance about how to fix them.  This talk will tell you what mutation testing is, how it works inside, how to use it, where in your development process to use it, and its benefits, drawbacks, and history.  There will be multiple examples, and a list of tools for many popular languages (and some others).

You will learn a powerful technique for making sure your test suite is strict and your code is meaningful!
results.notes:
On the submission forms, I usually pick "advanced" level because it's an introduction to an advanced topic.  If forms mean the level *within the topic*, it's more introductory.

I have given this talk many times before (including at PyCon US 2023, and being voted third best talk out of over 70 at Software Quality Days 2024), but not yet in the Philippines, or anywhere else in Asia.  You can see videos in my playlist at https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLMrm16n64Bub8urB-bsyMyHiNPMLG7FAS

results.title: Python for Good: Revealing the Philippines Through Data
results.abstract:
The Philippines continues to work toward the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), but its progress becomes clearer—and more actionable—when viewed alongside other countries facing similar social, economic, and environmental challenges. Many SDG issues in the Philippines mirror broader regional struggles, yet the lack of accessible, comparable data makes it difficult for citizens and decision-makers to understand where the country is ahead, where it is falling behind, and what lessons can be learned from peers.

This 2-hour, hands-on workshop uses Python to compare the Philippines’ SDG performance with that of other nations in Southeast Asia and beyond. Participants will use open global datasets to clean, explore, and visualize cross-country indicators—revealing patterns, inequalities, and standout practices. By grounding the analysis in international comparison, the workshop helps participants understand not only how the Philippines is doing, but why some countries progress faster and what strategies may be adapted.

This workshop is not about politics — it’s about essential skills for meaningful development analysis:
✔ Data literacy for interpreting global SDG indicators
✔ Critical thinking through country-to-country benchmarking
✔ Responsible analytics that avoid misleading comparisons
✔ Transparency through reproducible and open Python workflows

By the end of the session, participants will have built a concise, reusable “SDG Comparison Notebook” that compares Philippine performance to regional and global peers. The goal is to demonstrate how Python can illuminate where the Philippines stands in the world—and how data can guide smarter, more accountable pathways toward achieving the SDGs.
results.description:
Many development challenges across Southeast Asia remain difficult to address because the underlying data is fragmented, hard to compare across countries, or rarely examined with a critical lens. Python provides a practical way to change this by making data analysis transparent, reproducible, and accessible to anyone. This workshop introduces a simple civic analysis workflow that teaches participants how to question claims, identify gaps in public information, and use open global datasets to understand how the Philippines compares to other nations on key SDG indicators.

Participants will learn essential Python tools for exploring cross-country data—from basic cleaning and visualization to fast analysis and simple geographic mapping. They will apply these skills to SDG-related issues such as education outcomes, inequality, healthcare access, mobility, environmental resilience, and governance performance. By evaluating the Philippines alongside regional and global peers, participants will uncover patterns and insights that highlight both strengths and areas for improvement. By the end of the workshop, they will have built a small, reusable “SDG comparison notebook” that demonstrates how Python can strengthen data literacy, promote transparency, and empower citizens to understand where the Philippines stands in the world and how it can move forward.
results.notes: This is designed to be a workshop due to the content, but can be turned into a shorter talk if needed.

results.title: Python in the Browser: Building Interactive Apps with Pyodide & WASM
results.abstract: Python has long powered the backend — but what if it could finally run right in your browser? In this talk, I’ll explore how WebAssembly (WASM) and Pyodide make that possible, bringing the full Python runtime to the client side. I’ll show how Python can now interact directly with the DOM, JavaScript APIs, and visualization libraries, opening up new possibilities for web-based data apps, AI demos, and notebooks that run entirely offline. Attendees will learn how Pyodide works under the hood, how to build and deploy a Python-in-the-browser project, and when this approach makes sense (and when it doesn’t). I’ll also discuss performance considerations, package limitations, and future directions for full-stack Python experiences. You’ll get the most out of this talk if you have basic experience with Python web apps or JavaScript, but the session is accessible to anyone curious about the next frontier of Python on the web.
results.description:
Python has traditionally lived on servers and notebooks — far from the browser. But thanks to WebAssembly (WASM) and Pyodide, Python is stepping into a whole new world: the front end. In this talk, I’ll dive into how Pyodide compiles the full Python runtime to WebAssembly, enabling Python code to run directly inside your browser with no server calls or transpilation.
I’ll show how Python can interact seamlessly with JavaScript, manipulate the DOM, and render rich visualizations — all client-side. Through live demos, I’ll explore how this unlocks new use cases like browser-based data tools, offline notebooks, and interactive AI visualizations. We’ll also talk about the technical tradeoffs: performance bottlenecks, packaging quirks, and how developers can balance flexibility with speed.
Whether you’re a web developer curious about Python on the front end, or a Pythonista intrigued by the browser’s potential, this session will show you what’s possible when Python meets WASM — and why it might just reshape how we think about web apps.
