results.title: An Introduction to Vector Databases
results.abstract:
You have probably heard of vector databases while learning about embeddings used in deep learning models or come across an ad on why you should use one. 
In that case, you might be wondering why not just use SQL databases or NoSQL databases. In this talk you will learn what embeddings are, and what vector databases are, why they are useful and when to use them. Expect to understand why vector databases are preferred for certain applications over SQL and NoSQL databases. We will also work through an example of how vector search works in vector databases and its applications.

Even if you have never heard of vector databases before, come learn how they are powering deep learning systems and search capabilities.
You will get the most out of this session if you have some knowledge of python and SQL databases, but no prior experience with vector embeddings is required.
results.description:
Vector databases have become more commonplace with the adoption of LLMs in various systems. You may have heard of vector databases in passing and are wondering what they are and why they are all the rage despite SQL databases having stood the test of time. You might remember learning about vectors in school and are wondering what's their relation to LLMs.
In this talk, we will learn about vectors, embeddings and vector databases. You will form an understanding of when vector databases are applicable and work through how to use open source vector databases.
This session is excellent for Python developers, data engineers and ML engineers curious about data storage and retrieval in machine learning systems. Join us to learn the basics of vector databases and how they are powering some of the world's AI systems.

results.title: HighLoad Python: SIMD, GPU, and Horizontal Scaling. Looking into Silicon.
results.abstract: Learn how Python leverages AVX-512, hyper-threading, and GPU for extreme performance. We'll dive into hardware internals, code patterns, and scaling strategies for HighLoad systems. We'll look at how we get compute modules from sand and how they execute your Python code.
results.description:
Python has evolved from a slow scripting language into a high-performance tool capable of directly interfacing with cutting-edge C/GPU technologies: AVX-512/SVE, NVLink, and HBM. In this talk, we’ll dive deep into how exactly CPython interacts with "silicon" and which hardware internals—hyper-threading, NUMA nodes, issue ports, cache lines, SIMD (gather/scatter, hyper-threading)—determine your code’s performance.

Then, onto practice: we’ll compare CPU tools (NumPy 2 SIMD, Numba @vectorize) and GPU libraries through benchmarks—from multidimensional FFTs and SPH simulations to horizontally scalable ML inference and processing of billion-record datasets.
results.notes:
Timing description:
3m: How Python reached silicon: AVX-512, SVE, NVLink, HBM — what they are and why we need direct access from CPython
5m: CPU internals: hyper-threading, NUMA nodes, issue ports, cache lines — how they affect vectorized code
4m: SIMD in detail: gather/scatter, uop-fusion, vectorization with NumPy and Numba @vectorize
5m: Anatomy of a GPU: SM clusters, warp schedulers, Tensor Cores, caches, HBM/GDDR
3m: SIMT execution model and warp groups — why GPUs think differently
3m: MIG partitioning and NVLink/NVSwitch: how to split GPUs and connect them into a network
3m: CPU benchmarks: speeding up ETL and algorithms with NumPy 2 + SIMD and Numba @vectorize
3m: GPU benchmarks (FFT, SPH): CuPy, Triton, Taichi, Numba @cuda.jit, PyOpenCL
2m: Scalable ML inference on multiple GPUs: PyTorch/JAX and what NVLink brings us
2m: Memory management: pageable vs. pinned memory, inter-GPU copies, NUMA-pinning of buffers
2m: TCO, TFLOPS/Watt — how to calculate the true cost of acceleration and avoid energy pitfalls
1m: "SIMD or GPU?" checklist: when SIMD is enough and when only GPUs will save you
3m: Horizontal scaling of CPU/GPU: architectural patterns for HighLoad
1m: Final conclusions and a ready-to-implement plan for GPU/SIMD in your production code
Total 40 m

Example of a presentation for a related topic:
https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1OxJTm-dLZSBfIvcD_gLGjwO6LNzUYoZ-_t4WkHs4L5Y/edit?slide=id.g3a558318897_0_0#slide=id.g3a558318897_0_0

results.title: Beyond Celery: Building High Throughput Event Pipelines with Kafka + Python
results.abstract:
Celery works well for background jobs, task orchestration, and retries  but when applications grow, it often struggles to handle high volume event flows, out of order processing, and real time data needs. Kafka, on the other hand, excels at streaming, partitioning, and scalable message distribution. The challenge is knowing where each tool fits and how to combine them effectively.

In this talk, I’ll walk through how we evolved a Python backend from a Celery only setup into a hybrid architecture powered by both Celery and Kafka. We’ll look at practical patterns for integrating these technologies: using Kafka partitions for throughput, Celery for workflow execution, ensuring idempotency across distributed services, and handling backpressure gracefully. I’ll also share real world lessons learned monitoring consumer lag, preventing duplicated tasks, detecting stuck workers, and deploying changes safely in production.

If you’ve ever wondered when Celery is enough, when Kafka is necessary, or how to run both in the same system without creating chaos, this session will give you a clear roadmap. You’ll walk away with concrete strategies to build reliable, scalable, high throughput event pipelines in Python without a full rewrite.
results.description:
Many Python teams start with Celery for background tasks, and it works well until the system grows. Heavy workloads, out of order events, duplicate processing, and slow queues quickly reveal Celery’s limits as an event pipeline. Kafka approaches the problem from the opposite angle: it’s built for high-throughput, ordered, scalable event streams. The real power comes from using both tools together.

In this talk, we’ll explore how to evolve a Celery-based architecture into a hybrid system where Kafka handles event distribution and throughput, while Celery continues to manage workflow style tasks. We’ll break down practical integration patterns: consuming Kafka events with Python, pushing tasks selectively to Celery, designing idempotent event handlers, managing backpressure, and building reliable contracts using Pydantic.

Using real engineering stories, we’ll walk through what worked, what broke, and how production issues like retry storms, lag buildup, and duplicate events were diagnosed and solved. You’ll walk away with a clear understanding of when Celery is the right tool, when Kafka is essential, and how to combine them to build fast, scalable, and fault-tolerant event-driven systems in Python.

results.title: Supercharging DevOps with MCP (Without Opening a Security Hole)
results.abstract: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a powerful new way to extend LLMs with real-time access to tools, APIs, and infrastructure. It enables seamless workflows like querying Grafana dashboards, triggering CI/CD jobs, or fixing issues from Sentry all without leaving your IDE. In this talk, we’ll explore how MCP works, how to build your own MCP servers, and how to compose them to automate Ops tasks and boost productivity across your stack.
results.description: As we wire LLMs into our systems, security becomes a critical concern. Unrestricted use of MCP can open the door to various vectors of attack. We’ll cover the main areas of concern as companies start adopting MCP tools and discuss how to use them safely in production environments.

results.title: Self-Healing Deployments: Python-Driven Remediation in CI/CD Platforms
results.abstract: In this session, I’ll share how Python can power intelligent, self-healing mechanisms within modern CI/CD pipelines. From failed builds and flaky tests to container rollbacks and infrastructure drift, we’ll explore how automation can detect, diagnose, and remediate issues before humans intervene. Using real-world scenarios from SRE and DevOps experience, I’ll demonstrate how Python scripts and APIs can integrate with Jenkins, Docker, and Terraform to create resilient delivery systems. Attendees will learn how to design feedback loops, monitor signals, and build adaptive pipelines that automatically recover from common failures—reducing downtime and improving developer productivity.
results.description: This talk dives deep into the concept of self-healing CI/CD—where deployments don’t just fail, they fix themselves. By leveraging Python’s flexibility and API integration capabilities, we’ll walk through practical patterns for failure detection, automated rollbacks, drift correction, and notification systems. The session connects SRE principles with DevOps tooling, showing how Python bridges the gap between observability and action. Attendees will leave with hands-on strategies to design autonomous recovery workflows that enhance reliability, consistency, and speed of delivery pipelines.

results.title: From Loops to Logic: Turtle as a Gateway to Coding
results.abstract: Python’s Turtle module offers a simple yet powerful way to visualize programming concepts. By turning code into movement, color, and pattern, Turtle helps learners of all ages understand how logic, sequencing, and loops work in real time. This session explores how Turtle can serve as a bridge between creativity and computation, making programming more accessible, engaging, and fun. Attendees will learn how to use Turtle for teaching, learning, or self-discovery, and walk away with practical ideas for introducing coding concepts through visual, hands-on exploration.
results.description:
Learning to code can feel abstract at first, but Python’s Turtle module changes that. Through simple commands and visual feedback, Turtle transforms programming into an interactive experience that encourages experimentation and problem-solving.

In this session, we’ll explore creative ways to use Turtle to teach or learn coding fundamentals, from drawing shapes and patterns to understanding loops, logic, and algorithmic thinking. Participants will gain insights into how visual programming can boost engagement and comprehension for both beginners and experienced coders looking for new ways to teach or explore Python.

results.title: Circuit Tracing in Transformers: Peeking Inside the Black Box
results.abstract:
Understanding how large language models create outputs is essential for safe and secure AI use, otherwise we will have uncontrollted machines and skynet like situation. Old external techniques only show what a transformer outputs, not why (It cannot be REASONED with if we dont know the reason). 

Anthropic’s Circuit Tracer introduces a supersmart approach by mapping internal activations into human-interpretable features and tracing how they influence each step of the model’s reasoning.

Rest of the abstract is AI generated (some of it- I have addded some stuff about matrix) :)) - I am too lazy to write it all  - but I have read it - it is correct. 

## **Why Explainability Matters**

We need to understand **why** AI models make certain choices, not just what answers they give. Without this, the model feels like a black box.

---

## **What Transformers Hide**

Transformers have many internal steps and features that are hard to see. Normal tools only show the final output, not the thinking process. Infact - it comes as a surpirse to normal people that we dont know how models "actually" arrive at specific answers. 

---

## **How Circuit Tracer Helps**

Anthropic’s Circuit Tracer shows the **inside connections** of the model.
It turns hidden activations into **easy-to-understand features** and shows how they link together. It not that easy, but you can get used to the graphs (like the link guy in the matrix movie- he could just understand by looking at the matrix runtime code)

---

## **Seeing the Reasoning Path**

The tool draws a clear path from:
**input → inner features → final output**
This lets us see which parts of the model caused the answer.

---

## **Why This Is Important**

With this method, we can:

* check if the model is behaving safely
* fix mistakes inside the model
* build trust by seeing how it thinks

Circuit Tracer is currently the **strongest practical tool** for truly looking inside how a transformer works.
results.description:
In this talk, I want to explain how we can use the circuit tracing apprach for better explainability and interpretability in an inference. . 

I will talk about what circuit tracing is in large language models like Gemma. It's a new way to understand how models answer questions by looking inside them and checking which neurons activate when.

Anthropic's open sourced a python module - circuit-tracer, and also the Neuronpedia portal , helps us find neurons linked to real-world concepts. We can examine specific prompts on transformers and understand the various paths and thoughts that make use reach the output. (It is veery interesting - for me) 

I'll show how this works with a live demo from their Jupyter notebooks from atribution and intervention graphs. Also what nodes and supernodes are, and how they connect to form reasoning paths in the model. This can help us debug, understand.
results.notes:
I have been speaker in other pycons and other AI conferences. As 2025 ends I aspire to build transparent AI. For this speech I have created the slides much earlier, but I will use fun additions from Darius speech etc. I will ask audience for prompts that Ill create the graphs for on neuronpedia and show them how the model goes into crazy thoughts before it creates the output - it will be really ffun and learnign experience

I will create a youtube video of this topic before 2025 ends - so that you can evaluate the speech better :) ...

results.title: Breaking Free from Virtual Environments: Python's New Paradigm for 2026
results.abstract:
For years, Python developers have been taught that creating and managing virtual environments is fundamental to Python development. Commands like `python -m venv .venv` and `source .venv/bin/activate` have been ingrained in every tutorial and workflow. But what if I told you that in 2026, developers no longer need to manually create virtual environments?

This talk explores the paradigm shift happening in Python's ecosystem, where tools like `uv`, `pipx`, and inline script metadata (PEP 723) are liberating developers from manual virtual environment management. I'll show how we evolved from the pain points of manual `venv` management through solutions like Poetry and pip-tools, culminating in today's automated approaches with `uvx` and `pipx run` for commands, and inline script metadata for scripts.

Through code comparisons demonstrating the old way versus the new way, you'll see how Python development has become dramatically simpler. Whether you're running a linter like Ruff, executing a one-off script, or starting a new project, you'll learn practical techniques to let tools handle virtual environments for you. It's time to break free and embrace a more productive Python development experience.

**Target Audience:** Intermediate Python developers familiar with virtual environments who want to modernize their workflow.
results.description:
### Talk Structure (30 minutes)

**1. Introduction (3 minutes)**
- Brief self-introduction
- The central question: "What did developers used to create all the time, but rarely create anymore?"
- Preview of the paradigm shift

**2. Virtual Environment Fundamentals (3 minutes)**
- Quick refresher: What is a virtual environment and why does Python need them?
- Understanding the `.venv` directory structure
- The traditional workflow: create, activate, install, use

**3. The Pain Points of Manual Management (4 minutes)**
- Challenge 1: Uninstalling specific packages is painful
- Challenge 2: `pip freeze` output order issues
- Challenge 3: Proliferation of virtual environments for scripts
- Challenge 4: Keeping tools updated across projects
- Why these problems matter for productivity

**4. Evolution of Solutions (5 minutes)**
- How the community responded to these pain points
- Poetry's approach: project-level virtual environment management
- pip-tools: requirements.in → requirements.txt workflow
- The emergence of Rye and its influence on uv
- Setting the stage for the current generation of tools

**5. Modern Solution 1: Command Execution Without Installation (5 minutes)**
- Introducing `uvx` and `pipx run`
- Code comparison: Old way vs. new way
  - Before: Install Ruff in project venv, activate, run
  - After: `uvx ruff format` or `pipx run ruff format`
- How it works: temporary virtual environments under the hood
- Benefit: Always using the latest version without manual updates
- Real-world use cases: linters, formatters, cookiecutter, Sphinx, build tools

**6. Modern Solution 2: Inline Script Metadata (5 minutes)**
- The problem: Scripts need dependencies, leading to throwaway venvs
- Introducing PEP 723: inline script metadata
- Code comparison: Old way vs. new way
  - Before: Create venv, install httpx & rich, run script
  - After: Add metadata comment block, run with `uv run` or `hatch run`
- Tools supporting inline script metadata: uv, pipx, Hatch, PDM
- Making scripts portable and easy to run across environments

**7. Live Code Comparisons (3 minutes)**
- Side-by-side demonstration slides showing:
  - Running a formatter: manual venv vs `uvx`
  - Executing a script: manual venv vs inline metadata
- Highlighting the reduction in cognitive load and commands

**8. Choosing Your Tools & Wrap-up (2 minutes)**
- `uv` as the all-in-one solution (but not the only option)
- Alternative perspectives: Hatch's multiple environments, Poetry's maturity
- Call to action: Try one command (`uvx` or `pipx run`) this week
- The future: Developers freed from manual virtual environment management

### Why This Talk Matters

While much of the Python community discussion around `uv` focuses on its speed ("uv is fast!"), this talk emphasizes a more fundamental shift: **developers no longer need to manually manage virtual environments**. This paradigm shift has implications for:

- How we teach Python to newcomers (virtual environments may not need to be lesson #1 anymore)
- How we structure our daily development workflows
- How we share scripts and tools with colleagues
- How we reduce friction in Python development

By understanding both the historical context and modern solutions, attendees will appreciate not just the "what" and "how" of these tools, but the "why" behind this evolution.

### What Makes This Talk Unique

- **Balanced perspective**: While demonstrating uv's capabilities, I acknowledge it's part of a broader ecosystem evolution (Poetry, Hatch, pipx all contributed to this paradigm)
- **Historical context**: Understanding how we got here helps developers make informed choices about tools
- **Practical focus**: Clear code comparisons show immediate actionable improvements
- **Community impact**: Addressing how this changes our approach to teaching and onboarding
results.notes:
I've been using Python for 8 years and have experienced firsthand the evolution of virtual environment management. I've written a Japanese booklet on this topic and given talks at PyCon JP. I maintain the awesome-pipx-uvx collection documenting use cases for these tools. While I personally prefer Hatch, I'm committed to presenting a balanced view of the ecosystem to help developers find the right tools for their needs.

All code examples and slides will be made available after the talk. Sample scripts with inline script metadata will be provided for attendees to try immediately.

results.title: Offline First GenAI at Scale with a Delay Tolerant Python Architecture
results.abstract: This session blends system design with hands on engineering so that attendees see both the why and the how. It presents an offline first GenAI platform in Python that keeps working during outages, reconciles state when links recover, and ships small model updates instead of heavy weights. The talk alternates between architectural diagrams and code segments that implement the core building blocks. By the end, the audience understands the design choices and also leaves with a clear path to implement them in their own stacks.
results.description:
The session opens with the shape of the problem and the target user journey. We describe a request that can be served on edge machines or cloud workers without blocking the user, and we outline the rules that keep the system responsive and consistent. We then drop into our first coding segment where we build a minimal content addressed store in Python that assigns a stable digest to every artifact such as prompts, adapters, and vector shards. The goal is to show how a simple library call and a small file layout enable global deduplication and instant cache hits.

We return to the architecture to show how nodes discover one another and choose where to run work. Next we code a lightweight router that checks current load and proximity and then selects the best worker for each request. The example demonstrates back pressure with asyncio queues and graceful fallbacks when accelerators are unavailable. We examine how the router records decisions so that a later replay can reproduce the same path.

The third segment shifts to collaboration and correctness. We describe a mailbox model that allows teams to edit prompts and templates while offline and still converge without conflict. We implement a small conflict free data type in Python that merges edits deterministically and stores the merged result by content address. We then compare two executions of the same scenario and show how a manifest that captures code version, prompt variant, adapter hash, and input digest allows an engineer to answer what changed with confidence.

We close the build by wiring traces and run metadata end to end. The final coding slice adds structured logging and distributed tracing that travels with each request across nodes. We replay an earlier run and produce a human readable diff that highlights the exact edge that changed behavior. Throughout, we keep the code compact and idiomatic and we tie every snippet back to the system diagram so that the reasons behind each choice are clear. The audience comes away with three concrete modules they can lift into a real service and a blueprint for evolving them in production.
results.notes: Format is a balanced mix of architecture and code with roughly equal time for each. Target audience is senior Python engineers and platform owners who operate GenAI or data heavy services.

results.title: AI Coding Agents and how to code them
results.abstract: AI Agents are the next big thing everyone has been talking about. They are expected to revolutionize various industries by automating routine tasks, mission critical business workflows, enhancing productivity, and enabling humans to focus on creative and strategic work. Of course, you can apply them to your everyday coding tasks as well.
results.description: In this talk we’ll go over what those agents can bring to the table of coding world, and why they can deliver the promise of coding smarter that the current generation of coding assistants can’t. We will then dive right into a quick live coding session where I’ll show what such agents can do in real life and how you can start using them to enhance your everyday life already right after the talk. And we’ll finish off with some remarks on what the future of programming might look like in the near future as those agents get included into your everyday life.

results.title: Aysncio without Asyncio: Reconstructing Asyncio from First Principles
results.abstract:
Ever wondered what really happens when you write async and await? In this talk, we’ll peel back the layers of Python’s asyncio and rebuild it from scratch, no magic, no dependencies, just the Python standard library. Step by step, we’ll move from blocking I/O to a working event loop and coroutine scheduler that mirrors asyncio’s core design.

Along the way, you’ll discover how Python turns generators into coroutines, how the event loop orchestrates concurrent tasks, and why cooperative multitasking is both elegant and tricky. By the end, we’ll put it all to the test, running a fully functional asynchronous HTTP proxy powered entirely by our handcrafted runtime.

This talk is for Python developers comfortable with functions and iterators, curious about how async works beneath the surface. You don’t need prior asyncio experience, just a willingness to explore how Python’s “black box” really ticks.
results.description:
What really happens when you write async and await in Python? Beneath the clean syntax lies a beautifully simple, but often misunderstood machinery built on generators, cooperative multitasking, and a tiny event loop orchestrating everything. In this talk, we’ll dismantle asyncio down to its bones and rebuild it step-by-step, using only the Python standard library.

We’ll start with the pain of blocking I/O and why traditional solutions like threads and processes introduce unnecessary overhead. Then we’ll peel back the curtain on Python generators the unsung heroes that make coroutines possible and use them to craft a tiny manual scheduler. From there, we’ll engineer a minimal event loop using selectors, add tasks and futures for lifecycle management, and demystify what actually happens under the hood when Python compiles async def and await.

The journey culminates in a fully functional asynchronous HTTP proxy built entirely from scratch, a hands-on proof that you don’t need asyncio to understand or implement asyncio.

This talk is designed for Python developers who know how to write functions and iterators but want to finally understand what async is really doing. You’ll walk away knowing exactly how Python achieves concurrency without threads, how event loops schedule work, and how modern async frameworks build on these foundations. Perfect for anyone who has used await… but never quite trusted it.

results.title: Zstandard in Python 3.14 Faster Compression You Can Use Today
results.abstract: Zstandard is now first class in Python via the new compression.zstd module, bringing fast, high ratio compression to everyday workflows. This talk introduces Zstandard for Python developers, explains how it differs from gzip, bzip2, and LZMA, and shows clear benchmarks of compression ratio and speed. You will learn one‑shot, streaming, and incremental APIs, plus dictionary training for many small similar payloads. We will cover real scenarios such as log pipelines, HTTP payloads, data lakes, and packaging, including how integration with zipfile and tarfile improves usability. What is new is the unified compression namespace in Python 3.14 with an official Zstandard wrapper that simplifies API discovery and cross‑version compatibility. Compared to traditional choices, you can achieve smaller downloads and dramatically faster extraction while keeping CPU and memory costs reasonable. Attendees leave with practical patterns, compatibility tips, and reproducible tests to pick the right algorithm for their workloads.
results.description:
Introduction and goals (3 min)
• Explains the session’s objectives: empowering attendees to use Python 3.14’s compression.zstd for faster, smaller data handling in real-world scenarios.

Comparing gzip, bzip2, and LZMA trade-offs (7 min)
• Outlines strengths and weaknesses of gzip, bz2, lzma and zlib in terms of speed, compression ratio, and resource usage.
• Shows benchmark results and discusses how these legacy choices impact log pipelines, HTTP payloads, and data lakes.

Zstandard and the new compression namespace (8 min)
• Introduces the compression.zstd module and unified compression namespace in Python 3.14, explaining improvements in API consistency and discoverability.
• Details Zstandard’s architecture, focusing on its performance, dictionary support, and integration with zipfile and tarfile.

Demos: streaming, dictionaries, benchmarks (8 min)
• Demonstrates compress and decompress APIs, ZstdFile for streaming, and incremental usage with ZstdCompressor and ZstdDecompressor.
• Shows how to train dictionaries for many small, similar payloads and presents real benchmarks comparing speed, ratio, and resource consumption.

Q&A and wrap-up (4 min)
• Addresses migration tips, cross-version compatibility, cost considerations, and practical scenarios for adopting compression.zstd.
• Provides resources, reproducible test patterns, and guidance for further exploration and immediate adoption.
results.notes: I will begin by explaining the basics of compression, so even those who aren’t familiar with it can understand. Through demonstrations, I will clearly show how compression works and how it can be applied in Python.

results.title: Book Buddy: Interactive Storytelling using a Gemma-based RAG in Python
results.abstract:
Explore how to bring interactive storytelling to life using Python and a Gemma-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflow. In this session, attendees will learn how to preprocess text, create embeddings with `sentence-transformers`, store and retrieve vectors in Chroma, and integrate results with the open-source Gemma model to generate context-aware responses.  

A live demo will show multi-turn queries and counterfactual exploration while preserving the original story. By the end, participants will gain hands-on experience with RAG workflows and practical Python patterns for building engaging, interactive applications.
results.description:
Interactive storytelling is an exciting frontier in AI, allowing readers to explore narratives dynamically rather than passively consuming content. In this session, we’ll explore Book Buddy, a Python-based system that leverages a Gemma-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflow to answer questions, summarize content, and enable counterfactual exploration of stories.  

Attendees will learn how to:  
- Preprocess and split large text documents for embedding.  
- Create embeddings using Python libraries like `sentence-transformers` and store them in a vector database (Chroma) for efficient retrieval.  
- Integrate retrieval with the open-source Gemma model to generate context-aware, real-time responses.  
- Build a Python pipeline to handle multi-turn queries while preserving the original storyline.  

We’ll also cover practical considerations for real-world usage, including performance optimization, caching, modular code design, and deployment options. A live demo will show how Book Buddy can answer reader questions, provide summaries, and explore “what-if” scenarios, all without changing the original story.  

This talk is ideal for Python developers, ML engineers, and open-source contributors interested in hands-on experience with RAG workflows. By the end, participants will have a working prototype, concrete Python code patterns, and a clear understanding of how to combine embeddings, retrieval, and LLM generation to create engaging, interactive applications.

results.title: Parenting with Python
results.abstract: As a parent, you wanted to share your appreciation and love for the Python programming language with your children but does not know how to start or how to introduce Python to your kids. This session will feature some parenting tips on how to start ushering your kids early into their Python journey and give themselves a head start in learning one of the most versatile programming languages on the planet.
results.description:
You are a Pythonista parent and you wanted to introduce your kid to the Python programming language, not just to provide your kids the relevant skills that will help them appreciate technology more, but at the same time have some time to bond together through Python.

How will you start? What will you do? What are the challenges, and how will you overcome those challenges while still be able to teach your kids Python programming? This session will feature some parenting tips on how to start ushering your kids early into their Python journey and give themselves a head start in learning one of the most versatile programming languages on the planet.

results.title: The <ART> of designing LLM </SYSTEM>
results.abstract:
The `science` of LLM is everywhere in the media, white papers, and every second billboard.\
But the `art` of designing them? Almost **unheard**!

We’ll explore how to think systems which could be trusted, **design** with intention, and yes, draw with LEGO blocks 🧱
results.description:
When LLMs first appeared, the excitement was all about capability: what they could do?
But as the systems grew, so did the gaps: `trust`, `transparency`, and `reproducibility`.

This session rethinks that story from the inception of LLMs to their evolving ecosystem, through a design-thinking lens. We’ll unpack how to build an LLM system that is not only intelligent but intentional: secure by design, explainable by default, and reproducible in practice.

Drawing inspiration from [DataJourney](https://github.com/DataJourneyHQ/DataJourney), the design-first open-source data management framework, we’ll connect creativity with structure. Think of it as designing intelligence with empathy and rigour, one LEGO block at a time.

results.title: We build our own conference website : pycon.id
results.abstract: Since covid era, tech conference is force to change into online version. Turns out being online makes the conference more accessible a lot of people. After we change back to in person conference, those who live far from the city still want to attend. But not all of us can afford an expensive conference platform to make it available online. So we try to build it ourselves. Powered by python, we bring an opensource conference platform to everyone.
results.description:
This talk will cover 3 main topics.

1. The story of pycon.id : We will look to how pycon.id adapt online conference platform into building it's own.
2. How we build the website : Here we will talk about the technical side, which technology we use, how it works, and other thing that we put in our website.
3. How to contribute : Now we will talk about the future of our website, how you can contribute to it, or even you can fork it and add other feature to match with your need of conference.

We hope that with more people use the website we also gonna be able to make it better for everyone.
results.notes: Since the event is near the time of Eid Al-fitr in Indonesia. I only able to attend from the 2nd day.

results.title: Are we free-threaded ready? Looking at where free-threaded Python fails
results.abstract: Free-threaded Python aims to significantly improve performance, allowing multiple native threads to execute Python bytecode concurrently. In this talk, we will explore the current state of Python's free-threading initiative and assess its practical readiness for widespread adoption.
results.description:
We begin by exploring the background of free-threaded Python, summarising its origins, current status, and the technical differences distinguishing it from standard Python implementations. A key focus will be examining the compatibility landscape, specifically investigating how many popular third-party libraries are currently prepared for free-threading. We will distinguish between generic pure Python wheels and explicitly free-threaded wheels and I’ll explain how the community can contribute to compatibility verification. 

We then critically discuss free-threaded Python's necessity, weighing the disadvantage of increased thread safety concerns (and verification methods) against the promised advantage of speed (including multithreaded profiling).Will free-threaded Python become a critical future direction for the language? How can you contribute? If and how specific projects can immediately benefit from it? Let’s find out together!

Outline:

- Background about free-threaded Python
    - Where it started and where are we now
    - What are the differences between standard and free-threaded Python
- Looking at how many popular libraries are free-threaded ready
    - free-threaded wheels vs generic pure Python wheel
    - Testing the library built for free-threading compatibility and how you can help
- Do we really need free-threaded Python?
    - Disadvantage: thread safety - how to verify thread safety of a library
    - Advantage: speed up - how to perform multithreaded profiling
- Conclusion
    - Free-threaded Python is the future, and how you can contribute
    - Check if you can take advantage of it and equip yourself
    - Join the discourse and voice out your thoughts

results.title: How Switching to orjson Transformed My FastAPI Performance
results.abstract: My FastAPI application was being discreetly throttled by JSON handling.  The majority of the slowness was caused by serialization and parsing rather than the database, business logic, or network.  I used orjson in place of the default JSON layer after profiling and locating the bottleneck.  Significantly quicker read times, quicker replies, less CPU load, and more seamless performance under high concurrency are the outcomes.  The real before/after numbers, the profiling process, the unforeseen difficulties, and the quantifiable, production-grade impact of a drop-in Rust-powered serializer are all covered in this session.
results.description:
### Description

In actual FastAPI implementations, **JSON encoding and decoding frequently turn into an unnoticed performance bottleneck**. I discovered this through experience while troubleshooting slow endpoints in a production service that had already been optimized in both the database and logic layers. **The issue: Python’s built-in JSON library was spending excessive time serializing large responses.**

This presentation is a hands-on case study of how I enhanced my FastAPI application's responsiveness by substituting the default JSON implementation with **orjson**. It emphasizes the **effect**, rather than the API structure.

### Topic

- **The specific symptoms:** irregular latency, elevated CPU usage, slow large responses.
- **Profiling outcomes:** identifying where the JSON expense occurs and its actual size.
- **What was different after transitioning to orjson?**
- **Pre/post benchmarks** for actual endpoints
- **Enhancements** in read speed, reaction time, and output capacity.
- **Adverse effects:** memory, datetime manipulation oddities, and compatibility problems.
- **How this minor adjustment led to a significant improvement in performance.**

### Takeaway

By the conclusion, participants will understand **the extent of performance they are sacrificing with standard serialization** and how this one choice greatly enhanced the **scalability and stability** of my service.

results.title: Building Inclusive User Experiences with Python
results.abstract: In today’s diverse world, building inclusive user experiences is not just a nice-to-have but a necessity. This talk will explore how to create AI-powered, accessible applications that cater to all users, including those with disabilities. Attendees will learn how Python can be used to develop accessible user interfaces (UI) with features like voice control, gesture recognition, and assistive technologies that improve digital accessibility. This session will dive into practical Python libraries, tools, and techniques for ensuring that AI systems are not only functional but also usable and inclusive for everyone.
results.description:
As technology becomes increasingly integrated into daily life, creating applications that are inclusive and accessible for all users is paramount. Accessibility is about designing and building systems that everyone, regardless of ability, can use. This talk focuses on how Python can be a powerful tool for building inclusive AI systems that enable seamless interaction for users with disabilities.

Key topics will include:
 - Accessible design principles - Best practices for making AI-driven systems more inclusive, focusing on accessible user interfaces (UIs) for users with visual, auditory, and cognitive impairments.
- Voice and gesture-based control - Using Python libraries like SpeechRecognition, pyttsx3, and PyAutoGUI to build systems that can be controlled via voice or gestures, ensuring a hands-free experience.
- Assistive technologies - Integrating common assistive tools like screen readers with Python applications to help visually impaired users interact with the system.
- Python frameworks for accessibility - Leveraging tools like Tkinter for building accessible UIs that comply with the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG).

results.title: Building a Thriving Tech Ecosystem: The Role of PyLadies in Fostering Growth and Inclusion
results.abstract:
The global tech ecosystem continues to grow, yet challenges like limited mentorship, a lack of role models, and fragmented community support hinder progress, especially for underrepresented groups. PyLadies offers a powerful model for bridging these gaps. This talk explores how PyLadies chapters worldwide foster technical growth, increase mentorship opportunities, and drive collaboration to create a more inclusive and sustainable global tech community.
We can contribute to the growth of the global tech ecosystem by leveraging the PyLadies model of an inclusive and sustainable community.
results.description:
The strength of any tech ecosystem lies in its ability to grow, support, and sustain its members. However, many aspiring technologists from underrepresented backgrounds struggle to find mentorship, role models, and opportunities to thrive. PyLadies, a global network dedicated to increasing women's participation in the Python community, presents a proven model for addressing these challenges.
This talk will explore the impact of community-driven initiatives on the tech ecosystem, using PyLadies as a case study. We'll break down the discussion into three key sections:
Understanding the Challenges:
The barriers to entry and growth in tech for underrepresented groups
The role of mentorship and community in shaping successful careers


The PyLadies Model:
How PyLadies chapters empower individuals through mentorship, technical training, and networking
Success stories from PyLadies communities around the world


Scaling the Impact:
How local communities and organizations can adopt similar models to foster technical growth
Actionable steps for individuals and companies to contribute to a more inclusive and sustainable tech ecosystem
Through real-world examples, practical strategies, and interactive discussions, this talk aims to inspire attendees to take action, whether by starting or supporting PyLadies chapters, mentoring newcomers, or advocating for stronger community engagement.
By the end of the session, participants will leave with a clear understanding of how they can contribute to strengthening the global tech ecosystem and why investing in community-driven initiatives like PyLadies is key to sustainable growth.

OUTLINE
1. Introduction (2 mins)
 *Introduction
 *What inspired this talk (the importance of support systems in tech)

Overview of what to expect
2. Understanding the Challenges (8 mins)
  A. Barriers for Underrepresented Groups
    * Lack of access to mentorship, role models, and opportunities
    * Imposter syndrome and systemic bias
    * Financial, geographical, and cultural constraints
  B. The Role of Community and Mentorship
    * Why peer support matters
    * How mentorship can transform career journeys
    * Audience interaction: "Who here has been positively impacted by a tech community?"

3. The PyLadies Model (8 mins)
  A. What is PyLadies?
    * Mission, history, and global reach
    * Core activities: workshops, meetups, mentorship, speaker support
  B. Empowerment Through Community
    * Technical training and exposure
    * Networking and visibility for women in Python
    * Community-led leadership development
  C. Success Stories
    * Highlight stories from PyLadies chapters around the world
    * Testimonials

4. Scaling the Impact (8 mins)
  A. Adopting the Model Locally
    * How can any community replicate PyLadies' success
    * Partnering with schools, companies, and global organizations
  B. Individual & Organizational Action Steps
    * Starting or supporting a PyLadies chapter
    * Mentoring newcomers or junior developers
    * Advocating for inclusive hiring and speaker lineups
    * Supporting infrastructure: funding, venues, visibility

5. Call to Action (4 mins)
    *Recap of key takeaways
    *Share a personal reflection on the power of community
    * Call to action:
        i) Join/support PyLadies
        ii) Become a mentor
        iii) Create inclusive spaces in your local tech communities

6. Q&A

results.title: Accelerated BDD Testing with Python, Playwright, Behave & MCP (Using Cursor)
results.abstract:
In this workshop, participants will learn how to build modern, maintainable UI tests using Behavior-Driven Development (BDD), Python, Behave, and Playwright, while leveraging AI assistance through Cursor and the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Attendees will learn the fundamentals of Gherkin, write multiple BDD scenarios, implement them in Python, automate real browsers with Playwright, and harness powerful AI tools that understand and execute Playwright actions through MCP. By the end of the workshop, participants will have built a small but complete test suite, ready for local or CI environments.

This workshop is ideal for QA engineers, software developers, SDETs, and anyone interested in modern automation or AI-assisted tooling.
results.description:
Outline of topics

- BDD concepts and Gherkin syntax
- Behave as a BDD testing framework
- Playwright fundamentals
- MCP (Model Context Protocol) and Playwright MCP server
- AI-assisted development using Cursor
- Project setup and environment configuration
- Writing Gherkin feature files
- Implementing step definitions in Python
- Automating browsers with Playwright
- Valid & invalid login scenarios
- Behave tags and test organization
- Deep dive into Playwright capabilities (selectors, tracing, screenshots)

results.title: Weaponizing Python for Good: Building a Next-Gen CVE Scanner That Detects Zero-Day Vulnerability
results.abstract:
In an age where vulnerabilities are discovered daily and exploited faster than patches can be released, Python stands as the ultimate ally for defenders. This session takes you behind the scenes of building Toolshell, a next-generation SharePoint CVE scanner powered by Python that automates detection, analysis, and reporting of real-world vulnerabilities like CVE-2025-53770.

Attendees will learn how to:
1. Engineer an AI-assisted vulnerability scanner using modern Python libraries.
Build a config-driven detection engine with regex pattern matching, adaptive scoring, and SSL certificate analysis.
2. Transform raw scan results into beautiful HTML reports and structured datasets for security dashboards.
Apply defensive Python automation techniques to identify exposure safely before attackers do.

This talk blends cybersecurity and software craftsmanship, showing how Python empowers defenders to automate vulnerability discovery, accelerate incident response, and make threat detection transparent and reproducible. Whether you're a security analyst, developer, or DevSecOps enthusiast, you’ll walk away inspired to turn your scripts into battle-ready python security tool that protect real infrastructure.
results.description:
In a world where vulnerabilities emerge daily and attackers move faster than defenders can patch, Python has become one of the most powerful tools for proactive cybersecurity.
This talk unveils Toolshell, a next-generation SharePoint CVE scanner built entirely in Python, designed to simulate real-world attack detection and automate defensive scanning for vulnerabilities like CVE-2025-53770. Through the journey of building this tool, we’ll explore how Python’s modularity, concurrency, and simplicity can transform a traditional script into a cyber-defense framework capable of intelligent, large-scale scanning and reporting.

You’ll learn how to:
Architect a config-driven vulnerability scanner using Python’s standard library and dataclasses.
Implement adaptive detection logic with pre-compiled regex patterns, dynamic scoring, and error resilience.
Apply asynchronous and multithreaded scanning for performance at scale.
Build secure retry mehanisms with session persistence, timeouts, and SSL/TLS validation.
Generate rich output reports (HTML, CSV, JSON) that visualize vulnerability intelligence for analysts.
Safely simulate exploit detection — without performing real-world attacks.
Beyond the code, this session demonstrates how Python empowers cybersecurity professionals to detect weaknesses before adversaries exploit them. It’s a blend of ethical hacking, automation, and defensive engineering, proving that the most impactful security tools don’t come from billion-dollar companies, but from inspired developers who use Python creatively and responsibly.
By the end of this talk, participants will walk away with a blueprint to build their own intelligent vulnerability scanners, a deeper understanding of Python’s power in real-world security, and the inspiration to use code not just to automate tasks but to protect systems and people.

results.title: IaC Meets Python : When Terraform isn't Enough
results.abstract: In this talk, I’ll explore what happens when traditional Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform hit their limits. While Terraform is excellent for declarative provisioning, real-world automation often demands more flexibility — dynamic logic, conditional flows, API integrations, or handling complex dependencies. This is where Python bridges the gap. I’ll walk through practical examples of extending IaC with Python, from custom resource orchestration to automating post-deployment tasks and integrating with CI/CD pipelines. Attendees will learn when to go beyond Terraform, how to use Python to complement IaC, and best practices for maintaining reliability and readability in hybrid automation setups.
results.description: In this topic, I’ll explore how Python can complement Terraform when traditional Infrastructure as Code tools reach their limits. From dynamic resource creation to complex dependency handling and API-driven provisioning, we’ll dive into real-world scenarios where Python brings flexibility and control to IaC workflows. Attendees will learn how to integrate Python scripts with Terraform, automate edge cases, and build smarter, event-driven infrastructure pipelines that go beyond declarative definitions.

results.title: The Agentic twist
results.abstract:
**Agents.** Everyone seems to be talking about them now. Agents are to solve a wide range of business-use cases and should disrupt the market like no other. But do we get what is promised? What is the real business value of Agents? Join us as we cut the sales-pitch, make agents concrete and learn together what is behind the hype.
During this session, we will together look into the mechanics of agents, building multi-agent systems, the relation between Tools, MCP and A2A, the open source Agentic offering and designing an actual Agentic system for greater good.
results.description:
Let's learn more about Agents together! In this talk, we will learn what is behind the hype. There is a lot of attention going towards agents so it might be hard to grasp what in the end is valuable and can actually help your business forward. We will look into why Agentic systems can be powerful - and why you should think of applying Agentic patterns. We will specifically look into how to build Agentic systems, the open source platform Agentic offering and designing Agentic systems for greater good.

### Contents of the talk 📌
1. [3 min] Intro
    - Who I am
    - Agentic: what’s that twist? Let’s find out.
2. [4 min] Why agentic: what's the deal?
- Hype: justified or unjustified? 
- The possibilities that exist
3. [10 min] Building a multi-agent system
- The Agentic core: Tools, reasoning and loops
- Going multi-agent: sub-agents, loop-agents, sequential agents 
- The landscape of Agentic libraries and the Agent Development Kit
- MCP: where and how it comes in
- Summing up: a meaningful multi-agent application
4. [6 min] Taking the leap: towards deploying agents
- Evaluation and its necessity 
- Deployment patterns
- Hosting an A2A agent
5. [4 min] Summing things up
- When to consider building Agentic
- Designing Agentic systems for greater good
- AI community 👥
6. [3 min] End
    - The Agentic twist ✓
    - Thank you ♡

[30 minutes total]

### 🏡 What you will take home
At the end of the talk, you will be taking home an understanding of:
- The rationale behind Agents and its core concepts like Tools, reasoning and loops
- The status-quo of Agents in the rapidly environment of tech
- Concrete steps on building multi-agent systems
- MCP and A2A and their angle with respect to Agents
- The most important considerations to make when deploying Agents
- Understanding of how Agents can be applied; how to think about Agent-usecases; how to use Agents for good
- What communities are around to support you

### ❤️ Open Source Software
Agents are presented in a cloud-agnostic way. Software libraries mentioned are **open source**. There is no agenda for representing any major cloud or LLM provider.

### 🎒 Pre-requisites
The talk can be followed without GenAI/Agents familiarity ✓. Recommended, though, is interest in GenAI/Agents ♡.

results.title: Architecting Belonging with Graph Theory
results.abstract:
Community health is often measured by simple volume, such as total messages and active members. However, high-volume communities often suffer from "structural fracture," where loud cliques mask islands of isolated newcomers. For student leaders, manually tracking these trends is impossible, and trying to monitor a chat leads to burnout. This introduces a privacy-first, air-gapped analytics tool designed to perform periodic health audits on community data.
Unlike traditional sentiment analysis tools, this solution explicitly does not use Natural Language Processing (NLP). It relies strictly on interaction metadata, such as who spoke to whom and when, to map the social topology without ever reading message content. By utilizing NetworkX, it processes historical logs to generate a high-level "Presidential Briefing." It implements algorithmic filters to distinguish between "Noise Makers" (spammers) and "True Isolates" (ignored contributors) and uses centrality heuristics to scout for potential future leaders. This solution allows administrators to move from the impossible goal of constant oversight to a sustainable, data-driven routine of Community Engineering.
results.description:
We often assume that if a community chat is active, the community is healthy. But data often reveals a different reality: communities are frequently "archipelagos" of loud, active cliques separated by islands of lonely, disconnected newcomers.

In this talk, I will be sharing how I engineered a solution called CircleCare which is a privacy-first analytics tool designed to audit community health without acting as a surveillance bot. Unlike traditional tools that read message content, this solution explicitly does not use Natural Language Processing (NLP). Instead, it processes local Facebook Messenger exports using NetworkX to map social topology based purely on interaction metadata.

This talk is designed for community builders, student leaders, Python developers, and educators who recognize that manual oversight does not scale. It is for those who want to move beyond gut feeling and reinforce their leadership decisions with quantifiable metrics. Whether you manage a growing student organization, a remote team, or a digital classroom, this talk will provide the architectural blueprint to replace reactive scrolling with proactive engineering, allowing you to debug your social graph and scale empathy sustainably.
results.notes:
This project of mine has been in development for about a year now. It started out as a "what-if" solution because I don't have enough social battery to be chronically online. On my pilot testing on my previous term with around 120 members, I actually found officers to fill the vacant positions in my organization.
I didn't use the tool because I was disconnected. I used it to validate my intuition. I suspected certain members were stepping up. The graph confirmed it by showing me their Centrality. It gave me the objective data I needed to approach them with confidence and say, 'I see the work you are doing, and I want to recognize it.
Making this project and being in massive Discord servers highlighted a critical flaw in manual moderation: active sub-groups often monopolize the chat feed. Their rolling conversations unintentionally bury questions from new members. The project has since evolved to address this 'Clique Noise,' to show that isolated attempts at connection are seen and answered.

results.title: AI in Healthcare: Patient-centered Approach
results.abstract: The integration of AI into clinical settings requires moving past simple algorithmic performance metrics to prioritize patient trust and safety. Leveraging my dual experience as an AI Specialist in Precision Medicine and a developer of applied machine learning solutions, this talk presents a patient-centric framework for responsible AI deployment. We contend that advanced models, specifically RAG-powered LLMs used in applications like precision nutrition, pre-operative assistance, and terminology mapping, are inherently vulnerable to error, bias, and privacy breaches without robust health data governance. We will demonstrate practical strategies for designing systems anchored by stringent data quality and WHO L1-L5 ethical standards, ensuring technical innovation directly serves patient autonomy, mitigates systemic bias, and cultivates the essential clinical trust required for AI to successfully transition from the research lab to the hospital bedside.
results.description: The successful integration of AI in clinical settings requires moving past algorithmic performance to prioritize patient trust and safety. Leveraging my experience as an AI Specialist in Precision Medicine, this talk presents a patient-centric framework for AI deployment. I will demonstrate how advanced models, specifically RAG-powered Multimodal LLMs used in applications like precision nutrition and pre-operative assistance, are inherently vulnerable without robust health data governance. Attendees will learn how to design systems anchored by stringent data quality and WHO L1-L5 ethical standards, ensuring technical innovation directly serves patient autonomy, mitigates bias, and cultivates the clinical trust essential for AI to move from the research lab to clinical applications.

results.title: Tomorrow graphlib, Let us use everybody
results.abstract: Python 3.9 added the `graphlib` module to the standard library. `graphlib` is a library for performing topological sorting of graphs in graph theory. What exactly is a graph in graph theory? What is topological sorting? And ultimately, what can `graphlib` be used for? I'll explain everything—or at least as much as I can—from scratch. From tomorrow onward, let's all start using `graphlib`.
results.description:
### Introduction
Python 3.9 introduced the `graphlib` module to the standard library.
However, the official documentation provides limited explanation about the motivation behind its inclusion or practical use cases, making it a library that can only be effectively used by those who already understand topological sorting.
Ultimately, what can `graphlib` actually be used for? We'll explain everything in detail (as much as possible) before the "batteries" leak.

### Quick Review of Graph Theory
A "graph" refers to the mathematical concept in graph theory.
A graph (roughly speaking) is a mathematical model of objects and their relationships - examples include stations and railway lines in a transportation network, or people and their friendships in social relationships. There are many real-world phenomena that can be effectively modeled using graphs.
- What constitutes a graph
- Undirected graphs vs. directed graphs (the focus here is on directed graphs)
- Linear ordering concepts (partial order relations, total order relations)

### Topological Sorting and Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
Not all graphs can be topologically sorted.
We'll explain that a graph being a directed acyclic graph is equivalently defined as being topologically sortable.

- Definition of a directed acyclic graph
- Definition of topological sorting
- A graph being a directed acyclic graph is equivalently defined as being topologically sortable

### How to Use `graphlib`
Once you understand these concepts, you'll be able to use `graphlib` from tomorrow onward.
At least, you'll have `graphlib` in the back of your mind and be able to recall it instantly when needed.

- How to use `graphlib`
- Practical application: Task processing (determining task execution order, implementing a basic task runner)
results.notes:
This presentation was originally given at PyCon JP 2025 in Japanese by the author themselves.
I will be presenting it in English.

results.title: Multiple and Predicative Dispatch
results.abstract: Complex flow control decisions can be elegantly expressed as dispatch by function signatures.
results.description:
One of the fundamental operations in structured programming is "dispatching" operations to relevant blocks of code, based on some criteria a program can determine. Most Python programmers are familiar with object-oriented programming in which a call to `instance.method()` will examine the class of instance to determine the specific code called. This uses something called an MRO (method resolution order). However, OOP is far from the only possible dispatch strategy.

One alternative approach is something called multiple dispatch, also known as multimethods. Under this approach, the types (and number) of the arguments to a function rather than its inheritance tree determine which code is utilized to implement a function or method call. Function overloading in languages like C++, Java, Julia, or Fortran, are limited examples of multiple dispatch; Python itself also offers a limited version with its `@singledispatch` decorator.

Under true multiple dispatch, the types of ALL the arguments to a function are considered in the decision of which code to execute. Many people have implemented Python modules to support full multiple dispatch, dating from the early 2000s. Those are all nice tools, varying mostly in syntactic approaches and specific resolution rules.

Predicative dispatch is an idea has been implemented less widely. We can create several versions of a function that vary not only in the data types passed as arguments, but also by properties these arguments might have. In a simple example, we might have code paths for integer arguments, but also different paths depending on whether these integers are positive or negative.

Notes: This is a refinement of a talk delivered at PyCon Africa 2025. Since then, the API has been fleshed out and additional tests and documentation created for gnosis-dispatch.
results.notes:
* World_data module example and usage, 5 min
* Type systems as flow control, 5 min
* Types as predicates, 5 min
* Types and predicates in function annotations 10 min
* Wrap-up 5 min

results.title: pip-audit: dozens of vulnerabilities after
results.abstract:
Every modern Python project depends on dozens (sometimes hundreds) of third-party packages. Each of them can - and regularly does - receive security advisories, patches, or CVEs. Even if you “just build business logic”, you inherit all the risks of your supply chain.
This talk is a practical introduction for early-career developers: why dependency security matters, how to audit your environment with pip-audit, what went wrong in several real CVEs found in 2025, and how to build a lightweight but reliable patching workflow without breaking your production environment.
Perfect for anyone who wants to level up their engineering maturity, avoid supply-chain surprises, understand what it really takes to keep dependencies updated sustainably.
results.description:
This talk is a hands-on introduction to dependency security for early-career Python developers. Instead of abstract warnings about “supply-chain risk,” we focus on what engineers actually experience: installing common libraries, running into issues months later, and discovering that the problem was already known and fixed upstream.

We begin by exploring pip-audit, the official PyPA tool for identifying known vulnerabilities in your environment. Attendees will see how pip-audit consumes Python’s Security Advisory Database, how to use it both locally and in CI, and what its output really means. Real CI examples (based on GitLab pipelines) illustrate typical challenges: internal packages with no public advisories, vulnerabilities without available fixes, and strategies to keep the audit stage informative without constantly blocking deployments.

The second part highlights real CVEs from 2025 in widely used libraries. Instead of overwhelming the audience with a long list, we use several short case studies to show what actually went wrong and why these bugs matter. These examples help developers understand where vulnerabilities come from and why staying updated is not optional.

Finally, we outline what a sustainable dependency-maintenance workflow looks like in practice: automated update bots, safe CI validation, prioritizing security patches, and preventing dependency drift. The goal is to offer an approach that small teams and junior developers can adopt without heavy tools or bureaucracy.

By the end of the session, attendees will know how to use pip-audit effectively, recognize real-world risks in common Python packages, and keep their projects reliably up-to-date with minimal friction.
results.notes:
Talk structure and timing:
- 0-2 min: Introduction. What dependency security means for everyday Python development.
- 2–10 min: pip-audit basics. How it works, how advisories are matched, typical output, and CI integration.
- 10–18 min: 2025 CVE case studies. Several short, safe examples from common libraries; 1–2 slightly deeper explanations of root causes (no exploit material).
- 18–26 min: Sustainable update workflow. Automated update bots, safe CI validation, handling advisories with no fix, preventing dependency drift.
- 26–30 min: Recap and Q&A.

Audience level: Beginner to intermediate Python developers. No prior security background required.

Focus of the talk: Practical understanding of how to identify known vulnerabilities, interpret audit results, and build safe, low-overhead dependency maintenance routines in real projects.

What will not be shown:
- No exploit demonstrations or harmful code.
- No unpublished or sensitive vulnerability information.
- No vendor promotion; tools are mentioned only as examples.

Reviewer reassurance:
All examples are based on publicly documented CVEs. The talk focuses on safe engineering practices, CI workflows, and developer education, not offensive techniques.

results.title: From Pydantic v1 to v2: A Deep-Dive Migration for Real Production Systems
results.abstract:
Pydantic v2 is fast, elegant, and thoughtfully redesigned — but migrating a real production system isn’t always as simple as upgrading a dependency. In our project, the v1 → v2 journey started with excitement and quickly turned into detective work: broken validators, unexpected serialization differences, and a few “why is this failing?” moments.
In this talk, I’ll walk you through how we navigated the migration, what we refactored, what we kept, and which patterns saved us hours of debugging. If you’ve been postponing your migration or don’t know where to begin, this session will give you a clear, practical path forward.
results.description:
This talk shares a hands-on, experience-based walkthrough of upgrading a production codebase from Pydantic v1 to v2. We’ll explore why the Pydantic team rewrote the engine, how pydantic-core changes your mental model of validation, and how v2 encourages explicit, functional validators.
I’ll tell the story of how we discovered hidden v1 assumptions in our models, dealt with root_validator deprecation, rewrote custom types, and uncovered performance wins after the move. Along the way, you’ll see real migration patterns, failure points, and before/after code.
Attendees will leave with a battle-tested checklist, practical tips, and confidence to migrate their own systems smoothly.

results.title: 10 ways to make pytest a blast!
results.abstract:
Are you writing UnitTests, and how long does it take you to run a UnitTest?
The number of tests increases in proportion to the size of the test target, but it often happens that you find yourself taking a minute or two to execute a test.
Also, even if you think you are fast enough, your usage will change if your tests are completed 10 times faster.
In the PyPI case study, improved testing reduced the time from 163 seconds to 30 seconds, an 80% reduction.
In my case study, it went from 351 seconds to 33 seconds, an 90% reduction.

There are many advantages to having a test blast.
The obvious one is that you don't "have to wait" for the execution at hand, so you can easily rerun it.
It also eliminates the CI execution queue and reduces service fees.

This talk will cover 10 ways in which UnitTest execution time was reduced for real-world web service development code.
results.description:
10 Ways

1. Reduce setup time for database (3min)
2. Use pytest-xdist to run in parallel (3min)
3. Use sysmon for coverage (3min)
4. Squeeze pytest search paths (3min)
5. Stop unnecessary imports at test startup (3min)
6. Use pytest-profiling to find bottlenecks (3min)
7. Stop DB transaction testing (3min)
8. Narrow down the amount of data for testing (3min)
9. Use sqlite3 memory DB for DB (3min)
10. Find slowest test (3min)

The talk will also introduce the concept of "what should be tested" and how to make decisions to replace bottlenecks. If we think, "In testing, we should reproduce the production behavior as much as possible," the same RDBMS, the same amount of data, the same settings, the same communication, etc., as in production will become bottlenecks, and time will accumulate. In order to replace them with another method, a decision must be made to replace them with a different method than the production method. 

It is also important not to compromise the quality of testing for faster testing. The 10 methods described in this article will speed up testing without compromising the level of quality assurance that was originally done. And when testing is accelerated, you will be able to test areas that were not covered in the past.
results.notes:
This talk is the English version of the content presented in Japanese at PyCon JP 2025.
It also builds upon the PyPI blog post “https://blog.trailofbits.com/2025/05/01/making-pypis-test-suite-81-faster/” and includes additional countermeasures.

results.title: AI Ethics in Practice: Designing Fair and Transparent AI Systems with Python
results.abstract: As artificial intelligence becomes more pervasive, ensuring that AI systems are ethical, fair, and transparent is crucial. This talk explores practical approaches to designing ethical AI systems using Python, with a focus on mitigating bias, enhancing transparency, and ensuring fairness. We’ll discuss how Python libraries like Fairlearn, AIF360, and SHAP can be utilized to build responsible AI that aligns with ethical principles.
results.description:
AI systems can have far-reaching consequences, from discrimination in hiring algorithms to privacy violations in facial recognition systems. As AI continues to evolve, ensuring these systems are ethical and transparent is essential for fostering trust and accountability.

This session will give a comprehensive overview of the key ethical considerations in AI development and how Python can help build more responsible systems. 

We’ll focus on practical tools and techniques for:
- Bias detection and mitigation - How to identify and reduce bias in training data and AI models.
- Fairness in machine learning - Using tools like Fairlearn and AIF360 to assess and improve model fairness across different demographics.
- Explainability and transparency - How to use Python libraries like SHAP and LIME to make machine learning models more transparent and interpretable.
- Building ethically sound models - Practical strategies for integrating AI ethics into the design, deployment, and lifecycle of AI systems.
