results.title: Caring with Python & Data: What Public Information Says About How We Protect Our Communities
results.abstract:
Every year, the Philippines faces an average of 20 typhoons, but their impact tells a deeper story of unequal protection and recovery. Some communities rebuild stronger, while others are left behind. Through Python and publicly available data, we can uncover these patterns and use them to inspire awareness, collaboration, and collective action.

This workshop invites participants to explore how open datasets on hazard exposure, infrastructure, and social conditions can reveal where support is most needed and how data, when analyzed and shared openly, can bridge divides. Using Python for data integration, mapping, and visualization, we will turn numbers into narratives that highlight both challenges and opportunities for resilience.

More than a technical exercise, this session is a call for empathy-driven collaboration, showing how data can connect people, strengthen understanding between citizens and institutions, and help us care for one another through informed, shared responsibility.
results.description:
In this hands-on workshop, we will explore how to use Python to work with publicly available data related to disaster preparedness and recovery. Participants will learn where to find credible data sources, how to collect and organize datasets, and how to identify and address common data quality issues. The session will cover essential techniques for cleaning, analyzing, and visualizing information to communicate clear and impactful stories through data.

If time permits, we will also introduce how machine learning and AI can be applied to deepen insights and uncover patterns that traditional analysis might miss.

By the end of this session, participants will be able to use Python to turn public data into clearer understanding and meaningful insights, empowering citizens to stay informed, ask better questions, and take part in building more resilient communities.
results.notes: I originally designed this as a workshop because there is so much to unpack and teach about the entire process, from finding and preparing public data to analyzing and visualizing it. However, if we have limited time, this session can be repackaged as a talk. The goal is to inspire participants to use Python and public data not just for technical exploration, but as tools for understanding, accountability, and collective action toward more resilient communities.

results.title: Air: The Web Framework AI Can Actually Understand
results.abstract: Air is a Python web framework built from scratch for AI-assisted development. We'll show how designing for LLM legibility makes you a better coder whether or not you use AI. This includes explicit types, clear contracts, documentation that reads like a book. The goal is a framework that works astoundingly well with commercial or open source LLMs, or even the introspection features of your LLM. While Air is being architected by the speakers of this talk, a lot of the Air codebase is collaboratively being developed at sprints in the Python Asia community, starting in Davao, Baguio, and Manila but soon growing to Singapore and beyond.
results.description:
"Most Python web frameworks were designed before AI-assisted development existed. Their patterns, conventions, and documentation assume a human reader with years of context. LLMs struggle with them, even with all their documentation already ingested into the LLM catalogue. Air is different.

Built from scratch for developers who work alongside AI, Air prioritizes what makes code legible to both humans and machines: explicit types, clear contracts, disciplined testing, code examples in the docstrings for every callable that have their own tests, and documentation that reads like a book. The same practices that help an LLM understand your code help your future self, your teammates, and anyone who inherits your project. Designing for AI makes you a better coder.

These principles mean that whether or not you are using expensive hosted LLMs, open source LLMs running on your laptop, or just the introspection features of your IDE, your experience with AIr will be unusually positive.

We are here running sprints in the Philippines and soon throughout Asia, with the rest of the world to follow. Django started as a web framework built for a newspaper in the US, and its roots are clear. We want Air to be a web framework with global DNA starting with clear Filipino and Asian influence. That way we can be sure the future of the web here is free, open source, and designed for our needs here in Asia from the start."

results.title: Security Through Data: Building Interactive Forensic Analysis Tools with Python and Streamlit
results.abstract: Cybersecurity generates massive amounts of data, but making sense of it requires the right tools. Discover how Python's data science ecosystem combined with Streamlit can transform security logs, network traffic, and forensic data into interactive dashboards that empower both security professionals and stakeholders to make informed decisions. This talk demonstrates building real-time security analysis tools that are both powerful and accessible.
results.description:
Security teams are drowning in data—from network logs and intrusion detection alerts to forensic artifacts and threat intelligence. This session shows how Python bridges the gap between data science and cybersecurity, turning raw security data into actionable intelligence.
We'll explore building an interactive security analysis application using Streamlit that demonstrates:

Processing and analyzing security logs with pandas for anomaly detection
Visualizing network traffic patterns and security events with matplotlib and plotly
Creating real-time dashboards for threat monitoring and incident response
Implementing data security best practices in your analysis pipeline
Making forensic analysis accessible through interactive visualizations
results.notes:
Through live demonstrations, attendees will see how Python's data science libraries (pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn) combine with Streamlit's intuitive framework to create tools that security analysts, forensic investigators, and decision-makers can actually use. Whether you're in cybersecurity, data science, or simply curious about applying Python to security challenges, you'll leave with practical knowledge and inspiration to build your own security analytics tools.
No prior cybersecurity knowledge required—just curiosity about how data science can make the digital world safer.

results.title: Agent-Assisted Python at Enterprise Scale
results.abstract: Drawing from experience building and deploying multiple enterprise builds, this talk shares a pragmatic, repeatable workflow that speeds teams up **without** sacrificing code quality. We’ll cover: bootstrapping with `uv`; keeping repos healthy with `pre-commit`, `mypy`, and `vulture`; a daily loop that pairs Claude Code or GitHub Copilot with live human review; and a PR pipeline that uses Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools with a minimum pytest coverage floor. You’ll see fast feedback habits using `curl` for APIs and Playwright (via MCP) for UIs, how to pick well-maintained libraries, and why reading the source beats “vibes.” We’ll also tackle context rot, prompt hygiene, and my favorite lightweight stack, the **FAB** stack (Flask, Alpine, Bootstrap).
results.description:
## What This Session Covers
- **Setup that sticks:** `uv init`, `pre-commit`, `mypy`, `vulture` as dev deps. Keep prompts/tools minimal so agents aren’t flooded by lint noise.  
- **Daily development loop:** Draft with Claude Code or Copilot + *live* review → type checks, tests, static analysis → clean push routine.  
- **PR workflow that scales:** MCP tools in GitHub for CI signals; short-lived branches; auto-merge *only* when tests pass with **≥40%** pytest coverage and no long-running tests.  
- **Fast feedback over ceremony:** `curl` for API iteration; Playwright (MCP) for UI checks; tighten the build–run–inspect cycle. Prefer actively maintained libs with clear docs; when APIs shift, fetch current syntax straight into agent context.  
- **Principles that keep you sane:** Read the code; clarity over cleverness; suggestion to default to **FAB** (Flask, Alpine, Bootstrap) to limit moving parts. Small modules, targeted DRY, SOLID-ish seams so humans and models can navigate.  
- **Future-facing habits:** Watch for context rot, tailor tools per prompt, strengthen CLI/Bash for the coming CLI-agent era, and keep architecture explicit to reduce agent wander.

---

## Concrete Takeaways
By the end, attendees will be able to:
1. Bootstrap a Python repo with `uv`, `pre-commit`, `mypy`, and `vulture` in under 5 minutes.  
2. Run a daily human-in-the-loop agent workflow that reliably ships increments.  
3. Enforce a pragmatic coverage floor (≥40%) and CI gates using MCP-enabled tools.  
4. Shorten feedback loops for APIs/UIs with `curl` and Playwright (MCP).  
5. Prevent context rot and keep prompts lean, structured, and reusable.  
6. Apply the **FAB** stack to cut cognitive overhead for dashboards and internal tools.
results.notes:
- **Speaker:** Lead maintainer of *ingenious* and lead for multiple enterprise AI builds across classification, RAG, vision-assisted inspection, and rubric-based agent workflows.  
- **Project link:** [ingenious on GitHub](https://github.com/Insight-Services-APAC/ingenious)  
- **Blog post for context:** [AI-assisted programming tips](https://blog.insight-services-apac.dev/2025/08/16/ai-assisted-programming-tips)  
- **LinkedIn:** [kokkodev](https://www.linkedin.com/in/kokkodev/)

results.title: From Bytecode to Native Code with CPython’s JIT
results.abstract: CPython has merged an experimental just‑in‑time (JIT) compiler into the main branch, marking a major shift from “interpret bytecode” toward “compile hot paths to native code.” This talk is a friendly, practical tour of what that means. We’ll cover why the specializing adaptive interpreter hit limits, what bytecode and native code are in plain terms, and how CPython’s copy‑and‑patch JIT stitches together prebuilt machine‑code templates at runtime. What’s new is not just “a JIT,” but a template approach generated from the same instruction DSL that defines the interpreter—keeping maintenance low, avoiding heavy runtime dependencies, and enabling broad platform support. You’ll see how CPython’s JIT differs from AOT compilers (Cython), user‑space JITs (Numba), and alternative runtimes (PyPy), and where it fits in the execution stack. We’ll verify it’s working by inspecting compiled traces—no benchmark hype. You’ll leave with a clear mental model and practical guidance for evaluating it on Python 3.14.
results.description:
Welcome and goals (2 min)
	• Introduction: Who I am.
	• What you’ll learn in this session.
	
Why a JIT in CPython (4 min)
	• Limits of the adaptive interpreter.
	• Overhead from instruction dispatch.

JIT fundamentals in plain terms (5 min)
	• Difference between bytecode and native code.
	• JIT compilation vs ahead-of-time (AOT) compilation.

How CPython’s JIT works (6 min)
	• Internal mechanism of the JIT.
	• Copy-and-patch of templates from the instruction DSL.

Seeing it in action (5 min)
	• Inspecting compiled traces and deoptimizations.
	• Perspective from CPython’s implementation.

Choosing the right tool (4 min)
	• Comparing where CPython’s JIT fits among Cython, Numba, and PyPy.

Q&A (4 min)
results.notes: The implementation of a JIT compiler in CPython will be introduced for the first time in Python 3.14, so this topic has not yet been widely covered in existing sessions. My talk is mainly aimed at beginner to intermediate engineers, and I will cover the basics of JIT compilers. However, I also plan to discuss some aspects of the actual CPython code implementation, making the session valuable even for more experienced engineers.

results.title: uv add open-source: Start Without Fear
results.abstract: Open source looks exciting from the outside, yet intimidating when you try to take your first step. We’ll start with UV, exploring why the Python community is moving toward it and how it changes the developer experience, then pivot to the real upgrade: adding yourself to the open-source ecosystem. The main focus of my talk is to help participants find projects that match their skills, debunking common fears that hold beginners back, and navigating initiative programs that fast-track their journey from observer to active contributor. I bring experience as a maintainer of OpenAGI, a framework for multi-agent systems, and BeyondLLM, a toolkit to evaluate and experiment with RAG pipelines. I am also a two-time Google Summer of Code contributor.
results.description:
Most developers want to be part of open source, but pause at the thought, “I don’t know where to begin.” This talk simplifies that first step. We begin with uv, a modern Python packaging tool that supports how projects are managed and shared. From there, we explore how open source can shape your career and how taking initiative creates opportunities.

What myths hold us back from jumping in? I will share a practical roadmap and examples of small steps that can lead to a visible impact. By the end, you will know how to start, where to find the right projects, and how to stay consistent beyond your first contribution. 

I am a two-time Google Summer of Code contributor at Red Hen Lab (2024) and caMicroscope(2023). I will share my journey and learnings to help the audience get real value.

results.title: From Config to Cloud : A Pythonic Approach to Platform Independent Design
results.abstract:
Recent high-profile cloud outages - such as the **Amazon Web Services (AWS) service failure in October 2025** that disrupted thousands of applications worldwide , have exposed how fragile modern infrastructure becomes when locked into a single provider. For many businesses, hours of downtime translate directly into lost revenue, broken customer trust, and cascading failures.

In this talk, I’ll present a **configuration-driven, cloud-agnostic architecture** built with Python, designed to keep systems resilient even when one cloud fails. Instead of relying on vendor-specific services, we’ll explore how to design **modular abstractions** that let applications switch seamlessly between cloud providers through configuration alone.

We’ll also dive into **cross-cloud data synchronization** - for example, keeping datasets in Amazon DynamoDB mirrored in their equivalents on another cloud to minimize data loss during failover.

A live demo will showcase a **Python-based service** dynamically switching its back-end and data storage between clouds, proving that true resilience doesn’t require duplicating your codebase.
results.description:
Cloud reliability is no longer a guarantee. The **AWS service outage in October 2025**, which affected thousands of applications worldwide, reminded developers how fragile vendor-locked systems can be. When a single provider experiences downtime, dependent services cascade into failure, breaking user trust and business continuity.

This talk proposes a **configuration-driven, Python-based architecture** that makes applications *resilient by design*. Instead of hard-binding logic to a single cloud SDK, the pattern uses **modular abstractions**, **dependency injection**, and **runtime configuration switching** to dynamically route workloads between cloud providers. With Python libraries such as `importlib`, `pydantic`, and `boto3` / `google-cloud-storage`, developers can load the right integrations at runtime without changing the codebase.

#### The session will unpack the full architectural flow:
1. **Environment detection and configuration parsing** -> Using structured config files or environment variables to identify the active platform.  

2. **Dynamic module loading** -> Leveraging `importlib` and factory patterns to import the correct provider implementations (for storage, messaging, or secrets).  

3. **Cross-cloud data synchronization** -> Designing lightweight replication pipelines that mirror datasets between equivalent services (e.g., DynamoDB <-> Firestore <-> Cosmos DB) with minimal lag. 
 
4. **Failover orchestration** -> Coordinating a seamless cut-over through event-driven triggers and state reconciliation.

A live demo will showcase a small **Python service** that can switch its backend between AWS and GCP purely through configuration , while keeping its dataset synchronized across clouds. Attendees will see how metadata tables, replication queues, and conflict-resolution strategies preserve integrity during failover.

Rather than advocating any specific vendor, this talk focuses on **portable design principles**: how Python’s flexibility and dynamic import system can help developers build infrastructure-agnostic, fault-tolerant systems.

Ultimately, this is about rethinking reliability - proving that with the right design, *switching clouds can be as easy as changing a config file.*
results.notes:
If possible, I would also love to host a hands on workshop post this talk where participants and I will build the system together step by step.

Every participant will leave with a working mini multi-cloud Python app, hands-on experience with cross-cloud design, and a practical understanding of how to engineer for reliability through configuration.

results.title: Architectures of Ambiguity: Mapping the Technical Hurdles of Cultural Sensitivity in Localized LLMs
results.abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) expand into global markets, they often hit a "Nuance Gap" or a failure to distinguish literal meaning from cultural context. This presentation examines the technical hurdles of building culturally competent AI through two lenses, namely, linguistic ambiguity (sarcasm) and localized safety (toxicity). Using the Philippines as a case study, we identify four critical hurdles: (1) the Linguistic Inversion Problem, where sarcasm flips intended sentiment; (2) the Context Vacuum, where text lacks the "cultural scaffolding" necessary for interpretation; (3) the Data Desert of low-resource languages; and (4) the Western-Centricity of standard safety filters. We propose a roadmap for researchers to move beyond literal translation toward AI that respects the "unspoken" and "unseen" nuances of regional identities.
results.description:
"This talk examines the technical and socio-linguistic hurdles of developing culturally sensitive Large Language Models (LLMs) for the Philippines, a high-stakes digital environment where roughly 51% of citizens struggle to identify online misinformation. Using two major research pillars—FilSarcasm (sarcasm detection) and LLigtas (toxicity and safety)—we demonstrate why ""grammatical correctness"" is insufficient for safe AI deployment in Southeast Asia.

The talk is structured around the ""Architectures of Ambiguity"" that emerge when global, Western-centric models encounter localized, high-context languages.

Topic of the Talk
1. The Linguistic Inversion Problem (Sarcasm)
Sarcasm acts as a ""sentiment inverter,"" often flipping a literally positive statement into a sharp negative critique.  Rule-based and traditional machine learning systems often fail because they lack the ""paralinguistic cues"" (tone of voice, facial expressions) humans use to detect irony.  We introduce FilSarcasm, a novel benchmark of 10,000 political tweets. A key technical innovation is the use of ""Cultural Scaffolding""—leveraging high-resource models like Gemini 2.5 Pro to generate political and social context that ""teaches"" smaller models how to resolve linguistic ambiguity.

2. Localizing Toxicity and Safety (LLigtas)
While global models include safety filters, these are often WIRED (Western, Industrialized, Rich, Educated, and Democratic), causing them to miss localized ""dog whistles"" or culturally specific harms.There is a critical gap in assessing cultural safety, specifically in the domains of toxicity, subjective topics, and emotional harm in the Filipino context. The LLigtas project develops a manually curated benchmark of culturally relevant prompts. We evaluate models across eight specific harm categories, using human evaluators to establish a ""ground truth"" that respects local norms and beliefs.

Key Technical Challenges Explored:
1. The ""Data Desert"". Addressing the extreme scarcity of high-quality, annotated text corpora for low-resource languages like Filipino.
2. Cross-Lingual Transfer Learning.  Investigating sequential training pipelines—such as pre-fine-tuning on the English SARC dataset using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) before adapting to Filipino.
3. The difficulty of establishing ""inter-rater reliability"" for subjective concepts like sarcasm, where human annotators may disagree based on their own cultural perspectives.

Conclusion.  We conclude by framing the Philippines as a proxy for other Southeast Asian nations. The talk provides a roadmap for moving beyond literal translation toward culturally aligned AI that can mitigate online disinformation and protect users in diverse linguistic landscapes."

results.title: Teaching Python to 'Smell' Disease: A Signal Processing & ML Workflow
results.abstract: Raw sensor data from medical devices is often noisy, complex, and intimidating. How do we turn these chaotic signals into a clear "yes" or "no" for disease screening? This talk demystifies the end-to-end data science workflow for translating physical measurements from a sensor array into a reliable diagnostic prediction. We will build a complete, repeatable pipeline using Pandas, Scikit-learn, and modern visualization tools to process, analyze, and model real-world time-series data. Attendees will leave with a practical blueprint for tackling their own IoT and sensor-based projects, bridging the gap between hardware physics and impactful machine learning.
results.description:
Every biological process, from health to disease, has a physical footprint. This talk is about capturing and interpreting one such signature: the specific chemical compounds in a patient’s breath, measured by the shifting electrical resistance of a sensor array.

However, capturing this raw sensor data is only half the battle. This session provides a practical, repeatable workflow using Python to translate these complex physical measurements into a clear signal for medical screening. We'll bridge the gap between fundamental physics and applied data science, tackling a challenge at the forefront of IoT and med-tech that is rarely covered in depth at software conferences.

We'll tackle this in three practical steps:

* Part 1: The Data Pipeline \- From Raw Signals to a Clean Dataset (Approx. 9 minutes)  
  We'll build a robust data engineering pipeline to tame noisy, high-dimensional time-series data. This includes ingesting raw signals into a pandas DataFrame, implementing baseline correction to remove sensor drift, and performing feature extraction (peak intensity, response time, etc.) to create a tidy, model-ready dataset.  
* Part 2: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) \- Finding the Patterns (Approx. 6 minutes)  
  Before modeling, we must ask: is there a signal in the noise? We'll use visualization libraries to "see" the difference between healthy and sick samples. Through boxplots, PCA, and LDA plots, we will determine if our samples form distinct clusters and explore correlations between sensor features and medical records.  
* Part 3: Predictive Modeling \- Teaching Python to “Smell” Disease (Approx. 8 minutes)  
  In the final part, we'll build a scikit-learn classifier to automatically identify new, unseen samples. The focus will be on reliability and trust, covering best practices like train\_test\_split, feature scaling with StandardScaler, and building an interpretable Support Vector Machine (SVM) model. We will go beyond simple accuracy to discuss why metrics like precision, recall, and the confusion matrix are critical in a medical context.

### **Who Should Attend?**

This talk is designed for:

* Data scientists and analysts interested in moving beyond clean, tabular data into the world of noisy, time-series sensor signals.  
* Engineers and developers working on IoT projects who want to build more intelligent data processing and prediction pipelines.  
* Pythonistas curious about practical, real-world applications of machine learning in the medical technology and hardware space.  
* Students and researchers looking for a complete, end-to-end example of a signal processing and machine learning project.

### **Takeaways for the Audience**

Attendees will leave with a clear, practical, and repeatable blueprint for how to:

1. Implement a data pipeline in Python to process, clean, and extract features from noisy, real-world time-series sensor data.  
2. Apply powerful EDA techniques to validate whether a meaningful signal exists in sensor data before committing to complex modeling.  
3. Build, train, and robustly evaluate a machine learning classifier for a diagnostic task, understanding why metrics like precision and recall are critical for real-world applications.
results.notes:
## **Speaker Bio**:  
Iman Rahman is a physicist and researcher at Universitas Gadjada Mada specializing in AI-integrated sensor systems. His work involves developing novel measurement devices for diverse applications, including food authentication and medical health screening. By combining a deep understanding of sensor physics with the power of Python's data science stack, he aims to make complex diagnostic technologies more accessible.

## **Why This Talk is a Great Fit for PythonAsia 2026**

* **Originality & Insight:** By bridging the gap between the physics of sensor technology and the practice of data science, this session offers a unique perspective. Attendees will gain hard-to-find knowledge on how to handle the specific challenges of physical sensor data.  
* **Relevance:** The session is at the forefront of a major trend: the application of Python and AI in IoT and medical technology. It lowers the barrier for Python developers who are new to signal processing, adding value to both beginners and experienced practitioners.  
* **Feasibility:** The topic is structured to be delivered effectively within a 30-minute timeframe, with clear time allocations for each major section, ensuring a well-paced and comprehensive presentation.

results.title: Elevate Your Development Workflow: Building Production-Ready Apps with AI Coding Agents
results.abstract: AI Agents are transforming how we approach software development, promising to automate routine tasks, streamline complex workflows, and empower developers to focus on creativity and strategy. But what exactly are AI agents, and how do they differ from traditional Large Language Models (LLMs)? In this interactive workshop, you'll discover precisely what sets AI coding agents apart, explore best practices for effective collaboration, and delve into advanced concepts like tool integration and the MCP protocol.
results.description: After an insightful theoretical exploration, we will transition to a practical, hands-on coding session using Zencoder, a cutting-edge AI coding agent platform. Together, we will progressively develop a complete, production-ready web application - from backend architecture to polished frontend interfaces - leveraging AI collaboration at each step. This session is ideal if you have at least a foundational Python programming experience and a desire to elevate your development process. Please bring your laptop pre-installed with VSCode or JetBrains IDE (e.g. PyCharm) to fully participate.

results.title: From Genes to Grids: How Python Brings Sequence Alignment to Life
results.abstract:
This 30-minute talk is for intermediate Python users curious about scientific computing and bioinformatics beginners. It demystifies the fundamental algorithms for sequence alignment (Needleman-Wunsch and Smith-Waterman) by connecting them to core CS (dynamic programming) and familiar concepts (like diff tools).

The target audience of this talk is categorized into two (2): primary and secondary audiences.

a. Primary Audience:  Intermediate Python users curious about applying coding to science, and bioinformatics beginners

b. Secondary Audience: Data scientists exploring new domains, and educators seeking practical, high-impact examples of complex algorithms.

In this talk, I will cover the following topics:

1.  Introduction: The "Alignment" Problem (5 min)
->This section introduces the core concepts of sequence alignment (global vs. local) using a familiar analogy: file comparison tools.
     
2. The Logic: From Grid to Python Snippet (10 min)
-> A practical walkthrough of the dynamic programming logic behind sequence alignment, translating the algorithm's "grid" into an intuitive Python snippet.

3. Python in the Wild: Biopython & Beyond (5 min)
-> This segment bridges the gap from theory to practice, demonstrating how to use the foundational Biopython library to perform real-world alignments.

4.  Impact, Takeaways & Q&A Handoff (5 min)
-> This final part explores the broad impact of alignment in fields like genomics and NLP, summarizes key takeaways, and opens the floor for Q&A.
results.description: Note: For better understanding and appreciation of the talk, having an idea about bioinformatics can help but is not required.
results.notes:
Additional information about the speaker:
I am currently a Junior Cloud Engineer at eCloudvalley, and is also an AWS Community Builder in Data. I am passionate about being a multipotentialite in which I integrate different fields of interest, which are tech and bio. I believe in being an outlier who willingly explores the least explored path, Beyond the Vinculum.

results.title: CPython Under Load: NoGIL, Green Threads, AsyncIO vs. Other Langs: deep-dive in core and benchmarks
results.abstract:
CPython drops the GIL. Dive into NoGIL, green threads, and async — with real benchmarks vs. multiprocessing & other languages. Learn when free-threading gives a boost, where it doesn't, and how it works under the hood. 

What the audience will gain
- Developers – a clear mental model of when free threading delivers speed-ups, when CPython is “fast enough”, and when other tools or languages are the right choice.
- High-load engineers – fresh benchmark numbers compared with leading languages.
- Architects & CTOs – a risk map for migration and a step-by-step checklist for rolling FT into production.
results.description:
In 2024 - 2026 CPython is undergoing its largest performance leap in two decades: the GIL is gone, and the runtime now sports adaptive specialization, micro-opcodes, a JIT, and even a tail-calling interpreter. Using real code from CPython 3.14/3.15, I’ll show you how to obtain true thread-level parallelism without changing a single line of your application.

We will benchmark free-threaded CPython on practical workloads:
Monte-Carlo simulations
Heavy DataFrame crunching
ML-pipeline orchestration
Running hundreds of reinforcement-learning environments in parallel

You’ll see where free threading reliably beats multiprocessing, and where migrating isn’t worth the effort. We’ll compare the results with other languages, discuss migration strategies, and inspect the hot spots with perf record and flamegraphs. Bring your own cases — we’ll debate and hunt down bottlenecks together!
results.notes:

results.title: Life Under the _: A Guide to Python's Internal Conventions
results.abstract: Python's elegant simplicity often hides a powerful system of internal conventions, many revolving around the humble underscore (_). This talk, "Life Under the _: A Guide to Python's Internal Conventions," demystifies the rules and mechanisms behind the scenes of Python's most powerful features. We will explore the single underscore (_) for stylistic clarity, the purpose of name mangling using double underscores (__), and the practical application of "Dunder Methods" (e.g., __init__, __add__) for customizing object behavior and implementing language protocols. Finally, we will dive into Python’s attribute access logic and the sophisticated mechanics of Data and Non-Data Descriptors, the building blocks for features like @property, classmethod, and staticmethod. Attendees will leave with a clearer understanding of how to read and write more idiomatic, maintainable, and powerful Python code.
results.description:
**Talk Description**
Take a deep dive into the conventions that make Python a flexible and robust language. This session is a comprehensive guide to mastering the many roles of the underscore and its variations in Python development.

**Key Takeaways:**
- Underscore Conventions: Learn the practical uses of the single underscore (_) for accessing previous values in the interactive shell, enhancing big number readability, and acting as a throwaway variable in loops or unpacking.
- Variable Naming and Privacy: Understand the differences between the three main variable conventions: the weak-private indicator (_var), the keyword-avoidance suffix (var_), and the stronger privacy enforcement of name mangling (__var).
- Dunder Methods (Magic Methods): Unlock the power of double-underscore methods to customize fundamental Python operations. We will examine core dunders like __init__, __str__, __add__ (for operator overloading), __len__, __iter__ (for collection protocols), and __call__.
- Advanced Dunder Features: Explore powerful features such as __slots__ for memory optimization, __dict__, and the utility of __missing__ in specialized dictionary subclasses.
- Descriptors Explained: Master the concept of Descriptors—the mechanism behind properties, class methods, and static methods. We will break down the __get__, __set__, and __delete__ descriptor methods and distinguish between Data and Non-Data Descriptors, detailing how they take precedence during Python's attribute access logic.

*This talk is ideal for intermediate to advanced Python developers looking to move beyond surface-level syntax and understand the core language mechanisms.*
results.notes: Slides: https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Qb4_Wden5nxEwoWZgXlKxHEzM5VTILpAReg_NikwP4A/edit?usp=sharing

This session delves into the development of a Python-based MCP server that enables seamless interactions between large language models and YDB’s open-source Distributed SQL DBMS. Learn about the design decisions, challenges faced, and the practical benefits of integrating MCP into data architecture. Ideal for developers and AI practitioners aiming to enhance AI capabilities with direct database access.
