results.title: Test-Driven Golden Paths: Using Python to Validate Backstage Internal Developer Platforms
results.abstract:
Backstage has quickly become a de facto framework for building internal developer portals (IDPs) for teams implementing platform engineering. Powered by a centralized software catalog and opinionated "Golden Path" software templates, it lets platform teams standardize how services are created, documented, and deployed. 

The catch is that these templates are effectively part of your platform. If a Backstage template silently breaks, for example, by missing annotations, using outdated CI configuration, or including invalid infrastructure snippets, every new Python service created from it starts life already broken. 

In this talk, we treat Golden Paths like production code and show how to test them using Python. We will walk through building a small Python test harness that generates projects from Backstage templates, validates their catalog metadata and annotations, verifies that the generated Python service installs, lints, and passes tests, and optionally inspects dry-run plans to ensure required infrastructure actions are present. Attendees will leave with concrete patterns and code they can drop into CI so that every change to their Golden Paths is automatically tested with Python before any developer ever clicks "Create Service."
results.description:
Backstage is widely used as the foundation for internal developer platforms. It centralizes service metadata, documentation, and self-service operations via a software catalog and plugin ecosystem. A key feature is its software templates, often called Golden Paths, which define opinionated workflows for spinning up new services, pipelines, and infrastructure with consistent best practices baked in. Production deployments at various organizations lean on these templates to deliver standardized, governed self-service for developers, and industry guidance increasingly recommends versioning and testing templates as seriously as application code.

Despite this, many teams still treat templates as “just YAML.” When a template breaks, for example, by missing required metadata, misconfiguring CI, or triggering invalid infrastructure actions, every new Python project created from it inherits the same problems. The result is that bugs propagate from a single template into every new repository generated from it.

This talk walks you through treating Golden Paths as first-class, testable artifacts and using Python as the test harness around Backstage. After a short primer on Backstage, IDPs, and Golden Paths, with real examples such as deploying Python applications to Kubernetes using template-driven workflows, we will live-code a small Python test suite. That suite will call Backstage’s scaffolder backend to generate a project from a specific template, inspect the resulting file layout and catalog-info.yaml (including owner, system, lifecycle, and documentation configuration), and run basic Python project checks such as installation, pytest, and linting. We will also demonstrate how to parse the scaffolder dry-run output to assert that required infrastructure actions, such as repository creation or deployment pipeline registration, are present.

results.title: Cutting Our LLM Costs by 30% with Toon Python: Human-Readable, Schema-Aware JSON Prompts
results.abstract: Traditional JSON prompts frequently contain excessive punctuation, redundant keys, and structural excess, all of which utilize tokens. TOON is a concise, human-understandable, schema-recognizing serialization format designed especially for LLM prompts. Transforming JSON into TOON prior to dispatching it to the LLM greatly decreased our token consumption, resulting in a cost savings of approximately 30% (or higher) on actual workloads. In this presentation, I will discuss how implementing TOON in a production-quality LLM system resulted in significant cost reductions, enhanced reliability, and created a more explicit and manageable prompt structure, all while maintaining clarity and expressiveness
results.description: TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) is a schema-aware, human-readable alternative to JSON that dramatically reduces token usage for LLM prompts by removing punctuation overhead and compressing repetitive structure. In my production LLM pipeline, replacing JSON prompts with TOON cut token consumption by roughly 30%, lowered latency, and made prompts more predictable and easier to validate. This talk walks through how TOON works, why it’s more tokenizer-friendly than JSON, how I migrated an existing prompt system using toon-python, the before/after benchmarks, and the unexpected benefits in maintainability and reliability. Attendees will learn how a simple change in prompt format—not the model—can deliver significant cost and performance gains in real-world LLM applications.

results.title: Debug smarter, not harder - all you need to know about debugging in Python
results.abstract: This talk tackles the common, yet ultimately limiting, practice of using `print` statements for debugging in Python. We will explore why relying on `print` statements often becomes inefficient and cumbersome as applications grow in complexity.
results.description:
The presentation will guide attendees through a transition to professional-grade debugging tools, beginning with a detailed look at the built-in Python debugger, `pdb`, including essential commands and workflows. 

Next, I will demystify the powerful debugging capabilities integrated into modern Integrated Development Environments (IDEs), specifically demonstrating `debugpy` and its seamless application within popular tools like VS Code and PyCharm. 

Finally, the talk will introduce debug logging as a robust, scalable alternative to temporary print statements, covering best practices for when and how to implement a logging framework to manage application state effectively. 

By contrasting these strategies, this session aims to empower developers to choose the right tool. Be it a dedicated debugger, an IDE feature, or a logging framework, for any challenge. Enabling smarter, faster, and more effective code remediation in their daily work.

Outline:

- Why not use “print”?
    - Situation when you can use “print” to debug
    - Situation when using “print” is not helpful
- Debuggers used in Python
    - pdb : why use pdb not “print”
    - Debugging in IDEs
        - Debugpy: what is it?
        - Debugpy used in VS Code and PyCharm
- Debug logging
    - When should you use debug logging?
    - Logging vs “print”
    - How to manage debug logs?
- Conclusion and summary
    - Use the right tools and strategies for the situation
    - Summarise the debugging strategies that have been introduced

results.title: How I Put a Rocket Engine into Python: Porting ClickHouse for High-Performance App
results.abstract:
### What they'll learn from your talk

 - Practical techniques for embedding C++ libraries into Python using tools like PyBind11
 - How to handle performance, threading, and memory management in hybrid Python–C++ systems
 - How to profile and optimize data-intensive Python modules that rely on native code

### What background experience they should have to get the most out of your talk.

 - Developers familiar with C++-based Python libraries (NumPy, Pandas, PyTorch)
 - Engineers curious about pushing Python’s performance limits
 - Anyone who’s ever wondered what happens when you try to fit a rocket engine inside a Python module 🚀
results.description:
### What I'll be talking about.

- TLDR: What if you could bring the blazing speed of a C++ analytics engine directly into Python — without giving up the simplicity that makes Python so great?

- In this talk, I’ll share how I ported **ClickHouse**, a high-performance C++ OLAP engine, into a native **Python module**. You’ll see the real-world challenges behind bridging these two worlds — from managing memory across language boundaries to **overcoming the challenges of integrating Jemalloc in shared libraries and enabling zero-copy reads/writes Pandas DataFrames** — and how I turned a massive C++ system into something that could be imported with a simple `import` statement.

results.title: Why Python Works Well for Kids (Visual to Text Coding Transition)
results.abstract: Python offers a simple, readable syntax that helps young learners shift smoothly from block coding to text-based programming, supporting deeper problem-solving habits and clearer logical thinking.
results.description: This session looks at why Python fits well in early programming classes, with practical strategies for guiding students as they move from visual blocks to written code. The talk covers classroom techniques, sample activities, and ways to build confidence in beginners so they can write code with purpose.

results.title: Beyond Basic RAG: Boosting Accuracy with Hybrid Search and Fusion Algorithms.
results.abstract:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become the standard architecture for grounding Large Language Models (LLMs) on private data. However, developers often hit a performance ceiling with **Naive** RAG implementations that rely solely on vector-based similarity functions. While semantic search is powerful, it usually struggles with exact keyword matches, domain-specific acronyms, and queries that require precise lexical retrieval.

This talk explores the architecture of high-accuracy RAG pipelines by implementing Hybrid Search and Re-ranking strategies using **LangChain**.

We will demonstrate why relying on a single retrieval method is often insufficient for production-grade applications. Attendees will learn how to implement a Hybrid Search layer that combines the vector search and full-text search. Furthermore, we will also dive into the **Re-ranking** stage by using cross-encoders to score and a fusion algorithm to retrieve documents, ensuring the LLM sees only the most relevant context and reducing hallucinations and latency.
results.description:
- Evolution of the recommender system (2 minutes)
- Introduction to the Hybrid Search. (5 minutes)
  **Short description**: Why hybrid search matters - overview of keyword vs. semantic retrieval.
- Introduction to the Fusion algorithm (10 minutes)
  **Short description**: Why the fusion algorithm matters - overview of Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) vs Relative Score Fusion (RSF)
- Proposed RAG pipeline (5 minutes)
  **Short description**: Overview of key components and core snippet of fusion algorithm.
- Key takeaway (3 minutes)
- Q&A (5 minutes)
results.notes: I've been working with databases and software development for 20 years. Currently, I'm a MongoDB senior consulting engineer based in Singapore. I've previously spoken at conferences such as PyCon APAC 2025, PyCon Lithuania 2025, PyCon SG 2025, PyCon Thailand 2025, and Global Azure Thailand 2025. I’m also part of the community leader team for the MongoDB and PyLanna User Group in Thailand, which brings together over 3,000 developers.

results.title: Stop Spinning Up DBs Per User - Use Multitenant Vector Stores
results.abstract: When building AI/RAG apps where many users upload and search documents, a common instinct is to give each user their own vector database. But that architecture doesn’t scale costs, infrastructure complexity, and performance overhead explode fast. In this talk, you’ll learn how multitenancy in vector databases lets you serve many users efficiently using a shared index, while keeping data isolated. We’ll see why separate DBs per user is a bad default, and explore the alternative: payload-based multitenancy and tiered-shard multitenancy. The talk shows practical patterns and explains when and how to scale your system with safety and cost in mind.
results.description:
If you're building a document search application, a retrieval based AI assistant, or any multi user RAG system, a common question comes up: Should each user get their own vector database? The short answer is usually no, and this is where multitenancy becomes important.

This talk explains the problems with giving every user a separate vector database such as high cost, unnecessary storage duplication, performance issues, and operational complexity. It shows how a multitenant vector database that uses a shared collection with payload based filtering can isolate each user's data safely while keeping the system efficient and scalable.

We will also explore advanced patterns like tiered multitenancy and sharding where smaller users share a fallback shard and heavy users are moved to dedicated shards. A case study using tools like Qdrant and FastEmbed will demonstrate how this works in practice along with real world concerns like noisy neighbor behavior, performance tradeoffs, shard promotion, and designing for long term growth.

By the end of the session, you will understand when to use multitenant vector storage versus per user isolation and how to build scalable multi user vector search systems using open source tools and simple design patterns.

This is a practical and hands on talk. You will see real examples, design ideas, and code level guidance in Python and open source LLM frameworks.

# Outline:
0–3 min	Introduction & Problem Statement
3–7 min	What is a Vector Database & Multitenancy?
7–12 min Payload-based Multitenancy
12–16 min When Shared Index Is Not Enough Tiered Multitenancy / Shards	
16–21 min Live Real Example / Demo Outline (Python + Qdrant + Embeddings)	
21–25 min Operational Considerations & Trade-offs Performance
25–28 min When Per-User Databases Might Still Make Sense Edge cases
28–30 min Conclusion & Key Recommendations / Q&A
results.notes:
# Prerequisites:

Basic familiarity with vector databases / embedding-based retrieval (or RAG pipelines)

General understanding of LLM-augmented search / retrieval-based generation

Exposure to Python but no deep database internals required

Curious to experiment/learn new things

results.title: CPython’s Memory Engine: Allocation, Deallocation, and Free-List Tricks
results.abstract: Deep dive into CPython’s memory engine: how objects are allocated, freed, recycled via optimizations, and what really changes when you run Python on mimalloc instead of pymalloc.
results.description:
This talk is a practical tour of how CPython really manages memory: from obj = SomeClass() to the moment its bytes are reused or released back to the OS.

We’ll start with the basics: object layout, refcounts, and how CPython’s small-object allocator (pymalloc) hands out memory in arenas and pools instead of calling malloc for every tiny object. You’ll see what actually happens when you create lots of ints, tuples, and short-lived objects in hot loops.

Then we’ll simplify the lifecycle on the way out: what happens when a refcount hits zero, how CPython runs type-specific deallocation code and optional finalizers, and how user __del__ fits into that chain. We’ll look at how memory is either returned to the allocator or kept on freelists to be reused quickly, and why this can look like a “leak” from the outside.

In the second half, we’ll focus on optimisations and allocator swaps. We’ll look at freelists for ints, tuples, frames, and other core objects: why they exist, how they speed up hot paths, and when they keep memory around longer than you expect. Then we’ll compare pymalloc with mimalloc: how they differ in fragmentation, multithread behaviour, what actually changes when you run Python with mimalloc, and how to measure the impact with simple benchmarks and tracemalloc-style tooling.

You’ll leave with a clear mental model of CPython’s (de)allocation pipeline, an intuition for what freelists and alternative allocators are really doing.
results.notes:
I prepared one slide to demonstrate what I want to show about the deallocation overview process and where free-lists happen:
https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/15aOiea8J1Q2fLtIIqH-AfujOUsV91PHiden9fR71ZGA/edit?slide=id.g39820cb09ab_0_0#slide=id.g39820cb09ab_0_0

results.title: Python-Powered  Smart Hydroponic Systems with IoT and Machine Learning
results.abstract: Want to see Python control real hardware, process sensor data, and make intelligent decisions—all in one project? This talk walks through building a complete smart hydroponic monitoring system from scratch using Python. We'll explore how Python interfaces with IoT sensors on Raspberry Pi, processes time-series data with pandas, deploys machine learning models for anomaly detection, and creates real-time dashboards with Streamlit. You'll learn practical patterns for Python-based IoT development, see actual code for hardware integration, and discover how Python's ecosystem makes building intelligent physical systems surprisingly accessible. Live demos and code samples included.
results.description:
Python isn't just for web apps and data analysis—it's a powerful tool for building intelligent IoT systems that interact with the physical world. This talk demonstrates how to build a complete smart hydroponic monitoring and control system using Python, showing developers practical techniques for integrating hardware, processing sensor data, and deploying machine learning models in production IoT environments.
What We'll Build Together
A fully functional hydroponic monitoring system that:

Reads real-time data from multiple sensors (pH, temperature, humidity, nutrient levels)
Processes and validates time-series data using pandas
Detects anomalies and predicts issues using scikit-learn
Controls actuators for automated system adjustments
Visualizes data in real-time with Streamlit dashboards
Stores data efficiently for historical analysis
results.notes:
This is a code-focused, live-demo talk that shows real implementations rather than abstract concepts. The presentation will alternate between:

Code walkthroughs - Key functions and patterns explained on screen
Live demonstrations - Actual hardware responding to Python code
Architecture diagrams - How components fit together
Performance metrics - Real data from deployed system

results.title: Leveraging Cognitive Science to Build Smarter AI with Python
results.abstract: Understanding how humans think, reason and make decisions is critical to building smarter AI systems. This talk explores how principles from cognitive science can be integrated into artificial intelligence models, enabling machines to simulate human-like decision-making, perception, and learning. By utilizing Python libraries like PyTorch and SciPy, this talk demonstrates how cognitive models can be applied to enhance AI systems and create smarter, more adaptive technologies.
results.description:
Cognitive science and artificial intelligence are inherently intertwined, yet often operate in separate domains. This talk will bridge the gap by showing how insights from cognitive psychology, decision theory, and neuroscience can be leveraged to improve AI systems. By simulating human-like thinking and decision-making, we can create AI models that better understand complex problems, learn from experience, and adapt to new information.

This talk covers - 
- Cognitive models - Understanding decision-making, perception, and reasoning models, such as ACT-R and SOAR.
- Python tools - Leveraging Python tools like NumPy, SciPy, and PyTorch to build models that simulate cognitive processes.
- Human-like decision-making - How cognitive biases, heuristics, and learning patterns can be used to improve AI reasoning and behavior.
- Practical applications - From robotics to decision support systems, how these cognitive-inspired models can be applied to real-world AI systems.

results.title: Building Production Grade Data Pipelines in Python
results.abstract:
Every data engineering project starts with a simple Python script. Maybe it pulls data from an API, cleans it up, and writes it out. It works the first time, and the second time. Then one day it does not. It breaks quietly. It corrupts a file. It fails at 2 am. There are no logs or backups. Suddenly, what felt like a tiny utility script becomes a critical pipeline. A small patch here and there will work for now until the loop continues. 

This tutorial walks through that journey step by step. We start with the kind of script most engineers have written and grow it into a well-designed pipeline. We clean up the structure, break work into components, handle errors, add retries and logging, manage configuration, and introduce automation. 
Finally, introduce an orchestration layer that naturally fits the pipeline scripts.

The goal is to get the fundamentals right before touching any fancy tools.
results.description:
## Audience
- Application developers who want a sneak peek at the Data engineering world
- Engineers currently rely on notebooks, cron, or manual scripts
- People who want to understand orchestration but feel overwhelmed by jumping straight into Airflow

No prior Airflow experience needed.

## What participants will learn
- How to design a Python-based data pipeline from the ground up
- How to refactor messy scripts into clean, modular, and testable pieces
- How to add reliability features such as retries, logging, and idempotency
- How to schedule and automate pipelines with Python
- When orchestration becomes necessary
- How to convert a working pipeline into a minimal Airflow DAG

## Workshop structure (3.5 hours)

- Build a simple ETL script in Python  (15 mins hands-on)
- Refactor into reusable components and isolate logic (10 mins + 20 mins hands-on)
- Introducing reliability. Logging. Retries. Idempotency (10 mins + 20 mins hands-on)
- Convert the pipeline into an Airflow DAG (30 mins)
- Wrap and final discussions (15 mins)


Participants will leave with a working pipeline and the confidence to extend it. They will understand orchestration by first understanding the problem.

## Requirements
Laptop with Python 3.12 or higher  
Sample data and starter code will be provided

results.title: When Python Enters the Graph: Practical Challenges of Using AWS Neptune for Risk Analytics
results.abstract: Building risk-scoring models on top of phonebook-derived customer relations data sounds straightforward—until Python meets a graph database at scale. This talk explores the real engineering challenges of integrating Python with **AWS Neptune** to build a client-relation graph originally used for credit scoring and now informing antifraud use cases. Attendees will learn what worked, what didn’t, how to avoid “SQL thinking” when writing Gremlin, and practical lessons from handling large datasets through Python.
results.description:
Graphs are powerful for modeling relationships in credit scoring, antifraud, and KYC—but integrating Python with **AWS Neptune** introduces challenges that most teams underestimate.

In this talk, I share our experience of building a phonebook-based client relation graph and connecting it to Python pipelines using **gremlinpython**. The focus is on the practical problems we faced, the mistakes we made, and the patterns that eventually worked.

### What the talk covers
**1. Why graphs for risk analytics**  
How graph-derived signals enhance credit scoring and naturally extend to antifraud and KYC.

**2. Python + Neptune in practice**  
- Bottlenecks and quirks in `gremlinpython`  
- Async execution pitfalls and latency issues  
- Avoiding unconstrained traversals that cause cluster-wide slowdowns  
- Structuring ingestion and inference code to stay safe and efficient

**3. Escaping the SQL mindset**  
How traditional relational thinking leads to poor Gremlin queries, and how to redesign your mental model around traversals, k-hop searches, and graph-native patterns.

**4. Lessons learned**  
Neptune is excellent for multi-hop exploration but less suited as a primary credit-scoring engine. These insights now guide our next-generation antifraud platform, where graphs play a more natural role.

### Key takeaways
Attendees will learn when a graph database is the right tool, how to write efficient Gremlin from Python, and how to avoid the most common performance pitfalls when bringing graph analytics to production.
results.notes:
- **Speaker background:**  
  I have 13 years of software development experience, with 6 years also involving team leadership and technical project management. I regularly conduct developer training, lead knowledge-sharing sessions, and present complex technical topics internally, though this will be my first major conference talk.

- **Relevance to PyCon Asia:**  
  Graph-based analytics are becoming increasingly important in risk, fraud, and financial compliance across Asia. Python is the de-facto tool for building these pipelines, but practical guidance on using it effectively with Neptune and Gremlin is scarce. This talk aims to close that gap using real production lessons from the Salmon Bank, operating in the Philippines.

results.title: AnvPy - Android Versatile Python
results.abstract: This poster presents AnvPy, an (all in one) Python based development environment designed for Android and IOS platforms. AnvPy enables users to create full featured apps and games with built-in support for UI design, AI/ML, and system-level functionalities without external servers. The project introduces a novel framework called Crysta UI for modern mobile app interfaces and includes modules for ads, peer-to-peer communication, access to internal settings and rapid APK conversion. This innovation aims to simplify mobile development for students, educators, and startups by making native Android app creation faster, offline-capable, and more accessible.
results.description:
AnvPy is an advanced, Python based integrated development environment (IDE) designed to revolutionize mobile app development. It enables users to create fully functional Android applications using Python, without depending on external servers or long build times.

The platform consists of multiple key components — AnvCore, Crysta UI, AnvStream, AnvAds, and Conversion Engine — each serving a unique purpose in the development workflow.

~ AnvCore provides backend functionalities for accessing Android’s internal settings and hardware such as Bluetooth, GPS, Wi-Fi, camera, and file system, enabling deep system-level integration.
~ Crysta UI offers a modern UI framework that allows developers to build beautiful, dynamic, and responsive mobile interfaces easily.
~ AnvStream enables peer-to-peer communication and WebRTC-based data transfer.
~ AnvAds supports in-app advertisement integration through AdMob.
~ Conversion Engine allows developers to instantly convert Python-based projects into Android APKs within seconds.

AnvPy’s offline-first approach and modular design make it a powerful and accessible tool for students, independent developers, and startups. It significantly reduces development time, simplifies complex tasks, and empowers creators to build modern apps and games with native performance using only Python.

results.title: Open Source Doesn’t Just Need Talks It Needs Action
results.abstract: At events, we’ve all been inspired by open source, but once we get home, the enthusiasm usually wanes. Open source requires action, not just more words. This session looks at doable, realistic strategies for assisting people in starting, maintaining, and expanding an active, open source community.
results.description:
Many people listen to motivational presentations about open source, but all too frequently, the enthusiasm wanes after the conference is over. Open source requires action in addition to awareness. With the larger objective of creating robust, sustainable communities, this talk aims to bridge the gap between being inspired by open source and actually contributing to it.We’ll examine the typical obstacles that prevent people from beginning and offer doable solutions to make participation easy, inviting, and fulfilling. This session focusses on practical actions that anyone can take, from assisting novices in submitting their first pull request to creating communities that value contributions outside of code, such as documentation, design, mentorship, and advocacy.

In addition to being inspired, attendees will leave with tactics for expanding their own communities, encouraging sustained participation, and transforming interest into worthwhile action that benefits the open source ecosystem as a whole.

results.title: From Silicon Fabs to AI Clusters: Inside the Hardware Future Behind Your Python Code
results.abstract: From sand and EUV scanners to AVX-512 and GPUs: a practical tour of how HighLoad Python actually runs on silicon—and how to use SIMD, Numba, and CuPy to turn hardware into real speedups.
results.description:
What does your Python code actually run on—physically? In this talk we’ll walk the path from sand to a data-center GPU, and then all the way up to your python3 prompt.

We’ll start with the silicon story: how ultra-pure crystals are grown, sliced into 300 mm wafers, and turned into chips through photolithography and EUV scanners (ASML, Carl Zeiss, TSMC fabs). Along the way we’ll touch real numbers: temperatures, purity, mask prices, wafer economics, and why a single wafer batch is measured in billions of dollars of output.

Next we zoom into a single transistor and build up: logic gates → functional units → IP blocks → CPU cores. You’ll see how instruction sets (ISA), micro-ops, branch prediction, caches, SMT/hyper-threading and thermal budgets shape the performance envelope your Python code is allowed to use—even for something as “simple” as int(1000) in a tight loop.

From there we compare CPU and GPU architectures: few complex cores vs many simple ones, latency-hiding vs bandwidth-driven design, and why GPUs love “many identical, simple tasks” while CPUs shine on complex control-flow.
Google Docs
 We’ll then drop into SIMD: AVX/SVE vector widths, how many numbers fit into a vector register, and where the memory wall starts to hurt—plus what that means for NumPy 2 and Numba in real code.

Finally, we connect it back to everyday HighLoad Python. Using a simple math kernel benchmark
out = sin(a*b + c) + sqrt(|a-b|) we’ll compare pure Python, Numba SIMD, and CuPy on GPU, and look at an applied Kaggle-style metric benchmark (Quadratic Weighted Kappa) to see how different libraries behave under pressure.

You’ll leave with an intuition for the silicon underneath your code and a concrete checklist for when to stay on CPU+SIMD and when to move to GPU from Python.
results.notes:
I prepare a presentation (not complited):
https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1OxJTm-dLZSBfIvcD_gLGjwO6LNzUYoZ-_t4WkHs4L5Y/edit?usp=sharing

results.title: Beyond the GIL in Practice with Vectorcall and Zero Copy Techniques in Modern Python
results.abstract: This talk is a deep dive into how CPython actually runs your code today and how to shape everyday Python so the runtime takes its fastest paths. We explore the specializing adaptive interpreter with inline caches, show when call sites become cheap through vectorcall, and demonstrate how careful data layout unlocks zero-copy movement across libraries. We also cover what changes when you use the 3.13 free-threaded build that disables the GIL and what to expect from the experimental JIT, with emphasis on code that delivers durable speedups under real profiling. Attendees learn to trigger the right specializations, design APIs that are inexpensive to invoke, and move data without serialization or needless copies, all verified with live measurements.
results.description:
We begin with an inside view of the specializing interpreter in 3.13. The runtime observes hot bytecodes and replaces them with specialized forms backed by compact inline caches. We translate this behavior into concrete guidance such as stabilizing object shapes for faster attribute access, avoiding megamorphic call sites that block specialization, and structuring predictable loops that the adaptive tier can learn quickly. Each point is validated with microbenchmarks and perf traces so the audience can see exactly when the interpreter flips from generic to specialized and why that matters.

Next we attack call overhead in modern CPython. We map the cheap paths for builtin and method calls that use vectorcall, explain how positional-only and fixed-arity signatures reduce argument handling cost, and show patterns for public APIs that keep callers on the fast route without sacrificing clarity or type hints. We apply these rules to common frameworks and measure the savings so the guidance is actionable rather than theoretical.

We then turn to object layout and attribute access. Shared-key dicts and slot-based layouts can cut memory and speed up field access when shapes are stable, while data-class configurations help when you want clarity with a compact layout. We compare read-heavy and write-heavy cases and call out where compact layouts hurt flexibility. The theme is that layout is a performance feature you can control with simple, explicit choices that survive code review.

Data movement is often the hidden bottleneck. We use the buffer protocol to hand off memory between libraries without serialization, build a pipeline that flows from reader to transformer to writer using views of the same region, and show how memoryview, array, and bytearray enable end-to-end zero-copy paths. We extend this to columnar blocks to demonstrate partial processing with backpressure while avoiding duplication, then confirm the gains with allocation and RSS measurements.

With the building blocks in place we assemble a realistic request path. A payload arrives, is validated and normalized, runs through a hot loop that specializes, calls a small set of stable library entry points, and hands results off using zero-copy views. We compare before and after profiles with wall time, CPU time, allocations, and cache hit rates, and we connect each improvement to the exact change that produced it.

Finally we step into 3.13’s new execution modes. We show what changes when running the experimental free-threaded build with the GIL disabled, where throughput gains are possible, where data races can appear, and how to write thread-safe code that scales across cores. We close with a quick look at the experimental JIT and how to decide whether it helps your specific workload, keeping expectations grounded in current release notes.
results.notes: This session targets advanced Python engineers and library authors who want performance wins based on how CPython 3.13 works today. All examples run on stock CPython 3.13, with optional runs on the free-threaded build and the experimental JIT for comparison.

results.title: Programming Lessons from Japanese Poetry
results.abstract:
Haiku is a traditional Japanese poetic form typically known for its two-part structure (juxtaposition), use of seasonal words (kigo), brevity, simplicity, and objectivity. Modern haiku is being practiced in other languages as well, including English. The structure of modern English haiku is slightly different from the traditional Japanese form, but it still adheres to those principles.

This talk provides a brief introduction to haiku, its poetic ideals, and how it resonates with some of the ideas presented in the Zen of Python. It aims to share with the audience lessons from haiku (such as minimalism in writing code and objectivity in collaboration), which are helpful not just in Python programming and software engineering design but also in how we approach problems in general.
results.description:
Haiku is a traditional Japanese poetic form typically known for its two-part structure (juxtaposition), use of seasonal words (kigo), brevity, simplicity, and objectivity. Modern haiku is being practiced in other languages as well, including English. The structure of modern English haiku is slightly different from the traditional Japanese form, but it still adheres to those principles.

This talk provides a brief introduction to haiku, its poetic ideals, and how it resonates with some of the ideas presented in the Zen of Python. It aims to share with the audience lessons from haiku (such as minimalism in writing code and objectivity in collaboration), which are helpful not just in Python programming and software engineering design but also in how we approach problems in general.
results.notes:
Outline of the talk

1. Introduction: 2 mins
•	How I got introduced to haiku and got inspired to explore the parallels between programming and haiku ideals. 

2. What is haiku? : 3 mins
•	Traditional haiku structure ( 5-7-5 syllables) and modern haiku forms.
•	Emphasis on capturing a single, vivid moment.
•	The importance of brevity and minimalism in haiku.

3. A few works of Japanese Masters: 3 mins
•	Highlight a few works of haiku masters (Basho, Buson, and Issa) to showcase haiku’s depth. 
•	Interpret one or two haiku to convey the haiku poetic ideal.

4.  Zen of Python in synergy with haiku poetics: 12 mins
In this section, I will mainly talk about six aphorisms that align with haiku poetics:  
•	Simple is better than complex. 
•	Sparse is better than dense 
•	Beautiful is better than ugly
•	Readability counts
•	Now is better than never
•	Although *never* is better than often right now 

And one aphorism which contrasts which haiku poetics
•	Explicit is better than implicit

6. Lessons learned: 5 mins
•	Minimalism: How haiku’s principle of brevity inspires concise and elegant code 
•	Simplicity
•	Wabi-Sabi or Embracing imperfection in programming ( or acknowledging trade-offs and embracing iterative development)
•	Lessons on communication: The most effective form of communication is the simplest and also code as a form of communication
•	Awareness: In software engineering context, understanding one's weakness, strengths and limitations, also understanding limitations of the system / team. Creating an environment inclusive and collaborative for others. 

7. Q/A: 5 mins

results.title: Why Python Matters in Basic Education: Building Thinkers, Not Just Coders
results.abstract: Across the past decade, education research has shifted from teaching code through strict syntax to building broad problem-solving skills. Python fits this shift well because it is clear, flexible, and beginner friendly. This session highlights global trends in computational thinking and explains why Python works as a foundation for K to 12 learners. It helps students focus on logic, structure, and creative problem solving, which strengthens digital literacy and prepares them for a tech-driven future.
results.description: Many classrooms still rely on heavy syntax drills that leave beginners frustrated. Research shows that students learn better when lessons center on thinking skills rather than memorizing rules. Python supports this approach because its clean design lets learners focus on reasoning. The session connects key findings in computational thinking research with practical examples, from block-based transitions to simple robotics and data tasks. Attendees will see how Python can help schools worldwide build confident problem solvers who can adapt to any digital tool they meet later on.

results.title: FastAPI Meets Django ORM: Build Modern APIs with Familiar Tools
results.abstract:
FastAPI is changing how Python developers build APIs—it's fast, modern, and async-ready. But what if you're coming from a Django background and want to keep using the ORM you love? Good news: you can!

In this hands-on session, we’ll explore how to integrate FastAPI with the Django ORM to build powerful, modern APIs while retaining Django’s robust database layer. Whether you’re looking to speed up your API response times, go async, or modernize your stack without abandoning your Django experience, this tutorial-style talk will walk you through everything step-by-step.

By the end, you'll know how to structure a hybrid FastAPI-Django app, connect the ORM, perform CRUD operations, and build clean, high-performance APIs that scale.
results.description: FastAPI has become one of Python’s most exciting frameworks for building modern, high-performance APIs. But for developers who love Django’s ORM and don’t want to give it up, there’s good news you can have both. In this talk, I’ll walk through how to use FastAPI with the Django ORM, step by step. We’ll explore how to connect Django’s ORM to a FastAPI project, manage database models, handle migrations, perform CRUD operations, and integrate authentication. You’ll leave with a clear understanding of how to combine FastAPI’s speed and flexibility with Django’s reliable database layer to build scalable, production-ready APIs.

results.title: Agentic System is the New Full Stack for Developers
results.abstract: Remember when "full-stack" meant learning frontend, backend, and databases? Today, agentic systems—software that can reason, plan, and act autonomously—represent the next fundamental shift every developer must understand. They're not replacing developers—they're becoming the new layer in our stack. This session demonstrates how to build production-ready agents using Python and your existing knowledge of Infrastructure as Code (IaC) and serverless architecture. You'll discover how concepts you already know—like event-driven functions, resource orchestration, and API composition—directly translate to agent development. We'll build Strands Agents that showcase real-world patterns: agents that can read documentation and generate code, coordinate multiple cloud services intelligently, and handle complex workflows that traditional scripts can't manage.  You'll leave with hands-on experience building agents, understanding of how your current skills accelerate agent development, and a clear path to integrate agentic capabilities into your existing applications. Just as full-stack developers had to evolve beyond simple CRUD apps, today's developers need to build systems that don't just execute—they think and adapt.
results.description: This talk describes what aspects Pythonist need to understand to build agentic system.

results.title: A Day in the Life of a Grade 10 Lasallian Entrepreneur: Integration of Python Website
results.abstract: This proposal presents a student-led brownie business created by a Grade 10 Lasallian entrepreneur and explains how basic Python programming was used to support and improve the project. As a beginner in Python, the student developed a simple website using Python tools to show the products, share information about the business, and make it easier for customers to connect. Python was also used to help with small tasks such as recording sales, tracking how many brownies were left, and organizing data in a clearer way. These basic programs helped reduce mistakes, saved time, and made the business more organized. This proposal shows that even beginner-level programming skills can be applied to real-life entrepreneurship, and that Python can help young students create more modern, efficient, and accessible small businesses.
results.description:
This project introduces a small brownie business created and managed by a Grade 10 Lasallian student, showing how basic Python programming skills can be used to support and improve a simple student-run venture. The business focuses on producing and selling brownies in different flavors while using technology to stay organized and efficient. As a beginner in Python, the student created a simple website using Python-based tools to present the products, share information about pricing and availability, and provide a more modern way for customers to learn about the business.

Python was also used to help with practical tasks that are often difficult to manage manually. These include keeping track of daily sales, monitoring how much stock is left, saving customer feedback, and organizing data into easy-to-read formats. By using straightforward Python scripts, the student was able to reduce errors, understand customer demand better, and make smarter decisions about production and planning.

Overall, this project shows how young entrepreneurs can use even the basics of Python to make their business more accessible, efficient, and professional. It demonstrates that coding is not only for advanced programmers. It can also be a helpful tool for beginners who want to improve their business ideas and bring them to life in a more modern way.

results.title: Planning Hockey Careers With Python
results.abstract: How can data science help young athletes navigate their careers? In this talk, I’ll share my experience building a career path planner for aspiring ice hockey players. The project combines player performance data, career path patterns, and predictive modeling to suggest possible development paths and milestones. Along the way, I’ll discuss the challenges of messy sports data and communicating insights in a way that resonates with non-technical users like coaches, parents, and players.
results.description:
## Objective

To share the process of building a data-driven career path planner for young hockey players, highlighting both the technical challenges and the broader impact of applying data science in real-world decision-making.

## Central Thesis 

Data science can provide valuable guidance in high-stakes, personal decision-making — but building tools for non-technical users requires more than just models. It demands thoughtful data cleaning, careful feature selection, and communication strategies that make insights accessible and actionable.

## Audience

This talk is for data scientists, analysts, and practitioners who are curious about applying machine learning to sports. It will appeal both to those interested in the technical process (data wrangling, predictive modeling) and those interested in building data products that impact real people.

## Tone / Type of Talk

Informative and light-hearted — grounded in practical data challenges, with a touch of storytelling from the hockey world. This is not a heavily mathematical talk at all, suitable for broader audience as well.

## Key Takeaways

- How to wrangle and model messy sports data for career planning use cases.
- How to design predictive models that communicate uncertainty and possible paths.
- Lessons on building data tools for non-technical stakeholders.
- Inspiration for applying data science to creative and impactful real-world problems.

## Background Knowledge Expected

No prior knowledge of hockey is required — we’ll keep the sports side light and fun.
results.notes:
## Outline

- (2.5 minutes) Intro: Why hockey (and why career planning matters in sports)
- (5 minutes) Data collection & wrangling: turning messy, incomplete performance records into usable data
- (10 minutes) Modeling progression: techniques to identify likely career trajectories and milestones
- (5 minutes) Communicating results: designing tools that resonate with non-technical stakeholders (players, coaches, parents)
- (5 minutes) Lessons learned: challenges, surprises, and what translates to other domains beyond sports
- (2.5 minutes) Q&A

results.title: FinSight AI: The Conversation That Decodes Wall Street
results.abstract: FinSight AI is an agentic financial intelligence system that transforms real-time market data into human-readable insights through natural conversation. Designed to simplify the complexity of financial analysis, it leverages multi-agent reasoning to retrieve, interpret, and contextualize stock performance, trends, and risks. By bridging quantitative analytics with intuitive dialogue, FinSight AI empowers users whether investors, analysts, or students to make informed decisions with confidence and clarity.
results.description:
FinSight AI reimagines how people interact with financial data. Instead of sifting through charts and reports, users can ask plain-language questions like “Why is NVIDIA stock up today?” or “Compare Apple and Tesla’s fundamentals.” The system’s AI agents collaboratively analyze live market information, sentiment data, and historical context to generate clear, concise, and actionable explanations.

Built with accessibility and transparency at its core, FinSight AI makes institutional-grade analysis understandable for everyone. It’s not just a dashboard, it’s a conversation that decodes Wall Street, bringing financial literacy and intelligent investing to the next generation of market participants.

results.title: Tracking Planes and Snapping Photos with Python Like a True Engineer
results.abstract: Ever wondered what happens when you mix Python, Raspberry Pi, and a dash of radio wizardry?, in this talk we'll explore an unusual project, track planes in real-time and capture their photos using Python, Raspberry Pi, and SDR. Learn how to combine aviation and python into this unusual yet fun project, special for for pythonista plane enthusiast!, i mean who doesn't love plane, right?
results.description: Ever wondered what happens when you mix **Python** and **radio wizardry**? In this talk, we’ll explore an unusual yet fun project: **tracking airplanes in real-time and capturing their photos**, just like a true engineer (or at least, a very enthusiastic plane spotter). ## What we’ll cover ### ✈️ Basics of **aviation surveillance** and why ADS-B matters. - 📡 How **Software Defined Radio (SDR)** can unlock live aircraft data. generic SDR interface (Airspy SDR#) - device : these are the device used for SDR. in my case, i used the cheap RTL-SDR dongle (the blue ones). - 📡 what is **Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast (ADS-B)**. Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast Network ### 🔄 Understanding rotation concepts like **yaw, pitch, and roll**. - this concept is important to understand, because itll help us better understanding how the orientation used in this system (almost all system uses this!). The six degrees of freedom: forward/back, up/down, left/right, pitch, yaw, roll, and the secon image is The position of all three axes, with the right-hand rule for describing the angle of its rotations. the easiest way to understand this is to imagine it with your head like this. ### 🌍 Turning **coordinates into angles**. - we'll use haversine and many formula to calculate the angles where we point our camera, in a geospherecial plane. ### 🛠️ Building a setup with **Raspberry Pi** (and ESP32 cameo). the servo is used to align the camera, ### 🖼️ Automating **plane photo-snapping** in sync with live tracking. ### 📈 adding **machine learning** to spice it up! (to optimize it) ## Why this matters - 🏠️ for **plane enthusiasts** that doesn't wanna miss any planes that are passing by 👀. - 🏝️ **Remote surveillance**: useful even in areas without internet. - 🏭 Insights that could benefit **aircraft manufacturers** and researchers 🤑. ## Looking ahead Imagine extending this with : - 🔭 Telephoto lenses - 🌙 Night vision or thermal imaging - 🤖 AI for plane detection and classification ## Audience takeaway If you love Python, hardware, or simply planes, this talk is for you. ✈️ + 🐍 = Awesome! 💡 Bring your curiosity. results.title: Sssstainable Python: Code That Doesn’t Byte the Planet
results.abstract:
"Sssstainable Python: Code That Doesn’t Byte the Planet" isn’t just about writing better code—it’s about becoming a better developer. In this talk, we’ll explore how sustainable programming practices in Python can fuel your personal growth, boost your confidence, and help you build a career that lasts.
By focusing on clean, maintainable, and efficient code, you’ll not only reduce bugs and burnout but also improve your ability to collaborate, mentor, and lead. We’ll cover practical techniques that make your code easier to understand and evolve—skills that translate directly into stronger communication, problem-solving, and long-term impact.
Whether you're early in your journey or looking to level up, this session will show how sustainable coding habits can shape you into a more thoughtful, resilient, and future-ready developer. Bonus: you’ll leave with a few laughs, a checklist for sustainable coding, and a renewed sense of purpose in your craft.
results.description:
Sssstainable Python: Code That Doesn’t Byte the Planet is a lighthearted yet impactful dive into the world of sustainable software development—Python style. As developers, we often focus on shipping fast, but what happens when our code becomes a tangled mess of inefficiency, waste, and tech debt?
In this session, we’ll explore how to write Python code that’s not only clean and maintainable but also mindful of performance and resource usage. You’ll learn practical strategies for reducing computational overhead, designing scalable systems, and refactoring without fear. We’ll also touch on how sustainable coding can contribute to greener tech practices—because yes, even your code can help save the planet.
Expect a mix of humor, real-world examples, and actionable takeaways that will leave you inspired to write code that lasts longer than your favorite coffee mug. Whether you're a seasoned developer or just starting out, this talk will help you charm the snake and tame your codebase—sustainably.
results.notes:
"Sssstainable Python: Code That Doesn’t Byte the Planet" is an insightful session that connects clean coding practices with personal development. In this talk, we’ll explore how adopting sustainable programming habits—like writing readable code, refactoring with intention, and designing for long-term maintainability—can sharpen your problem-solving skills, boost your confidence, and make you a more effective collaborator.
You’ll learn how thoughtful coding leads to better communication, smoother teamwork, and a stronger sense of ownership in your projects. Whether you're early in your career or looking to refine your craft, this session will leave you with practical strategies and a fresh perspective on how sustainable code can support your growth, reduce burnout, and help you thrive in any development environment.

results.title: Weaponizing MCP Servers: Production-Ready AI Agent Infrastructure with Python
results.abstract:
Most MCP servers break under real-world load. This talk reveals the Python patterns that transform basic implementations into production-ready systems handling serious workloads.
We'll explore advanced async techniques, intelligent error handling, and performance optimization that separate prototypes from scalable MCP infrastructure. Through live examples, we'll build servers that handle concurrent requests, implement smart caching, and recover from failures gracefully.
You'll discover practical patterns for connection management, memory optimization, and horizontal scaling that apply to any high-throughput Python system. This isn't about building your first MCP server it's about engineering reliable, fast infrastructure ready for production.
Perfect for Python developers tackling AI agent
results.description:
Most MCP server tutorials show you how to build basic examples, but they don't prepare you for what happens when real users start hitting your servers. This talk bridges that gap, showing you the Python patterns and architectural decisions that make MCP servers production-ready.

We'll start by examining common failure points - why MCP servers crash under load, leak memory, or become unresponsive. Then we'll dive into the practical solutions: async patterns that actually work at scale, error handling that prevents cascading failures, and performance optimizations that keep your servers responsive.

### Core Topics Covered

**Production-Ready Async Patterns:** Moving beyond basic asyncio to patterns that handle real-world complexity - connection pooling, request queuing, and resource management that prevents your MCP server from becoming a bottleneck.

**Smart Error Handling:** Implementing retry logic, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation that keeps your MCP servers running even when dependencies fail. We'll explore Python-specific techniques for handling partial failures and maintaining system stability.

**Performance and Memory Optimization:** Practical techniques for profiling MCP servers, identifying bottlenecks, and optimizing for sustained load. Learn how to prevent memory leaks and tune garbage collection for long-running MCP processes.

**Scaling Strategies:** Patterns for horizontal scaling, load balancing, and state management that let you grow your MCP infrastructure as demand increases. We'll examine real-world architectures that handle thousands of daily requests.

**Security and Monitoring:** Implementing request validation, resource sandboxing, and comprehensive logging that gives you visibility into your MCP server's behavior and protects against malicious requests.

### Real-World Case Studies

Through detailed examples, we'll examine MCP servers powering actual applications - from data processing workflows to API integration services. You'll see the specific challenges each faced and the Python solutions that solved them.

### Live Debugging Session

We'll debug a failing MCP server in real-time, showing you how to identify problems, implement fixes, and verify improvements using Python profiling and monitoring tools.

This talk is designed for Python developers who want to move beyond toy examples and build MCP infrastructure that can handle real workloads. You'll leave with battle-tested patterns, practical debugging techniques, and a clear roadmap for scaling your MCP servers from prototype to production.

## Talk Outline (30 Minutes Total)

### Opening: When MCP Servers Break (3 minutes)

- **Live failure demo:**
- **The Reality Gap:** Why tutorial MCP servers don't survive real usage
- **Common Failure Patterns:** Memory leaks, connection exhaustion, unhandled errors
- **Practical patterns that solve these problems** 

### Part 1: Async Patterns That Actually Work (8 minutes)

#### Beyond Basic AsyncIO
- **Connection Management:** Building connection pools that don't leak resources
- **Request Queuing:** Handling concurrent agent requests without blocking
- **Resource Limits:** Using semaphores and guards to prevent resource exhaustion

#### Code Deep-Dive
```python
# Robust async patterns for stable MCP servers
# Connection pooling with proper cleanup
# Request batching and intelligent queuing
```

#### Performance Improvements
- **Before/After Metrics:** Response times and memory usage comparison
- **Profiling Tools:** Using Python tools to identify async bottlenecks

### Part 2: Error Handling and Recovery (7 minutes)

#### Smart Retry Patterns
- **Circuit Breaker Implementation:** Preventing cascading failures in MCP networks
- **Exponential Backoff:** Intelligent retry strategies that don't overwhelm failing services
- **Graceful Degradation:** Keeping MCP servers functional when dependencies fail

#### Memory Management
- **Leak Detection:** Identifying and fixing memory leaks in long-running MCP processes
- **Resource Cleanup:** Proper async context management and resource disposal
- **GC Optimization:** Tuning garbage collection for stable performance

#### Live Debugging Session
- **Real Problem Solving:** Debugging a failing MCP server with Python profiling tools
- **Monitoring Integration:** Adding observability to MCP servers for production visibility

### Part 3: Scaling and Architecture Patterns (8 minutes)

#### Horizontal Scaling Strategies
- **Load Balancing:** Distributing agent requests across multiple MCP server instances
- **State Management:** Handling session data and shared resources across servers
- **Health Monitoring:** Implementing health checks and automatic failover

#### Case Study: Real-World MCP Infrastructure
- **The Challenge:** Scaling from prototype to handling thousands of daily requests
- **Architecture:** Multi-server setup with load balancing and monitoring
- **Lessons Learned:** What worked, what failed, and key architectural decisions

#### Security and Validation
- **Input Sanitization:** Protecting MCP servers from malicious agent requests
- **Resource Sandboxing:** Limiting agent access to system resources safely
- **Audit Logging:** Tracking agent actions for debugging and compliance

### Part 4: Real Applications and Next Steps (3 minutes)

#### Production Examples
- **Data Processing Pipeline:** MCP server handling batch processing requests
- **API Integration Service:** Managing external API calls for AI agents
- **File Management System:** Secure file operations with proper access controls

#### Immediate Action Items

- **Implementation Roadmap:** Which patterns to implement first for maximum impact

### Closing: Your MCP Production Journey (1 minute)

results.title: Inside ASGI: The Engine Behind Modern Python Web Frameworks
results.abstract:
Most of today’s popular Python web frameworks like **FastAPI**, **Starlette**, and **Django Channels** are built on a powerful idea called the **ASGI protocol**. It’s what makes them fast, concurrent, and capable of handling everything from simple *HTTP* requests to real-time *WebSockets*.

In this talk, we’ll take a deep dive under the surface to see how *ASGI* really works, from **event loops** and **async I/O** to **ASGI scopes**, **message passing**, and **lifespan events**. We’ll also look at how *ASGI* connects web servers like **Uvicorn** with frameworks like **FastAPI**, forming the bridge that keeps the entire async ecosystem running smoothly.

By the end of the session, you’ll not only understand how *ASGI* powers modern web frameworks, but also gain the insight that will empower you to debug better, extend confidently, and even build your own.
results.description:
Over the years, Python’s web ecosystem has evolved from simple, thread-based *WSGI* apps to powerful, asynchronous frameworks built on top of the **ASGI protocol**. ASGI changed the game introducing true concurrency, *WebSocket* support, and the ability to scale *Python* apps far beyond what *WSGI* could handle.

In this talk, we’ll take a clear, step-by-step look under the abstractions to see how *ASGI* actually works. You’ll learn how it acts as the bridge between web servers like **Uvicorn** or **Hypercorn** and frameworks such as **Starlette** and **FastAPI**.

We will explore:

* How **ASGI scopes** represent *HTTP* and *WebSocket* connections.
* How **async message passing** with `receive` and `send` enables non-blocking communication.
* How frameworks like **Starlette** build on *ASGI* to implement **routing**, **middleware**, and **lifespan events**.
* And how *ASGI*’s design makes modern frameworks scalable, flexible, and ready for real-time workloads.
results.notes:
### Talk Outline

* **Introduction and Motivation**: Explain why ASGI was introduced to replace WSGI and how it enabled the rise of modern async frameworks in Python.
* **The Big Picture**: Break down the full ASGI stack and show how data flows through the event loop, server, application, and back as a response.
* **ASGI Protocol Internals**: Dive into the core components: `scopes`, `receive` and `send` call pattern, and how HTTP and WebSocket messages are handled.
* **Framework Deep Dive**: Explore how Starlette uses ASGI to implement routing, middleware, and request handling, and how FastAPI builds on it with validation and dependency injection.
* **The Power of Async I/O**: Discuss concurrency, event loop scheduling, and how ASGI achieves scalability and non-blocking performance in real-world systems.
* **Wrap-up and Takeaways**: Summarize how ASGI powers the modern Python web frameworks and how understanding it helps you build faster, more efficient backends.

results.title: The Automated Arsenal: Building Comprehensive Vulnerability Assessment Multiple CVE Scanner
results.abstract: In the ever-evolving landscape of cybersecurity, timely identification of vulnerabilities is crucial to safeguarding critical infrastructure. The paper presents the development of an automated vulnerability scanning tool capable of assessing multiple CVEs across various platforms. The tool integrates multiple scanners for a wide array of vulnerabilities, including critical threats like CVE-2024-47176 (CUPS), CVE-2020-0796 (SMBGhost), CVE-2022-3590 (WordPress), and many others. By leveraging concurrent multi-threaded scanning techniques and offering customizable timeout configurations, the system provides fast and comprehensive vulnerability assessments. The tool is designed to be extensible, allowing for easy integration of new CVEs and customized scanning methods for specific environments. Additionally, the system features a graphical user interface (GUI) for simplified configuration and monitoring, providing users with an intuitive and effective vulnerability management solution. This system’s unique capability lies in its scalability and flexibility, making it suitable for both large enterprise networks and individual cybersecurity practitioners. The paper also explores its application in real-world scenarios, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting vulnerabilities and assisting in proactive remediation.
results.description:
In today’s rapidly evolving cybersecurity landscape, organizations face an increasing number of threats from known vulnerabilities. These vulnerabilities, often identified through the Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) database, are crucial points of attack that can be exploited by cybercriminals if left unchecked. To address this, vulnerability scanners are widely used to detect and assess these weaknesses in an organization’s infrastructure.
However, traditional vulnerability scanners typically focus on detecting individual CVEs or vulnerabilities specific to a given service or platform. While effective, these scanners can be time-consuming and limited in scope, especially when dealing with large-scale environments or multiple CVEs across different systems.
This work presents "The Automated Arsenal", a comprehensive, automated tool designed to address these limitations by enabling the simultaneous scanning of multiple CVEs across diverse platforms and services. The tool incorporates multiple scanners, each specialized to detect vulnerabilities in specific services, and is designed to be highly scalable, allowing users to quickly assess their infrastructure for known vulnerabilities.

Key Features of the Tool:
Multi-CVE Scanning: Unlike traditional scanners that target a single CVE or service, this tool can scan for multiple CVEs concurrently, providing a more comprehensive assessment of a system’s security posture.
Modular Design: The tool is built with a modular architecture, where each scanner is responsible for detecting vulnerabilities in a specific service (e.g., SMB, WordPress, CUPS). This modularity allows for easy integration of new CVE scanners as new vulnerabilities are discovered.
Scalability: The scanner supports configurations that allow it to scale from small networks to large enterprise environments. Users can customize thread counts, timeouts, and target configurations (single targets, IP ranges, or file lists) to optimize scanning speed and resource usage.
User-Friendly Interface: The tool provides a graphical user interface (GUI) that simplifies the configuration of scanning parameters, making it accessible for both novice and experienced cybersecurity professionals. Users can easily select which scanners to run, configure scan settings, and track scan progress in real-time.
Comprehensive Reporting: After the scan, the tool generates a detailed HTML report summarizing the findings, including the risk levels of vulnerabilities (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Info), and visualizations (pie charts and bar graphs) to help users easily interpret the data and prioritize remediation efforts.
Real-Time Monitoring and Logging: The GUI displays real-time logs and progress updates during the scan, allowing users to monitor the scan’s status and intervene if necessary. This feature helps users understand the ongoing scan's current state and avoid time-consuming errors.

Use Cases:
Enterprise Security Assessments: The tool can be employed by organizations to scan their network, identify weaknesses in services like SMB, WordPress, and CUPS, and prioritize patching efforts for high-risk vulnerabilities.
Web Application Security: Security professionals can use this tool to detect vulnerabilities in widely used web platforms, such as WordPress, helping website administrators maintain secure environments.
Penetration Testing: Ethical hackers and penetration testers can use the tool to assess client systems for known vulnerabilities, providing an automated solution that saves time while increasing detection coverage.
Compliance Audits: For organizations that need to meet specific compliance standards (e.g., PCI-DSS, GDPR), the tool can be used to ensure systems are free from critical vulnerabilities that could lead to non-compliance.

Innovation:
The main innovation behind The Automated Arsenal lies in its ability to scan for multiple vulnerabilities simultaneously across a broad range of platforms and services. The multi-scanner approach enhances efficiency, enabling security teams to conduct more thorough assessments in less time. The modular nature of the tool allows for easy integration of future CVE scanners, ensuring that it remains relevant as new vulnerabilities emerge.
By combining speed, scalability, and usability, this tool provides an efficient, automated solution to the ongoing challenge of vulnerability management, making it a valuable asset for any cybersecurity professional or organization seeking to protect its infrastructure from known threats.

results.title: Your code in the cloud: Using Python as a DevOps tool on AWS
results.abstract: This talk explores how Python can serve as a powerful ally in cloud resource management and automation using Amazon Web Services (AWS). Attendees will learn how to leverage Boto3 (the official AWS SDK for Python) to interact programmatically with cloud services, automate infrastructure operations, and build scalable solutions. Through examples, we will cover essential concepts such as instance management, storage automation, and Python’s role within DevOps workflows. This session is designed for developers, DevOps professionals, data engineers, and anyone interested in expanding their cloud computing skills with Python.
results.description:
Python has become one of the most versatile languages in today’s tech ecosystem, and its integration with the cloud unlocks a world of possibilities. In this talk, we will explore how to use Python to manage and automate resources in Amazon Web Services (AWS) through Boto3, the official AWS SDK for Python.

We’ll dive into practical examples that demonstrate how to create and manage instances, handle storage, automate repetitive tasks, and optimize workflows in DevOps environments. The session will also cover best practices and recommendations for building scalable and secure cloud-based solutions.

Perfect for developers, DevOps engineers, data engineers, and cloud enthusiasts, this talk offers a clear and hands-on perspective on how Python can transform the way we work with infrastructure on AWS.

results.title: Tying Up Loose Threads: Making your Project No-GIL Ready
results.abstract: If you, a project maintainer, want to support the free-threaded interpreter, but are unsure how, then this is for you! I'll show why ditching the GIL will yield extra performance, tips on making your compiled extensions compatible with the new ABI, and catching bugs as part of your automated workflows.
results.description:
If you messed around with Python's command line options or read the official documentation, you might wonder what the -Xgil option or the PYTHON_GIL environment variable did to your scripts, and whether setting either affects performance. The hubbub on popular wheels such as pyo3, python-zstandard, numpy, uv, cffi, and cython supporting the free-threaded interpreter is no passing fad either. For Pythonistas that don't read PEPs in their spare time or contribute to the cpython project itself, an adventure that delves into a less known, yet jaw-dropping aspect of Python awaits!

Python's Global Interpreter Lock, which determines which single thread can execute native Python code and call C API functions, simplifies writing multithreaded code.  However, sticking with this execution model leaves out extra performance afforded by modern multicore CPUs with hyperthreading, as automatic locking and unlocking of the GIL does not scale well with thread counts, especially in performance-sensitive workloads.

The newfangled free-threaded interpreter promises salvation when running either pure Python code or with compiled extensions. General multithreading rules apply (prefer thread-local variables, using locks to prevent simultaneous access of shared data), but when dealing with projects containing compiled extensions that directly or indirectly interface with Python's C API, more porting rules also apply. 

 Key porting tips, including projects using the Limited API, include: port native code away from C API functions that avoid borrowed references because they aren't thread-safe; modify unit tests to catch concurrency bugs  arising from assuming the presence of the GIL; and extend CI coverage of Python interpreters both for testing and to build free-threaded compatible wheels.
results.notes:
https://discuss.python.org/t/pep-779-criteria-for-supported-status-for-free-threaded-python/84319/123
https://peps.python.org/pep-0703/
https://peps.python.org/pep-0779/

results.title: From Instant Answers to Thinking Partner: Building Socratic AI in Python
results.abstract:
Humans have evolved over the centuries developing critical thinking skills which fueled many eureka moments. But as we rely more on AI assistants for instant answers, our ability to think critically has taken a hit. So can we use AI to think more deeply instead of less?
This talk demonstrates how to build a Socratic-style chatbot in Python using LangChain and LangGraph that guides the user through questioning rather than providing quick answers. We will explore the system architecture, conversational flow, prompt design and techniques to keep the users engaged and think critically.
results.description:
We live in an age of instant gratification and today's AI assistants reflect that providing us with quick answers to our queries. But this process of providing instant answers is not ideal while learning new concepts, as it does not engage the user to think critically about the problem.

Drawing inspiration from the Socratic style, in this session we will design a bot that encourages learners to engage with it to reason, debug and reflect instead of blindly copying the answers it gives. This can be achieved by building a Socratic style chatbot using LangChain and LangGraph. The bot asks the right questions to help the user think critically. 

Through this talk we will explore the following:
1) The system architecture for a stateful Socratic agent
2) How to design conversational flows with LangChain and LangGraph to encourage critical thinking.
3) Prompt engineering techniques to build an engaging AI thinking partner.
4) Having strong Evals using Ragas that evaluates the LLMs response 

My aim is to show how we can transform AI from being a teaching assistant to a thinking partner that helps the users to build understanding rather than dependence.

results.title: Creating Presentation Slides with the Retro Game Engine Pyxel
results.abstract: Pyxel is a retro game engine developed by kitao-san, which is attractive because of its ability to create NES-like retro games in Python, its simple and intuitive API, and its expressive power limited to 16 colors and 4 sounds. On top of this strong Pyxel limitation, we created a presence-slide viewer that requires expressive power. We also incorporated an interesting mechanism using WebSocket communication. In this talk, I will show how we implemented the technology from loading Markdown to displaying slides, with a demonstration.
results.description:
Combining existing libraries to achieve a goal within constraints is an everyday endeavor in modern programming.
In this talk, I will give you tips on how to deal with such efforts and details on how we specifically combined each library to create the presentation slides.

Agenda

- Introduction to Pyxel, a retro game engine (3min)
- Demonstration of Pyxel presentation in action (2min)
- Tokenize in markdown-it and output in visitor pattern (2min)
- Rendering in Pyxel, character display in BDF font (2min)
- Code highlighting in Pygments (2min)
- Browser behavior with Pyodide + micropip (4min)
- Multi-person operation via WebSocket communication (5min)

Pyxel: https://github.com/kitao/pyxel

This is a technical introduction to Pyxel Slide, implemented for PyCon mini Shizuoka 2024 Continue (Feb, 2025).
