results.title: High-Performance Web Application Development in the Python 3.14 Era
results.abstract: Python 3.14 introduces important performance improvements, including refined execution paths, enhanced profiling and debugging tools, and more mature support for free-threaded mode beyond the traditional GIL. This talk examines how these changes influence real production behavior through ISUCON, a competition that pushes web applications to extreme load.
results.description:
1. Introduction (2 minutes)

   * Why Python performance matters in 2026
   * Python 3.14 and ISUCON as the context

2. Key changes in Python 3.14 (5 minutes)

   * Faster interpreter and bytecode execution
   * New profiling and debugging capabilities
   * Incremental GC and memory behavior
   * Free threaded mode and the remaining role of the GIL

3. ISUCON style load model (3 minutes)

   * High traffic with mixed CPU and I O pressure
   * Database contention and latency constraints
   * Why these conditions expose real bottlenecks

4. Profiling under load (5 minutes)

   * Using Python 3.14 profiling tools
   * Detecting opcode level hot paths
   * Identifying I O stalls with async introspection
   * Observing GC behavior under stress

5. Effective optimization techniques (10 minutes)

   * Choosing between threads and async
   * Reducing database load with caching and batching
   * Improving serialization and request handling
   * Eliminating N plus 1 queries
   * Before and after performance measurements

6. Practical guidance for 2026 (3 minutes)

   * Updated design principles for concurrency and memory
   * What Python 3.14 improves and what it does not

7. Q and A (2 minutes)

results.title: A reliable development/release workflow for open source Python libraries
results.abstract: This talk provides a complete blueprint for architecting a development and release workflow specifically for open source Python libraries published to PyPI. We move beyond basic test-build-deploy workflows to address more topics necessary for developing and maintaining trusted libraries: solid test-build strategies, changelog management, handling untrusted contributions, managing feature branch preview builds, automated semantic versioning, documentation generation, ensuring package quality, and securing the release process and final distribution.
results.description:
This presentation delivers a comprehensive guide to construct a reliable development/release workflow for open source Python libraries that may require more things than a basic CI/CD pipeline for internal/private apps.

Key topics include:
- Solid test and build strategy: Implementing robust testing and build processes that ensure package quality and reliability on diverse environments with idempotent builds.
- Change management: Techniques for implementing automated semantic versioning (SemVer) and generating accurate changelogs.
- Security in open source: Designing CI systems to safely handle untrusted contributions from external pull requests and mitigating risks inherent in open source collaboration. Security is also emphasized in the release process to ensure the integrity of the distributed packages.
- Documentation: Automating the generation and deployment of comprehensive documentation.
- Developer expertise: Setting up contributor-friendly workflows. A good developer experience enables high-quality achievements of above goals.

results.title: Depths of Python: An Advanced Python Workshop
results.abstract:
This workshop aims to illuminate Python's beautiful features, which become apparent only when building complex interfaces or meta systems. This workshop is designed for beginners and students who know how to write plain scripts but struggle with larger programs and libraries.

Unlike other workshops, we will draw on real-life examples and deconstruct them, building intuition in the process. For example, to learn decorators, the best example is a logger, but a real-life example is `@app.get` from Flask.

By the end of the workshop, participants will gain an intuitive understanding and learn to use advanced Python features, including decorators, context managers, and metaclasses. Etc.
results.description:
The workshop starts with a code sample, a simple FastAPI app. From there, we start asking questions. what does `app = FastAPI()` do? What does `@app.route` do?  Slowly, we unwrap features of Python in the following order

1. Intro + Understanding existing libraries (fastapi, pytest) (30 mins)
2. Python's Data Model (30 mins)
3. Memory References (30 mins)
4. Decorators (30 mins)

By the end of the workshop, attendees will understand Python's features, their functions, and how to apply them in real-world examples.

results.title: Democratize serverless web apps for Python devs (with AI)
results.abstract:
Once you have a Python script for your business logic e.g. AI models, you may want to (1) serve it as a web app to your users.
In some situations, you may also want to (2) make it serverless for reasons such as cost, scalability, security, performance, and offline-first.

This talk will introduce you to the Python ecosystem that helps you build serverless web apps, with or without AI models. You can publish your ideas as a web app without any frontend coding.

For (1), you can use pure-Python web UI frameworks such as Streamlit and Gradio, with any Python libraries for the backend logic. With these frameworks, you can get an interactive web app with a nice UI.
For (2), I'd like to introduce serverless versions of such frameworks such as Stlite and Gradio-Lite. With these, you can serve the Python app as a serverless web app/static web page.

In this talk, we will walk through the development of such serverless web apps using only Python.
results.description:
1. Introduction: needs for pure-Python web app development and serverless web apps (3 mins)
2. Pure-Python web UI frameworks (5 mins)
3. Walkthrough: development of pure-Python web apps (5 mins)
3. Serverless versions of these frameworks (5 mins)
4. Walkthrough: development of pure-Python serverless web apps (5 mins)
5. Discussions: pros and cons of such technologies, and future directions (2 mins)
6. Q&A (5 mins)

results.title: Inside CPython: A Guided Tour of the Interpreter’s Core
results.abstract:
Most of us write Python every day, but very few of us ever look under the hood to see how CPython actually runs our code. The interpreter quietly handles everything parsing your script, generating bytecode, managing memory, juggling threads and unless something goes wrong, we rarely think about what’s happening behind the scenes. But once you understand how CPython really works, a lot of Python’s “mysteries” suddenly make sense: performance quirks, memory behavior, async vs threads, and even why certain bugs happen.

In this talk, I’ll walk through the internals of CPython in a friendly, approachable way. We’ll start from the moment you run a .py file and follow the journey all the way through the compiler pipeline (AST → bytecode), the evaluation loop, stack frames, reference counting, and garbage collection. Along the way, I’ll use simple code examples, dis, and a few snippets from the CPython source to show what’s actually happening at each step.

We’ll also look at what’s new in modern Python versions: the adaptive interpreter, faster opcodes, the revamped bytecode format, and the ongoing work toward a free threaded, no GIL CPython.

By the end, you’ll have a much clearer picture of how Python executes your code, makes performance decisions, manages memory, and handles concurrency. Whether you build microservices, async systems, or performance critical tools, understanding CPython’s core will help you write faster, safer, and more predictable Python.
results.description:
Most Python developers use CPython every day without ever seeing what actually happens beneath the surface. We write functions, import modules, catch exceptions, and run async code but very little of that makes sense until you understand the internals of the interpreter itself.

This talk is a guided, beginner-friendly tour of CPython’s core: how your Python code is parsed, compiled, optimized, and finally executed inside the virtual machine. We’ll follow the full journey of a Python program, starting from source code and moving through the tokenizer, parser, AST, bytecode compiler, and into the evaluation loop that drives the CPython VM.

Along the way, we’ll look at how CPython manages memory using reference counting, how the garbage collector handles cycles, why Python behaves the way it does with threads and the GIL, and how modern releases (Python 3.11–3.13) have made the interpreter significantly faster using adaptive bytecode, inline caches, and execution fast-paths.

This talk aims to demystify Python’s internals and make them approachable even if you’ve never read a line of C. By the end, you’ll have a clearer mental model of how CPython works, why certain coding patterns are faster than others, and how the interpreter’s design influences your application’s performance, memory usage, and concurrency behavior.

Whether you build web services, work with async systems, or just love Python, understanding CPython’s core will make you a stronger engineer.

results.title: Unpack Polars- The Turbo Engine Beyond pandas
results.abstract: This talk will introduce Polars, a library promising speed over pandas. While this talk cover introduction of Polars, it will also unpack it and will talk about Arrow + Rust and how these two can conquer over pandas.
results.description:
Polars is new data processing library but not much adopted. Reasons could be the doubts over the performance, a laziness to switch to a different library or a trust over the pandas more than polars.
This talk will emphasise on key components of Polars: Rust and Arrow. And will explain what these two offer and make Polars a essential library to switch to.
This talk will give a solid reasons to the developers to adopt Polars over Pandas.
Sometimes an architectural understanding is better than the demo. A demo can show a performance over a data set, while architecture makes you believe that this performance will always be there.
This is why, this talk is very much essential to those who fear switching to polars and still using pandas.

results.title: Python + SQLAlchemy: Your First ORM Adventure
results.abstract: Databases are a core part of almost every Python application. Writing raw SQL can be repetitive and error-prone. This workshop introduces SQLAlchemy ORM, a Python library that allows you to interact with databases using Python classes.
results.description:
Through a hands-on mini-project, you’ll learn how to define models, create tables, insert and query data, and understand the basics of relationships—all in a simple, approachable way.

Learning Objectives / Takeaways:

Understand the purpose of ORM and when to use it.

Create basic models and tables using SQLAlchemy.

Insert, query, update, and delete data using ORM methods.

Understand simple one-to-many relationships.

Build a small Python project (e.g., a to-do app or mini blog) interacting with a database.

Workshop Flow:

Quick introduction: Why ORM?

Setting up SQLAlchemy and connecting to a database.

Defining models and creating tables.

Basic CRUD operations with hands-on exercises.

Introducing simple relationships (one-to-many).

Mini-project exercise.

Wrap-up & Q&A.

results.title: How a Non-Engineer Built a Python Application with SQLite That Improved Workflow Efficiency and Spread Across the Team
results.abstract: In many long-established companies, legacy core systems still operate today, and employees manually extract data, process it in Excel, and perform daily tasks within various organizational constraints. Working as a non-engineer in such an environment, I discovered Python and SQLite as practical tools for improving efficiency without changing the existing infrastructure. This talk shares how I built simple workflow applications using Python and SQLite, and how these tools gradually spread to my colleagues. Attendees will gain realistic insights into how non-engineers can start small, automate repetitive tasks, and introduce Python-based solutions even in highly constrained workplaces.
results.description:
I work in the administrative department of a company with over 100 years of history. In long-established organizations, mission-critical systems built decades ago are still in operation, where data extraction is manual, cross-department permissions are strict, and large-scale system redesigns are difficult to implement. As a result, employees must find ways to improve efficiency within significant technical and organizational constraints.

Many colleagues manually extract data from these legacy systems, manipulate it in spread sheets and complete required tasks—processes repeated every day. I had been improving my own workflow using Excel VBA and Microsoft Access, but encountered long-standing issues such as difficulty in sharing tools and declining performance as data grew.

My perspective changed when I began learning Python. Its flexibility in data handling and broad automation capabilities revealed possibilities far beyond what I had previously experienced. Later, I discovered SQLite—a lightweight, installation-free database that integrates seamlessly with Python. Its simplicity, reliability, and compatibility made it an ideal choice for environments where server-based databases cannot be deployed and where non-engineers must work within tight constraints. Incorporating SQLite significantly accelerated my workflow automation.

In this session, I will share:
- practical examples of simple workflow applications built with Python and SQLite,
- the process of how these tools were adopted and used by colleagues in the workplace,
- realistic, incremental approaches for non-engineers to achieve measurable impact.

This talk is intended for attendees working in companies that rely on legacy systems, teams dependent on spread sheet-based operations, and administrative or non-engineering professionals interested in automation. Participants will leave with practical ideas they can apply immediately in their own organizations.

results.title: Building Production-Grade Database Migration Systems in Python
results.abstract:
Database migrations are a critical component of production deployments, yet they remain a significant source of system failures. Partial migration failures can result in data inconsistency, requiring complex manual recovery procedures. This talk presents the architecture and implementation of a MongoDB migration framework that addresses these challenges through three core principles: atomic execution, automated dependency resolution, and AI-assisted code generation.

The session examines a production-tested migration engine built in Python, exploring its five core components. The dependency resolver implements topological sorting over a directed acyclic graph (DAG) to guarantee correct migration execution order, with cycle detection and missing dependency validation. The atomic executor wraps all operations in MongoDB session-based transactions, ensuring all-or-nothing semantics with automatic rollback on failure. The operation system provides composable, reversible primitives including AddField, RenameField, AlterField, and RunPython, with batch processing capabilities for large-scale data transformations handling over 100,000 documents. The AI-powered generator integrates git diff analysis, Python AST parsing, and large language models such as Gemini and Ollama to automatically detect model changes and generate migration code, achieving 85% accuracy in rename detection. Finally, the state recorder maintains persistent migration history for rollback capabilities and audit trails.

The talk includes live demonstrations and code walkthroughs covering topological sort implementation for dependency resolution, transaction management patterns in MongoDB, AST-based model parsing for change detection, LLM prompt engineering for code generation, and performance optimization strategies for batch operations. Drawing from real-world deployment experience, we discuss zero data loss incidents achieved through transactional guarantees, performance characteristics of 10,000 documents per second in batch processing, error handling patterns and graceful degradation strategies, and security considerations with production safeguards.

While demonstrated with MongoDB, the architectural patterns presented are transferable to relational databases like PostgreSQL and MySQL using standard transactions, existing migration frameworks such as Alembic and Django for enhanced safety, and distributed systems requiring ordered, atomic operations. The target audience includes backend engineers managing NoSQL databases, DevOps professionals responsible for deployment reliability, and technical leads concerned with data integrity, with basic Python knowledge and database familiarity recommended.

Attendees will gain practical understanding of migration system architecture, learn production-tested patterns for safe schema evolution, and receive working code examples applicable to their own projects. The talk emphasizes transferable concepts over tool-specific implementation, enabling application across different technology stacks.
results.description:
The presentation opens by establishing database migrations as a critical production challenge, examining how schema drift creates risks including partial failures and data inconsistency. We contrast manual SQL scripts and ORM-based tools with our approach using atomic transactions, dependency graphs, and AI-assisted generation.

The architecture overview introduces five core components: the loader for file discovery, dependency resolver for DAG construction, operation system for reversible transformations, executor for transactional execution, and recorder for state tracking. A live CLI demonstration shows successful migrations and automatic rollback on failures, illustrating the developer workflow from detection through execution.

The dependency resolution section examines how directed acyclic graphs ensure correct migration order through topological sorting, with cycle detection and dependency validation. Code examples demonstrate the real implementation handling complex dependency chains.

The atomic execution deep dive covers MongoDB session-based transactions providing all-or-nothing guarantees. We explore operation design patterns including AddField, RenameField, AlterField, and RunPython, with performance optimizations for batch processing of 100,000+ documents. Live code demonstrates transaction rollback mechanics.

The AI generation section details the pipeline from git diff through AST parsing to change classification, achieving 85% accuracy in rename detection. LLM integration with Gemini and Ollama generates validated migration code, with fallback mechanisms and interactive review workflows.

Production lessons cover operational metrics including zero data loss incidents and 10,000 documents per second throughput, design trade-offs between safety and requirements, error handling patterns, and security safeguards including dry-run validation and audit trails.

Framework-agnostic principles demonstrate how atomic execution, DAG-based ordering, and operation abstraction transfer to PostgreSQL, Django, Alembic, and distributed systems. Applications extend beyond migrations to data pipelines and infrastructure automation.

The conclusion emphasizes that atomic execution eliminates partial failures, dependency graphs ensure correct ordering, and AI assistance reduces human error.
results.notes: Live demo of how we can implement it in the existing and new project will be showcased.

results.title: AI x Insurance: Leveraging LLMs in Claims Operations
results.abstract:
AI has been democratized—it’s not confined to tech giants or the First World. The tools to use them are accessible, affordable, and ready to be deployed in the Philippines, across all industries. What is needed are strategic, entrepreneurial thinkers who can turn this technology into real-world solutions.

This presentation will showcase a local use-case of AI in the insurance industry. 

Every day, insurance companies handle thousands of claims-related emails—each requiring classification, prioritization, and routing. Traditionally, this process depends on human review, creating bottlenecks and delays. In this session, we explore how Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Large Language Models (LLMs) can transform this workflow by automating email triage at scale.

Audiences will learn why conventional machine learning approaches often fail in real-world settings where data is unstructured and unlabeled, and how LLMs offer a practical alternative—performing inference without costly model training. The presentation will break down the architecture of an AI-driven pipeline that classifies claim types, detects urgency, and even identifies emotional distress—built on a simple, streamlined design that’s easy to understand and implement.
results.description:
Traditional machine learning workflows depend on labeled datasets, feature engineering, and supervised training. These steps are often impractical when dealing with unstructured text like emails and customer inquiries. In the real-world, labelled data is rare, and data collection is unscalable. This makes machine learning impossible for most applications.

These obstacles are eliminated through approaches using pretrained Large Language Models (LLMs), making it possible to perform inference directly on raw natural language and unlabeled datasets. 

This session introduces how LLMs can act like human readers—interpreting incoming claims emails as they arrive, applying chain-of-thought reasoning, and producing structured outputs that enable multiple models to collaborate. To further enhance accuracy, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is incorporated, grounding responses in insurance-specific context.

Key concepts include:
- Why traditional ML pipelines struggle with unlabeled enterprise data
- How LLMs, powered by RAG, enable dynamic classification and prioritization without model training
- Techniques such as prompt engineering, chain-of-thought reasoning, and orchestrating LLMs to work together

The discussion highlights a paradigm shift: AI is no longer just a chatbot—it can be taught to operate as a digital workforce tailored to organizational needs.
results.notes: Presentation will not get into the nitty gritty details of the code but discuss the high level workflow and architecture of a local AI use case. Presentation will be designed to make sense for all levels of expertise in python. While the use case discussed will be in insurance, goal of the presentation is to encourage young innovators to think about how to implement AI in their own projects.

results.title: Hello, PyPI! How to Share Your Python Tools with the World
results.abstract:
In this talk, we’ll walk through the complete journey of creating and publishing your first Python package. We’ll cover the essentials of structuring your project, writing a pyproject.toml, adding metadata, versioning your library, and using tools like build and twine to upload your package to PyPI. Along the way, we’ll also share tips on documentation, licensing, and best practices to make your library more maintainable and user-friendly.

By the end of the session, you’ll not only know how to publish your own package to PyPI but also feel empowered to share your code with the Python community and beyond.
results.description: Publishing your Python library doesn’t have to be intimidating. In this session, we’ll explore the step-by-step process of taking your Python code and making it installable by anyone in the world through the Python Package Index (PyPI). From structuring your project and writing essential configuration files to building, testing, and uploading your package, you’ll gain practical knowledge on how to share your tools with the global Python community. Whether you’re a beginner curious about open source or an experienced developer looking to distribute your work, this talk will equip you with the confidence and skills to say: “Hello, PyPI!”

results.title: Seeking Hidden Message Through Memory Dump with Volatility
results.abstract: Reading a memory dump is a hard work, choosing a good tool is another hard work. Then come volatility, a framework-based python to help malware researcher, forensics, and CTF-enjoyer to seek the hidden message from memory dump. This free and opensource tool was designed to perform digital investigation and incident response. Because volatile memory contains valuable information about the runtime state of the system, provides the ability to link artifacts from traditional forensic analysis (network, file system, registry), and provides the ability to ascertain investigative leads that have been unbeknownst to most analysts.
results.description: This talk will cover about volatility and how to use the tool in digital forensics case.

results.title: Contributing to Open Source: Azure Developer CLI Template
results.abstract:
The Azure Developer CLI (azd) has quickly become a powerful tool for developers looking to streamline cloud-native application development on Microsoft Azure. But beyond using azd, contributing to its growing ecosystem especially through templates, opens the door to deeper collaboration, community impact, and real-world innovation.

In this talk, we’ll explore how developers can actively contribute to open source by building and improving Azure Developer CLI templates. You’ll learn the fundamentals of the azd template structure, best practices for designing reusable and production-ready templates, and how to navigate the contribution workflow, from ideation to pull request. Whether you're new to open source or looking to expand your involvement, this session will equip you with the knowledge, tools, and confidence to start contributing meaningfully to the azd ecosystem and empower the global developer community.
results.description:
Open source thrives because developers share, collaborate, and build tools that help others succeed, the Azure Developer CLI (azd) is a perfect example of this ecosystem in action. This session dives into how you can contribute to open source by creating and enhancing azd templates that accelerate cloud development on Azure.

We’ll break down what makes a great azd template, how the folder structure and configuration work, and the standards the community follows to ensure quality and reusability. I’ll also walk you through my own first contribution to the azd templates: a FastAPI application with a PostgreSQL database, fully deployed to Azure Container Apps. You’ll see the considerations, challenges, and steps involved in turning a real-world project into a community-ready template.

I’ll also share my experience contributing to this open-source project, including the actual review process and feedback from Microsoft maintainers, so you can better understand what to expect when making your own contributions.

results.title: Developing Data Pipelines Anywhere with dlt
results.abstract:
Big Data has been a prevalent concept in recent years, and Python is no stranger to this world. However, running big data pipelines means getting a lot of compute, dancing around distributed computation, and maintaining a stack of complex tools.

What if it didn't have to be that way? What if you can have the power to get data to and from anywhere, just with the machine you're already working on?

This talk introduces `dlt`, a lightweight Pythonic framework for developing ELT pipelines, along with its vision of the Composable Data Stack. You'll get to experience how to develop an end-to-end data pipeline all contained in one Python script and deploy it anywhere, whether it be a local notebook, a `cron` job on your server, or a full-on workflow orchestrator.
results.description:
**Target Audience**  
This talk targets experienced data practitioners with general Python knowledge, including data analysts, scientists, and engineers. It's a fit for those building or simplifying data pipelines, from beginners facing initial setup challenges or experienced users optimizing workflows.

**Key Takeaways**  
By the end of this talk, attendees will:  
1. Grasp `dlt`'s role in creating composable, lightweight ELT pipelines.  
2. Know how to construct an end-to-end data pipeline with `dlt` by extracting API data and loading it into DuckDB.  
3. Be capable to assess if their projects can leverage simple and composable Python tools over complex Big Data frameworks, reducing overhead.

**Talk Outline**  
- **Introduction to and dlt (5 minutes)**  
  - Challenges of Big Data: complexity, costs, and overkill for typical volumes.  
  - Overview of `dlt`: open-source Python library for ELT, with features like schema inference, data normalization, and no-backend deployment.  
- **Building a Pipeline with dlt (15 minutes)**  
  - Core features: composability for reusable components, support for sources like REST APIs.  
  - Step-by-step walkthrough: creating a pipeline to extract from a public API and load into DuckDB, including code snippets for resources and pipeline runs.  
  - Deployment flexibility: run in Jupyter notebooks, cron jobs, or integrators like Airflow, preserving existing security and stacks.  
- **The Composable Data Stack and Future Vision (7 minutes)**  
  - Defining the Composable Data Stack: modular components (e.g., catalogs, query engines) for incremental upgrades.  
  - How `dlt` and similar tools fit: enabling data democracy, cost reduction, and integration with other data tools.  
  - Inspiring rethink: when lightweight solutions outperform Big Data, making data accessible to all Pythonistas.  
- **Wrap-Up (3 minutes)**
  - Key call: Explore `dlt` and the Composable Data Stack to simplify data workflows.
results.notes:
Past Talks:
- [Python in a Click: PyCon APAC 2025 Lightning Talk (GitHub Repo)](https://github.com/jarcelao/python-in-a-click)
  - [Past Recording (starts @ 14:30)](https://youtu.be/tEXZ2lIr5Xk?si=LBPnGCBSo9w-aSWo&t=868)

References:

- [dltHub GitHub](https://github.com/dlt-hub/dlt)
- [The Road to Composable Data Systems: Thoughts on the Last 15 Years and the Future](https://wesmckinney.com/blog/looking-back-15-years/)

results.title: Notebooks that dream with marimo
results.abstract:
Notebooks are beloved for data exploration and education, but dreaded for production code. Poor reproducibility, hidden state, and out-of-order execution have kept them quarantined from "real" software engineering. Not to mention the thousand-line git merge conflicts.

Maybe it's time to rethink the notebook, and marimo does just that.
results.description:
This talk introduces marimo, a reactive Python notebook where cells automatically re-execute when their dependencies change—eliminating hidden state and ensuring reproducibility.

Through live demos, we'll see how this enables new workflows: notebooks that run as scripts and deploy as web apps.

We'll then peek under the hood: how reactive execution works using DAGs and what modern Python tooling like uv, DuckDB, and anywidget unlocks for the notebook experience.
results.notes:
I was an open-source contributor for marimo (late 2024) before being brought in full time around March 2025. Currently we've been acquired by CoreWeave and the whole team has moved over, so I am a software engineer at CoreWeave. Marimo remains an open source project and is in rapid development: https://github.com/marimo-team/marimo.

I gave a talk in India but that was much longer (1 hour+), so I will pare down the slides and focus on demos for this talk.
Previous slides: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1rEwqwLr61RiiBXHP80NIjMx5vCxsfaCT/view?usp=sharing
Colleague's presentation: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oOqRctpii6I

I hope to live-code during this talk as that's the best way to demonstrate the features.

results.title: Python for Planet Earth: Climate Modeling and Sustainability in Action
results.abstract: Discover how Python is shaping the future of our planet in this engaging talk. I’ll explore how Python is used to model climate change, monitor environmental shifts, and drive sustainability projects. See how this powerful language is enabling scientists and developers to turn data into actionable insights, helping to tackle some of the most pressing challenges facing our world today. Whether you’re a coder or a climate enthusiast, this session will show you how Python can make a real difference for the Earth.
results.description:
In this talk, I'll delve into how Python is revolutionizing climate modeling and sustainability efforts. As the planet faces unprecedented environmental challenges, the need for precise data analysis and actionable insights has never been greater. Python, with its powerful libraries and tools, is at the forefront of this scientific revolution.

I'll begin by exploring how Python is used in climate modeling, focusing on libraries like netCDF4 and xarray to handle and analyze complex climate datasets. Attendees will see how these tools enable scientists to simulate climate systems, predict future scenarios, and assess potential impacts on global and regional scales.

Next, I'll shift to environmental monitoring, where Python plays a crucial role in processing and visualizing geospatial data. Using tools like Rasterio, GeoPandas, and PyProj, I'll demonstrate how Python facilitates real-time monitoring of environmental changes, such as deforestation, air quality, and ocean health.

Finally, I'll examine how Python supports sustainability projects, from renewable energy forecasting to resource management. By leveraging Python's data analysis capabilities, these projects are driving meaningful change in the pursuit of a more sustainable future.

Throughout the talk, attendees will gain hands-on insights into key Python libraries, see real-world examples of their application, and understand how Python is empowering scientists, researchers, and developers to make a tangible impact on our planet's future. Whether attendees are involved in environmental science, data analysis, or just passionate about sustainability, this session will provide you with the knowledge and tools to contribute to the cause.

results.title: Do you know how well your model is doing? Evaluate your LLMs
results.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are becoming central to modern applications, yet effectively evaluating their performance remains a significant challenge. How do you objectively compare different models, benchmark the impact of fine-tuning, or ensure your LLM responses adhere to safety guidelines (guard-railing)? This hands-on workshop addresses these critical questions.
results.description:
## Prerequisites:

Have experience coding in Python (with Python installed in the local machine)
Basic understand of machine learning and LLMs
Experience with Hugging Face Transformers preferred but not necessary
A Hugging Face Hub account (sign up for free)
A modern computer that can fine-turn small LLMs locally

## Description:

We will begin with an essential revision of the Hugging Face Transformers library, covering basic LLM inference and fine-tuning. The core of the workshop will introduce and provide deep practice with Lighteval, an efficient and powerful LLM evaluation framework. Participants will learn how to leverage Lighteval to compare various LLMs available on the Hugging Face Hub using a range of pre-built tasks and metrics.

Finally, we will delve into advanced evaluation techniques, focusing on creating custom tasks and metrics tailored to unique, real-world application requirements. Participants will learn how to prepare custom datasets on the Hugging Face Hub and integrate them into Lighteval for precise, domain-specific evaluation. By the end of this workshop, you will possess the practical skills to rigorously evaluate, benchmark, and fine-tune your LLMs with confidence.

## Outline:

#### Part 1

- Presentation: The importance of evaluation of LLMs
    - Compare performance of LLM for specific tasks
    - Benchmark the fine-tuning performance
    - Rail guard the LLM responses
- Coding exercise: Introduction and revision of  Hugging Face Transformers
    - Revision of using Transformers for LLM influence
    - Fine tuning a LLM with transformers

#### Part 2

- Presentation: Introduction of Lighteval
    - What is Lighteval and what can it do
    - Different tasks and metrics available in Lighteval
- Coding exercise: Using Lighteval to compare LLMs
    - Familiar the use of Lighteval
    - Compare two LLMs on Hugging Face Hub
    - Experiment with different tasks and metrics

#### Part 3

- Presentation: Advance use of Lighteval
    - Introduction of custom tasks and metrics
    - What is needed for creating custom tasks and metrics
    - How to put custom tasks and metrics together
- Coding exercise: Practice with custom tasks and metrics
    - Uploading datasets to Hugging Face Hub
    - Creating custom tasks and metrics
    - Using custom tasks and metrics to compare LLMs

results.title: Python Isn’t My Language, But the Community Is
results.abstract:
I work in tech—as a data cloud engineer—but Python isn’t at the center of my daily work. Still, I’ve been part of the Python Philippines community for years. I’ve attended events, followed the conversations, volunteered behind the scenes, and supported others in ways that didn’t involve writing code.

I’ve often wondered: Do I really belong here if Python isn’t my main tool?

This talk is for anyone who feels adjacent to Python—drawn to the language and its people, but not immersed in it professionally or visibly. It’s for the quiet community builders, the volunteers, the career-shifters, and the people who show up even if they’re not shipping packages or giving talks.

I’ll share what kept me here despite not “building with Python,” what I’ve learned by being part of the community without always feeling central, and why I believe the Python world thrives not just because of the code we write—but because of the people we welcome.

You don’t need to be a Python pro to belong. You don’t need to contribute code to contribute meaningfully.

 If you care, show up, and support others—you’re already part of the community.
 I’m proof.
results.description:
This talk is a reminder that belonging in the Python community isn’t defined by how much code you write. Through a personal story of showing up, supporting others, and staying involved despite not using Python daily, it highlights the quiet but powerful impact of community participation beyond programming.

The audience will leave encouraged, knowing they don’t need to be Python experts to contribute meaningfully. If you care, show up, and support others—you already belong.

results.title: Building Self-Improving AI Agents with Agentic Context Engineering (ACE)
results.abstract: In this talk, we will explore how modern AI agent systems go beyond traditional prompt-engineering and instead require deliberate context engineering designing the information, memory, feedback loops and workflows that enable agents to reason, learn and improve autonomously. You’ll take away a clear framework for building agentic systems that continuously refine themselves, using context as the backbone of intelligence rather than only relying on larger language models. We will cover the what, why and how of self-improvement in AI agents, and practical steps you can apply today in enterprise or research settings
results.description:
AI agents today can do far more than respond to prompts they can reason, adapt, and improve when we design the right context around them. This talk introduces Agentic Context Engineering (ACE), a practical approach to building agents that learn from their interactions through structured memory, state management, feedback loops, and reflection workflows.

We’ll explore how context not just larger models serves as the backbone of intelligence in agentic systems. You’ll learn how to design context flows that support decision-making, tool-use, iteration, and long-term behavior refinement. We will break down a clear architecture for building self-improving agents, discuss common challenges such as context drift, memory bloat, long-context limitations, and agent-to-agent communication, and outline governance considerations for safe deployment.
Whether you’re building an enterprise AI assistant, workflow automation agent, or research prototype, this session will equip you with a framework and actionable steps to start creating context-engineered, self-improving agents that evolve with every interaction.

Note- This talk is hands-on, not theoretical. We will walk through real-world agent workflows implemented in Python using open-source LLM frameworks and APIs.

#Outline:
Why Context matters(3mins)
Prompt Engineering vs Context Engineering(2mins)
Core Components of Agentic Systems(5mins)
Context Engineering Framework & Best Practices(3mins)
Building a Self-Improving Agent (Architecture Blueprint)(3mins)
Real domain case study(10mins)
Pitfalls, Governance & Ethics (2 min)
Q&A / Discussion (2 min)
results.notes:
Prerequisites:  
* Basic development experience (e.g., Python, or at least comfort with software workflows) so that the architecture discussion is meaningful. 
* Basic understanding of AI or LLMs 
* A willingness to think beyond “single prompt → output” and engage with system architecture, memory/state, feedback loop

results.title: Python for Audio Analysis: Seeing Sound
results.abstract:
What if you could visualize music, detect beats, or classify songs — all in Python? In this talk, I’ll dive into audio signal processing and visualization using powerful libraries like librosa, numpy, and matplotlib. I’ll show how sound can be transformed into data, visualized as spectrograms, and analyzed to extract rhythm, pitch, and genre.
Attendees will learn the fundamentals of digital signal processing (DSP) for audio, how to work with waveforms and frequency data in Python, and how to use machine learning to recognize patterns in sound. By the end, you’ll know how to turn any sound file into a visually engaging dataset — and how to make Python “see” sound.
No prior DSP experience is required — just familiarity with Python and curiosity about the intersection of code, math, and music.
results.description:
Sound is one of the most fascinating forms of data — complex, continuous, and emotional. In this talk, I’ll explore how Python can transform raw audio into something we can visualize, understand, and even teach machines to interpret.
Using libraries like librosa, numpy, and matplotlib, I’ll demonstrate how to load and process sound files, visualize waveforms and spectrograms, and extract meaningful features such as tempo, pitch, and timbre. From there, I’ll show how those features can drive creative and analytical applications — from beat detection and genre classification to real-time sound visualizations that bring music to life.
Along the way, I’ll explain the key concepts behind digital signal processing in an approachable way, showing how frequency, amplitude, and time interact to shape what we hear. You’ll see practical demos of Python code “listening” to and “seeing” music — and learn how to apply the same techniques to speech, environmental sounds, or any audio dataset.
Whether you’re a data scientist curious about signals, a musician intrigued by algorithms, or a Python developer looking for a new creative outlet, this session will show you how to make your code hear the world and visualize it like never before.
