results.title: Building a Self-Hosted Voice AI Platform with LiveKit & Pydantic AI
results.abstract: In this talk I’ll walk through how we built a fully self-hosted Voice AI platform in production using Python, LiveKit, and Pydantic AI. The system connects directly to our telephony SIP trunk, spins up real-time voice rooms, and runs low-latency speech-to-text, LLM, and text-to-speech in a tight loop. On top of that we added full observability, per-component latency tracking, and a data pipeline for daily analysis. The talk is aimed at backend and ML engineers who are comfortable with Python and want to see what it really takes to run voice agents at scale, with strict latency and cost constraints, without being locked into a SaaS vendor.
results.description:
This talk is a case study of how we designed and shipped a self-hosted Voice AI platform at a neobank using Python and an open-source stack.

The problem: we needed voice agents for several business flows (customer support, collections, outbound campaigns), but typical SaaS call-center bots couldn’t meet our requirements on latency, cost per minute, data control, and vendor lock-in. We decided to build our own platform around LiveKit and Python.

The core architecture looks like this:

- Telephony sends calls via a SIP trunk into the open-source LiveKit SIP server.
- The SIP server forwards calls into the LiveKit server, which creates a room and wires up participants (caller + Voice AI agent).
- The Voice AI agent is implemented using LiveKit Agents and Pydantic AI.
- Inside the agent loop we run low-latency streaming STT → LLM (via Pydantic AI graph) → TTS, and stream audio back to the caller.
- During and after the call we log all events and state to Kafka; a daily batch job loads this into our DWH.
- We push detailed status and latency metrics (STT, LLM, TTS, end-to-end) into Prometheus/Grafana and traces into Jaeger so we can debug individual calls and watch P95/P99 across the whole system.

The Python part is where most of the “brains” live:
- Pydantic AI is used as the agent framework: typed state, tools, and a graph of deterministic steps for our call flows.
- We started with strictly scripted flows and gradually expose more decisions to the LLM, while still keeping the graph transparent and debuggable.
- Each tool call is logged with context, so we can inspect why the agent made a given decision and replay problematic calls offline.
- STT and TTS providers are abstracted behind open-source livekit-agents framework, so we can swap vendors without rewriting any code.

On the non-functional side:
- Platform is self-hosted and open-source (LiveKit, SIP server, agents framework), so there is no hard vendor lock-in at the platform level.
- Per-minute cost of Voice AI calls ended up roughly 2–3x cheaper than a human agent, while being easier to measure, iterate, and A/B test.

results.title: CareLink — a privacy-first SmartApp that matches community needs to volunteers & local resources
results.abstract:
## Abstract
CareLink is a privacy-first smartapp that helps people quickly find help — food, medical aid, shelter, volunteers — during everyday needs and crises.  
In this talk I’ll show a practical Python stack (**Streamlit + FastAPI + lightweight ML**) for building an accessible, low-bandwidth service that understands natural-language requests (including Taglish), performs semantic matching with a vector index, and ranks results by proximity and trust signals.  
You’ll see a live demo, an architecture blueprint, and a checklist for safely deploying a community pilot.  
Attendees will leave with a ready-to-run repo, concrete implementation notes, and the tools to adapt CareLink for local communities.
results.description:
## Description
### 🎯 Audience
Intermediate Python developers, civic-tech practitioners, community organizers, and anyone interested in building high-impact, deployable apps.

### 💡 What this talk covers
- **Problem:** People often can’t quickly locate local help during urgent or everyday needs — friction comes from language, low bandwidth, and lack of verification/trust.  
- **Solution overview:** *CareLink* — Streamlit UI + FastAPI backend + semantic search (sentence-transformers → FAISS/Chroma) + geospatial scoring + volunteer verification & notification.  
- **Demo:** Submit a request (Taglish / English) → semantic match + geolocation ranking → volunteer/resource accepts.  
- **Implementation highlights:** Embedding generation, compact vector index for low-resource hosting, Haversine-based ranking, simple local-language normalization, on-device/offline fallback strategy (ONNX/torchscript), and safety (rate-limiting, moderation, verification).  
- **Deployment & operations:** Hosting options, privacy considerations, and community adoption checklist.

### 🧠 What attendees will gain
1. A reproducible architecture blueprint and a link to a starter repo they can run locally.  
2. Practical code patterns for semantic matching, geospatial ranking, and low-bandwidth fallbacks.  
3. A checklist for safety, verification, and community piloting.  
4. Ideas to adapt the app to other local languages and contexts.

### 🕒 30-minute outline
| Time | Topic |
|------|-------|
| 0:00–0:02 | Hook: local scenarios and problem framing |
| 0:02–0:06 | Architecture overview (diagram + rationale) |
| 0:06–0:14 | Core implementation: embeddings + vector DB + language fallback |
| 0:14–0:22 | Live demo: request flow, volunteer matching |
| 0:22–0:27 | Deployment & privacy considerations |
| 0:27–0:30 | Impact metrics, next steps, call-to-action |

### ⚙️ Tech Stack
- **Frontend:** Streamlit  
- **Backend:** FastAPI  
- **Semantic Search:** sentence-transformers + FAISS/Chroma  
- **Geospatial:** Python `haversine`  
- **Notifications:** SMTP / Telegram bot  
- **Optional:** ONNX for offline inference  
- **Deployment:** Streamlit Cloud or Heroku/GCP free tier
results.notes:
## Notes (Private)
- I will present a live demo and can provide a public Streamlit demo link or short screencast. Demo repo: `[to be added]`.  
- **Audience level:** Intermediate Python users; useful for community orgs and developers new to semantic search.  
- **Deliverables:** GitHub starter repo (Streamlit + FastAPI + small dataset + setup guide), slide deck, and a deployment checklist.  
- **Safety & ethics:** Privacy-first design, volunteer verification, rate-limiting, and guidance for partnering with local NGOs or LGUs.  
- **Background:** Prior experience building ML-driven mobile and web apps (e.g., rice-disease classifier with edge deployment).  
- **AV needs:** Projector + internet for live demo (backup video available).

results.title: Observability & Alerting in the Python Ecosystem: From Logs to Metrics to SLOs
results.abstract: In this topic, I’ll explore how observability practices can transform the way Python applications are monitored, debugged, and optimized in production. From structured logging and distributed tracing to metrics and SLO-driven alerting, this talk connects the dots between development and reliability. Using real-world examples from SRE and DevOps experience, I’ll demonstrate how to implement robust observability pipelines with Python tools like OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, and Grafana. Attendees will gain practical insights into building feedback loops that reduce MTTR, prevent alert fatigue, and drive a culture of reliability through data.
results.description: This talk focuses on bringing SRE-grade observability into Python systems. We’ll break down what true observability means—going beyond dashboards to actionable insight. Through live examples, I’ll show how Python services can emit rich telemetry data, integrate with open-source observability stacks, and enforce service-level objectives (SLOs) that actually matter. Whether you’re a Python developer or DevOps engineer, you’ll learn how to design meaningful alerts, visualize key signals, and evolve from reactive monitoring to proactive reliability management in your applications.

results.title: Python-Powered Data Storytelling
results.abstract: Python enables data professionals to move beyond analysis and transform information into clear, compelling stories. With libraries like Pandas, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly, Python supports insightful exploration, expressive visualizations, and interactive elements that enhance communication. This work highlights practical techniques for turning patterns, trends, and insights into engaging narratives, making data more understandable, impactful, and actionable.
results.description: This talk introduces how Python can be used to create impactful data stories that resonate with any audience. We will cover how to identify key insights, structure a narrative, and design visuals that highlight the message. Through examples and practical demonstrations, you’ll see how Python’s visualization tools can be used to build clear, creative, and interactive data stories. The session is designed to be accessible, engaging, and immediately applicable to real-world analysis and reporting.

results.title: Demystifying Firecracker: Building and Deploying a Custom MicroVM with Firecracker and Python
results.abstract:
MicroVMs (micro-virtual machines) provide a lightweight, secure, and efficient way to run isolated workloads, blending the isolation of traditional virtual machines with the speed and low overhead of containers. Firecracker, an open-source virtualization technology developed by AWS, is designed specifically for such use cases, powering services like AWS Lambda. It enables fast boot times (often under 200ms), minimal resource usage, and strong security through KVM-based hardware virtualization.

This guide expands on beginner tutorials to demonstrate how to launch a basic MicroVM using Python for API interactions, configure networking for internet access, and customize the root filesystem (rootfs) to automatically run a simple Flask web application on boot. We'll cover everything in detail, including code snippets, configuration files, and troubleshooting tips. This workflow is Python-centric, making it accessible for developers, and could form the basis for a PyCon MY 2025 proposal on "Demystifying MicroVMs: Python-Driven Virtualization from Boot to Deployment." We'll assume a Linux host (e.g., Ubuntu) with KVM support enabled.
results.description:
## Prerequisites

- A Linux system with KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine) support. Verify with `kvm-ok` or `lsmod | grep kvm`.
- Python 3 installed (no external libraries needed for the basic script).
- Basic command-line tools: `curl`, `wget`, `git`, `screen`, `socat`, `iptables`, `ip` (from iproute2).
- Administrative privileges (sudo) for networking and file operations.
- A working directory, e.g., `~/firecracker-demo/`, for downloads and scripts.
- Firecracker binary (version 1.12.1 or later).
- Demo kernel and rootfs images from AWS S3 for initial testing (Alpine Linux-based for simplicity).

Ensure your host has internet access, as the guest MicroVM will rely on it via NAT.

## Step 1: Install Firecracker and Prepare Kernel/RootFS

Download and install the Firecracker binary:

```bash
curl -LOJ https://github.com/firecracker-microvm/firecracker/releases/download/v1.12.1/firecracker-v1.12.1-x86_64.tgz
tar -xvzf firecracker-v1.12.1-x86_64.tgz
chmod +x release-v1.12.1-x86_64/firecracker-v1.12.1-x86_64
sudo mv release-v1.12.1-x86_64/firecracker-v1.12.1-x86_64 /usr/local/bin/firecracker

```

Verify the installation:

```bash
firecracker --version

```

This should output the version number, confirming Firecracker is ready.

Next, download a pre-built kernel and root filesystem for testing:

```bash
cd ~/firecracker-demo/
wget https://s3.amazonaws.com/spec.ccfc.min/img/hello/kernel/hello-vmlinux.bin
wget https://s3.amazonaws.com/spec.ccfc.min/img/hello/fsfiles/hello-rootfs.ext4

```

These provide a minimal bootable Linux environment (based on Alpine) that we'll use initially.

## Step 2: Launch the Firecracker Process and Basic MicroVM with Python

Firecracker is API-driven, using a UNIX socket for control. Start it in a background session for persistence:

```bash
mkdir -p /tmp/firecracker && rm -f /tmp/firecracker/firecracker.socket
screen -S firecracker
sudo firecracker --api-sock /tmp/firecracker/firecracker.socket

```

Detach the screen session with `Ctrl+A+D`. You can reattach later with `screen -r firecracker` to view console output.

Now, create a Python script (`launch_vm.py`) to interact with Firecracker's API over the UNIX socket. This script uses built-in modules (`http.client`, `json`, `socket`) for simplicity—no external dependencies like `requests`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import http.client
import json
import socket
import os
import sys

# Unix socket path
SOCKET_PATH = '/tmp/firecracker/firecracker.socket'

def unix_http_request(method, endpoint, data=None, timeout=5):
    # Create Unix socket connection
    sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_UNIX, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    sock.settimeout(timeout)
    try:
        sock.connect(SOCKET_PATH)
    except Exception as e:
        sock.close()
        raise

    conn = http.client.HTTPConnection('localhost')  # host is ignored when using unix socket
    # attach the connected unix socket to the HTTPConnection instance
    conn.sock = sock

    headers = {
        'Content-Type': 'application/json',
        'Accept': 'application/json'
    }
    body = json.dumps(data) if data is not None else None

    try:
        conn.request(method, endpoint, body=body, headers=headers)
        response = conn.getresponse()
        raw = response.read()
        text = raw.decode('utf-8') if raw else ''
        # try to parse JSON; if empty or not JSON return status+text
        try:
            parsed = json.loads(text) if text else {'status': response.status}
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            parsed = {'status': response.status, 'body': text}
        return parsed
    finally:
        try:
            conn.close()
        except Exception:
            pass
        try:
            sock.close()
        except Exception:
            pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Use absolute paths so running under sudo won't change them unexpectedly.
    kernel_path = os.path.abspath('./hello-vmlinux.bin')
    rootfs_path = os.path.abspath('./hello-rootfs.ext4')

    # quick checks
    if not os.path.exists(kernel_path):
        print(f"Kernel image not found: {kernel_path}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    if not os.path.exists(rootfs_path):
        print(f"Rootfs image not found: {rootfs_path}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    # Step 1: Configure boot source (kernel)
    boot_data = {
        "kernel_image_path": kernel_path,
        "boot_args": "console=ttyS0 reboot=k panic=1 pci=off"
    }
    print("Configuring boot source:", unix_http_request('PUT', '/boot-source', boot_data))

    # Step 2: Configure root drive
    drive_data = {
        "drive_id": "rootfs",
        "path_on_host": rootfs_path,
        "is_root_device": True,
        "is_read_only": False
    }
    print("Configuring root drive:", unix_http_request('PUT', '/drives/rootfs', drive_data))

    # Step 3: Machine config (optional: 1 vCPU, 128MB RAM)
    machine_data = {
        "vcpu_count": 1,
        "mem_size_mib": 128
    }
    print("Configuring machine:", unix_http_request('PUT', '/machine-config', machine_data))

    # Step 4: Start the instance
    start_data = {"action_type": "InstanceStart"}
    print("Starting microVM:", unix_http_request('PUT', '/actions', start_data))
    print("MicroVM launched! Check console in screen session.")

```

Run the script:

```bash
python3 launch_vm.py

```

Expect output like `{'status': 204}` for each step, indicating success. Reattach the screen session (`screen -r firecracker`) to observe the boot process. After approximately 125ms, you'll see "Welcome to Alpine Linux!" Login with username `root` and password `root`.

If the VM doesn't start, check for errors in the screen output (e.g., socket issues) and ensure the paths are correct.

## Step 3: Configure Networking on Host and Guest

To enable guest-host and guest-internet communication, set up a TAP (virtual Ethernet) device on the host.

Stop any running VM first: In the screen session, press `Ctrl+C` to terminate Firecracker, then remove the socket:

```bash
rm -f /tmp/firecracker/firecracker.socket

```

Create the TAP device:

```bash
sudo ip tuntap add dev fc-tap0 mode tap
sudo ip addr add 172.16.0.1/24 dev fc-tap0
sudo ip link set fc-tap0 up

```

Update `launch_vm.py` to add a network interface. Insert this after the machine config section (before starting the instance):

```python
net_data = {
    "iface_id": "eth0",
    "guest_mac": "06:00:AC:10:00:02",
    "host_dev_name": "fc-tap0"
}
print("Configuring network:", unix_http_request('PUT', '/network-interfaces/eth0', net_data))

```

Restart Firecracker:

```bash
screen -S firecracker
sudo firecracker --api-sock /tmp/firecracker/firecracker.socket
# Detach: Ctrl+A+D

```

Run the updated script:

```bash
sudo python3 launch_vm.py

```

Reattach screen (`screen -r firecracker`). After boot, log in (root/root) and configure the guest network:

```bash
ifconfig eth0 172.16.0.2 netmask 255.255.255.0 up
ip route add default via 172.16.0.1 dev eth0
echo "nameserver 8.8.8.8" >> /etc/resolv.conf

```

Test connectivity:

- Ping the host: `ping 172.16.0.1`
- Ping the internet: `ping 8.8.8.8`

If internet ping fails, ensure the host has internet access and enable IP forwarding/NAT:

Check IP forwarding:

```bash
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward

```

If 0, enable it:

```bash
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
echo "net.ipv4.ip_forward=1" | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
sudo sysctl -p

```

Install `iptables` if needed:

```bash
sudo apt install iptables

```

Add NAT rule:

```bash
sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 172.16.0.0/24 -o wlp2s0 -j MASQUERADE  # Replace wlp2s0 with your host's internet interface (e.g., eth0 or wlan0)

```

Verify:

```bash
sudo iptables -t nat -L -v -n

```

Add forwarding rules if needed:

```bash
sudo iptables -A FORWARD -s 172.16.0.0/24 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A FORWARD -d 172.16.0.0/24 -j ACCEPT

```

Persist iptables (install `iptables-persistent` if needed):

```bash
sudo apt install iptables-persistent
sudo iptables-save > /etc/iptables/rules.v4

```

Retest pings in the guest. This setup allows the guest to route traffic through the host.

## Step 4: Build a Custom RootFS with Python and Flask App

For advanced use, customize the rootfs to include Python, clone a Flask app repo, and set up auto-start via systemd.

Clone the Firecracker repository for build scripts:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/firecracker-microvm/firecracker --depth=1
cd firecracker/resources/

```

This directory includes `rebuild.sh` (build orchestrator), `chroot.sh` (for custom commands in chroot), and `overlay/` (for file overlays).

Edit `chroot.sh` to install packages, create a Python venv, clone/install the app, and enable systemd service. Here's the full updated `chroot.sh`:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Copyright 2023 Amazon.com, Inc. or its affiliates. All Rights Reserved.
# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0

# fail if we encounter an error, uninitialized variable or a pipe breaks
set -eu -o pipefail

# be verbose
set -x
PS4='+\t '

cp -ruv $rootfs/* /

packages="udev systemd-sysv openssh-server iproute2 curl socat python3-minimal iperf3 iputils-ping fio kmod tmux hwloc-nox vim-tiny trace-cmd linuxptp strace python3-boto3 pciutils python3-venv python3-pip git"

# msr-tools is only supported on x86-64.
arch=$(uname -m)
if [ "${arch}" == "x86_64" ]; then
    packages="$packages msr-tools cpuid"
fi

export DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
apt --fix-broken install -y
apt autoremove -y
apt-mark showhold | xargs -r apt-mark unhold  # Unhold any held packages if present
apt update
apt install -y --no-install-recommends $packages
apt autoremove

# Create Python venv for isolation
mkdir -p /home/venv
python3 -m venv /home/venv/myapp

# Clone and install app
mkdir -p /home/projects
git clone https://github.com/hmrafid/simple-flask-app.git /home/projects/app
source /home/venv/myapp/bin/activate
python -m pip install -r /home/projects/app/requirements.txt
deactivate

# Make myapp.sh executable
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/myapp.sh

# Enable the myapp systemd service
systemctl enable myapp.service

# Set a hostname.
echo "ubuntu-fc-uvm" > /etc/hostname

passwd -d root

# The serial getty service hooks up the login prompt to the kernel console
# at ttyS0 (where Firecracker connects its serial console). We'll set it up
# for autologin to avoid the login prompt.
for console in ttyS0; do
    mkdir "/etc/systemd/system/serial-getty@$console.service.d/"
    cat <<'EOF' > "/etc/systemd/system/serial-getty@$console.service.d/override.conf"
[Service]
# systemd requires this empty ExecStart line to override
ExecStart=
ExecStart=-/sbin/agetty --autologin root -o '-p -- \\u' --keep-baud 115200,38400,9600 %I dumb
EOF
done

# Setup fcnet service. This is a custom Firecracker setup for assigning IPs
# to the network interfaces in the guests spawned by the CI.
ln -s /etc/systemd/system/fcnet.service /etc/systemd/system/sysinit.target.wants/fcnet.service

# Disable resolved and ntpd
#
rm -f /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/systemd-resolved.service
rm -f /etc/systemd/system/dbus-org.freedesktop.resolve1.service
rm -f /etc/systemd/system/sysinit.target.wants/systemd-timesyncd.service

# make /tmp a tmpfs
ln -s /usr/share/systemd/tmp.mount /etc/systemd/system/tmp.mount
systemctl enable tmp.mount

# don't need this
systemctl disable e2scrub_reap.service
rm -vf /etc/systemd/system/timers.target.wants/*
# systemctl list-units --failed
# /lib/systemd/system/systemd-random-seed.service

systemctl enable var-lib-systemd.mount

# disable Predictable Network Interface Names to keep ethN names
# even with PCI enabled
ln -s /dev/null /etc/systemd/network/99-default.link

#### trim image https://wiki.ubuntu.com/ReducingDiskFootprint
# this does not save much, but oh well
rm -rf /usr/share/{doc,man,info,locale}

cat >> /etc/sysctl.conf <<EOF
# This avoids a SPECTRE vuln
kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1
EOF

# Build a manifest
dpkg-query --show >/root/manifest

```

This script installs essential packages (including Python tools), fixes broken dependencies, creates a venv, clones a sample Flask repo (replace with your own if needed), installs dependencies, makes the startup script executable, and enables the service. It also sets up auto-login and other optimizations for Firecracker.

Create systemd service files in `overlay/`:

- `overlay/etc/systemd/system/myapp.service`:

```bash
[Unit]
Description=My Flask App
After=local-fs.target network.target

[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/myapp.sh
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

```

- `overlay/usr/local/bin/myapp.sh`:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
source /home/venv/myapp/bin/activate
python /home/projects/app/app.py

```

For persistent networking, modify `overlay/usr/local/bin/fcnet-setup.sh` (add inside the `main` function):

```bash
ifconfig eth0 172.16.0.2 netmask 255.255.255.0 up
ip route add default via 172.16.0.1 dev eth0
echo "nameserver 8.8.8.8" > /etc/resolv.conf

```

This ensures networking is configured on every boot without manual intervention.

Increase rootfs size in `rebuild.sh` to accommodate additions (e.g., 400M). Edit the `build_rootfs` function:

```bash
local SIZE=${3:-400M}

```

Build the custom rootfs:

```bash
sudo ./rebuild.sh rootfs

```

If you encounter errors like `E: Error, pkgProblemResolver::Resolve generated breaks, this may be caused by held packages`, purge conflicting packages:

```bash
sudo apt purge docker.io containerd.io containerd runc -y
sudo apt autoremove -y
sudo apt autoclean -y

```

The build generates `x86_64/ubuntu-24.04.squashfs` and a manifest.

## Step 5: Update and Relaunch the MicroVM with Custom RootFS

Copy the new rootfs:

```bash
cd ../..  # Back to ~/firecracker-demo/
rm hello-rootfs.ext4 hello-vmlinux.bin  # Remove old files
cp ~/firecracker/firecracker/resources/x86_64/ubuntu-24.04.squashfs ./custom-rootfs.squashfs

```

Update `launch_vm.py` to use the new rootfs (change `rootfs_path`):

```python
rootfs_path = os.path.abspath('./custom-rootfs.squashfs')

```

Stop any running instance, recreate the TAP device (as in Step 3), restart Firecracker, and run:

```bash
sudo python3 launch_vm.py

```

Reattach screen: Expect `ubuntu-fc-uvm` hostname and auto-login as root.

## Step 6: Create and Integrate the Simple Flask App

The app is cloned in `chroot.sh`, but for reference, here's the simple Flask app (`app.py`):

```python
from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/')
def hello():
    return "Hello from microVM!"

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000)

```

And `requirements.txt`:

```
flask

```

The systemd service auto-starts it on port 5000.

## Step 7: Forward Ports and Test the App

On the host, forward the guest's port 5000 to localhost:

```bash
socat TCP-LISTEN:5000,fork TCP:172.16.0.2:5000

```

Test the app:

```bash
curl http://localhost:5000/

```

This should return `Hello from microVM!`. If not, check the guest console for app logs or systemd status (`systemctl status myapp` in guest).

## Conclusion

This detailed guide demonstrates Firecracker's flexibility with Python for MicroVM management, from basic launches to custom rootfs with auto-deployed apps. Boot times remain fast (~125ms), and the setup is secure for multi-tenant environments. For PyCon MY 2025, this could include live demos, error handling discussions, or extensions like multi-VM orchestration. Refer to Firecracker's GitHub for updates and community resources. Experiment safely, and scale as needed!

results.title: From $19 to CFO Attention: The Pricing Spiral of AI Assistants
results.abstract:
There was a time - not long ago - when AI coding assistants were glorified autocomplete toys. They helped you write console.log("hello world"), occasionally guessed what you meant, and only cost $19/month. For a while, that felt right. After all, $19 was less than your Uber Eats service fees.

But then the pricing got weird. What used to cost less than a large pizza started creeping up, then shot straight into enterprise-grade pricing models where costs scale aggressively and justification feels optional. Some tools now charge hundreds of dollars per developer per month for something that, just a year ago, felt like a neat autocomplete party trick. That’s not just inflation, that’s a whole new economic reality. The question is: what exactly are we paying for, and when did $19 become the opening bid?

results.title: Python × Signal Processing! The Sound World Visualized with Spectrograms
results.abstract:
* Python offers a wide range of libraries for signal processing and visualization, including PyAudio, NumPy, Matplotlib, etc. This talk will cover the basics of audio analysis and sound visualization for Python beginners and Pythonists who want to try signal processing and audio analysis.
* I will start by introducing the basics of the spectrogram algorithm, which allows us to "see sound," and explore various sounds. During my talk, I will analyze the sound visualization results using Python, and by adding my thoughts on the physical phenomena that create the sound, I hope to convey the idea of ​​"enjoying sound visually."
results.description:
This talk will cover the following content:

* Self-introduction (1 min)
* About this talk (1 min)
* Benefits of signal processing with Python (2 min)
  * Integration with a wide range of libraries
* Essential knowledge for audio analysis (10 min)
  * Recording audio
    * Recording with PyAudio
    * Bit depth and sampling rate
    * Frame-based recording
* Fourier transform
    * Relationship between time and frequency domains
    * Real-world waveform examples
* Creating spectrograms (8 min)
  * What is a spectrogram?
  * Spectrogram Processing Steps
  * Spectrogram Resolution
    * Frame Size
    * Overlap Processing
* Visualization Results for Various Sounds (Multiple Sources) (5 min)
  * What Sound Is This?
  * What Elements Make Up This Sound?
* Summary (1 min)
results.notes:
* Since this is an explanation for beginners, technical details will be kept simple and not overly complex.
* Code will be linked to GitHub via QR codes displayed on the page.

results.title: ACRUMEN: What IS Software Quality Anyway?!
results.abstract:
This talk introduces ACRUMEN, my definition of software quality in the form of a list of six aspects, including:
 - Why do we need a new definition?
 - What’s in this one?
 - Basic FAQs, such as: how do we use it, how can we quantify it, and is ACRUMEN always the right order?
 - Explore nuances and tips for each of the six aspects in turn
 - Further FAQs if time allows
 - Conclusion
results.description:
Without a good definition of software quality, you'll have a tough time achieving it, and if you don't _share_ your definition with someone, you'll have a hard time _convincing_ them that you _have_ achieved it!

This talk introduces ***ACRUMEN***, my simple yet fairly comprehensive definition, that devs of any level can use, with any tech stack, for free.  It stands for the idea that software should be ***A***ppropriate, ***C***orrect, ***R***obust, ***U***sable, ***M***aintainable, and ***E***fficient.  (Come to the talk to see what the ***N*** stands for!)  I will explain why we need a new definition, and how to apply this one, then explore each aspect in turn, including further nuances and immediately useful tips on how to _achieve_ each one.

You will come away better equipped to analyze the quality of anybody's software, give precise feedback on its shortcomings, teach the next generation of developers how to produce high-quality software, and most importantly, improve the quality of your own software.
results.notes:
This is aimed mainly at intermediates and maybe some advanced juniors, but seniors will still find the ideas useful.  Even non-techies will benefit from having a more precise vocabulary to tell us exactly _how_ our software falls short (to put it politely).

I've given this talk before at several conferences and Meetups.  You can see videos of many of them at https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLMrm16n64BuaRo9N8xY8OnTOWZ5K2aFG1

There is deliberately zero code in this, to emphasize that "code quality" is just a part of the overall picture of "software quality" that I am addressing -- and that the concepts are completely technology-agnostic (unlike some other definitions).

I would like to do this as an inspirational/motivational plenary keynote (as I did at Voxxed Days CERN 2025), but could also do it as a regular conference session.

results.title: From Code to Character: Creating an AI VTuber
results.abstract: Yup, you've seen the memes, AI anime characters streaming on Twitch. But how does it work though? This talk explores how Python can be used to build an AI VTuber capable of real-time conversation, expressive animation, and adaptive personality. We will walk through the inner workings of a modern AI VTuber, covering language models, voice synthesis, face/pose animation, and streaming integration. Furthermore, we will highlight the challenges, tools, and best practices involved in making a character that feels both alive and entertaining.
results.description: This session covers methods and learnings when creating an AI VTuber using Python's diverse ecosystem of machine learning, audio, and graphics libraries. Attendees will learn how to combine components such as LLM-based dialogue systems, TTS/voice cloning, and 2D animation to produce a virtual streamer. We will discuss common issues, latency optimization, safety considerations, and how to maintain a coherent character persona. The talk is aimed at developers interested in AI-driven media, or creative Python projects, and no prior VTubing experience is required.

results.title: Building Real AI Agents Learn Design, Memory, Guardrails and AgentOps
results.abstract: This workshop teaches you how to build AI agents that are not only smart but also safe, reliable, and observable. We start with the basics of what an agent is and how it uses tools and memory. Then we explore multi agent coordination and show how agents make decisions step by step. Along the way, we introduce guardrails, safety checks, and operational tools such as AgentOps that help you trace, debug, and control agent behavior. You will see real live demos built with Python and open source frameworks and learn practical patterns that you can use in your own projects.
results.description:
This two hour workshop gives a practical and beginner friendly introduction to AI agents. We begin with a clear understanding of what agents are, how they think, and how they act using memory, tools, and reasoning steps. From there, we move into multi agent workflows and show how multiple agents can cooperate and share tasks.

Participants will see real live demos using Python and open source frameworks. You will learn how agent workflows are built, how tools are connected, and how memory helps the agent stay consistent. We will also focus on an important part of real world agent systems which is operations and guardrails. You will learn how to observe agent behavior, trace its decisions, and evaluate outputs using tools like AgentOps. We explain how simple guardrail patterns create safer and more predictable agents that you can trust in real use cases.

The workshop is fully hands on and easy to follow. No heavy theory. You will leave knowing how agents work, how to build them, and how to make them safe and reliable for practical applications.

2 Hour Workshop Outline (Updated with Guardrails + Ops)
0:00 to 0:10 Welcome and Setup

Introductions
Environment check
What we will build today

0:10 to 0:25 Agents 101

What is an agent
How it thinks, reasons, and acts
Agent vs LLM vs normal AI system
Simple mental model

0:25 to 0:45 Single Agent Design

How agents use tools
Short term and long term memory
Action loops
Live demo in Python of a simple agent flow

0:45 to 1:10 Multi Agent Design

How multiple agents coordinate
Role based agents
Communication between agents
Working example of a small multi agent task

1:10 to 1:30 Live Domain Demos

Examples from real world use cases
Showing the actual flow of decisions
Interactive walkthrough

1:30 to 1:45 Frameworks and Best Practices

Overview of CrewAI, LangGraph and similar tools
When to use which
Why Model Context Protocol matters
Building agents the right way from day one

1:45 to 1:55 Guardrails and Ops

How to observe and debug agents
Tracing decisions with AgentOps
Evaluating agent actions
Simple guardrail techniques to keep agents safe
How to prevent runaway or incorrect behavior

1:55 to 2:00 Wrap Up

Final questions
Resources and next steps
results.notes:
# Prerequisites:
• Familiarity with Python is required
• Basic knowledge on how to leverage APIs
• No prior agent experience needed
• Curiosity about building smart and reliable AI systems

results.title: From Serverless to Speed: Our Migration Story from Lambda API to FastAPI
results.abstract:
Lambda APIs are great—until they start slowing you down. Between cold starts, debugging puzzles, and event-shaped restrictions, our API began to feel like a maze. What started as a simple “let’s improve local development” idea turned into a full architecture migration from Lambda-based APIs to FastAPI. Along the way, we met cold-start dragons, middleware mysteries, performance revelations, and a few staging-only surprises.
This talk is a real story of how we migrated our system end-to-end—what broke, what got better instantly, and what we wish someone had told us before we started.
results.description:
Through a story-first approach, we’ll explore:
The turning point that made us question our Lambda architecture
Mapping event-driven Lambda handlers to clean, expressive FastAPI routes
Handling authentication, dependency injection, middlewares, and async patterns
Debugging the “only breaks in prod” issues that surfaced during migration
Setting up observability and structured logging that finally made sense
Performance improvements we saw the moment Lambda cold starts disappeared
Deploying FastAPI with zero downtime, and how the team’s workflow transformed
If your team is evaluating or planning a migration, this talk will help you anticipate the path—with honesty, humour, and patterns you can use right away.

results.title: Building a Cyber Sentinel: Creating an Enterprise Malware Analysis Platform with Python's Hidden Gems
results.abstract:
In the escalating arms race of cybersecurity, malicious actors increasingly weaponize everyday documents PDFs, Word files, PowerPoint, Database and spreadsheets to bypass traditional defenses. This talk unveils how we built a powerful, open-source malware analysis platform using Python, turning the language into a formidable "Cyber Sentinel."
We will journey beyond basic scripting into a multi-layered architecture that leverages Python for deep file format deconstruction (PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, MDB), advanced obfuscation detection using Shannon entropy and regex, and real-time threat intelligence fusion. Attendees will discover practical applications of libraries like pdfplumber, olefile, and python-docx for binary forensics, see how to implement the MITRE ATT&CK framework for tactical classification, and learn to generate dynamic, data-rich HTML reports with matplotlib and seaborn.
This is not just a security talk; it's a masterclass in leveraging Python's extensive ecosystem to solve complex, real-world problems. You will leave with a blueprint for building intelligent systems that can dissect, analyze, and defend against modern digital threats.
results.description:
In today's digital battlefield, malicious actors have evolved beyond simple viruses to weaponize the very documents we use every day innocent looking PDFs, Word files, PowerPoint, Database and Excel spreadsheets that conceal sophisticated threats. Traditional antivirus solutions, relying on known signature databases, are increasingly powerless against these polymorphic and fileless attacks. This session unveils how we built "Cyber Sentinel," a powerful, open-source malware analysis platform that turns Python into a formidable digital forensics tool. We'll take you on a deep technical journey through our multi-layered architecture that demonstrates Python's underestimated capabilities in cybersecurity. You'll see how we:
1. Deconstruct complex file formats using pdfplumber, olefile, and python-docx to extract hidden payloads and malicious components from PDFs, Office documents, and even legacy OLE structures
2. Detect advanced obfuscation techniques using Shannon entropy calculations, regex pattern matching, and behavioral analysis to identify everything from Base64-encoded payloads to PowerShell obfuscation chains
3. Implement real-time threat intelligence by orchestrating multiple security APIs (VirusTotal) into a cohesive threat-scoring system
4. Map to industry frameworks by automatically classifying findings using the MITRE ATT&CK framework and assessing compliance impacts (NIST, HIPAA, PCI-DSS)
Build an enterprise-grade GUI that manages complex analysis workflows, threaded operations, and generates stunning visual reports with matplotlib and seaborn
This isn't just another security tool demo; it's a masterclass in software architecture that showcases how Python's extensive ecosystem can be woven together to solve critical real-world problems. You'll leave with not just theoretical knowledge, but practical code patterns and architectural insights that you can immediately apply to build your own security tools or enhance existing systems.
Whether you're a security professional looking to automate your workflow, a Python developer interested in cybersecurity applications, or a software architect designing complex systems, this talk will change how you view Python's role in the modern security landscape.
